introScreen.title: Your First Week on the Job
introScreen.scenarioTitle: Swastikas Painted on the Jewish Student Center
introScreen.scenarioBody: It’s 7 AM on a Tuesday. Your phone is already ringing. Swastikas were spray-painted on the Hillel building at the university last night. The campus rabbi is asking where your organization stands. A reporter from the Jewish Telegraphic Agency wants a quote by noon. Your board chair is texting — she thinks you should wait for the police investigation. Three of your staff members are Jewish college graduates; one is crying in the break room.

choices.text: Issue a strong public condemnation immediately — name it as antisemitism, demand university accountability
choices.outcome: Your statement makes the evening news. The campus rabbi thanks you publicly, but your board chair is furious you didn’t consult her first. Three Reform congregations share your statement; the Orthodox council says you’re grandstanding. A local columnist calls it “exactly the moral clarity we need.” Your development director warns that two major donors have gone quiet.

choices.text: Coordinate a joint response with the Interfaith Council and campus civil rights groups first
choices.outcome: It takes 36 hours to assemble the coalition statement, and by then the news cycle has partly moved on. But when the joint statement drops, it carries the signatures of 14 organizations — churches, mosques, the NAACP chapter, campus groups. The Jewish Forward runs it as a model for interfaith response. Your staff feels proud. The Orthodox council quietly signs on.

choices.text: Focus inward — organize support for affected students and staff before making any public statement
choices.outcome: You spend the day at the university. You sit with the crying students, bring in a counselor, order lunch for the Hillel staff. Your program director organizes an emergency support circle. It’s the right thing to do — but by 3 PM, Twitter is asking why your organization hasn’t said anything. A competing org issues a fiery statement and gets all the press. Your board chair approves, but the campus rabbi feels abandoned.

introScreen.transitionText: That was day one. You have twelve months to go.
introScreen.revealSubtitle: A leadership simulator for an impossible job
introScreen.revealPremise: You’ve just been hired to lead a Jewish organization at the most polarized moment in a generation. Your donors want one thing, your staff wants another, and every constituency in the community thinks you should be doing more.
introScreen.revealStakes: Deliver results and you’ll be promoted to lead a national organization with higher stakes and a bigger stage. Lose the trust of your community, burn through your budget, or let morale collapse — and your board will fire you.
introScreen.ctaButtonPost: That was day one. Ready for twelve months? →
introScreen.ctaTagline: Choose your organization, build your team, and begin.

charScreen.heading: Who Are You?
charScreen.description: Your background and traits shape how the community responds to you — and what options open up when crises hit.

orgScreen.heading: Choose Your Organization
orgScreen.description: Each organization type comes with its own culture, donor base, and political pressures. Your org's traits combine with your own throughout the game.

missionScreen.heading: What Is Your Mission?
missionScreen.description: Your mission defines what your organization stands for. Every decision you make will be flagged as Mission Aligned ✓, Mission Opposed ✗, or neutral — and you'll see it before you choose.

gameScreen.noApMessage: No action points remaining. Click "End Round" to continue.

promoScreen.heading: A Larger Stage Awaits
promoScreen.description: Your success has attracted national attention. Based on your track record and political tilt, the following organizations are recruiting you.

advisorScreen.heading: Build Your Advisory Team
advisorScreen.description: Choose the people who will counsel you through the hardest decisions. Your advisors shape your starting position and influence outcomes throughout the game.

onboarding.msg: Let me be honest with you about what you're walking into. This job is a balancing act — and the board is always watching.
onboarding.label: What do I need to know?

onboarding.msg: You have 3 actions each round. Click an object on your desk to spend one. Your phone is free — inbox messages don't cost actions.
onboarding.label: Got it. What am I trying to do?

onboarding.msg: Watch your three stats up top: Trust, Morale, and Donors. If any one of them drops to 15 or below, the board calls an emergency meeting and your tenure ends immediately.
onboarding.label: How do I win?

onboarding.msg: At year's end, the board evaluates your overall performance. Score above 75 and you'll be offered a promotion to a national organization. Stay above 30 and you keep your job. Below that… well, don't go below that. Your mission stars also significantly impact your score — stay true to your stated priorities.
onboarding.label: Any other advice?

onboarding.msg: You have a gelt (budget) you can invest in your organization — marketing campaigns, security upgrades, staff development, signature events. Use the investment bar to spend wisely. These don't cost actions but they do cost money.
onboarding.label: One more thing?

onboarding.msg: One more thing — there are no perfect choices in this job. Every decision has a cost. The best leaders are the ones who know which costs they can afford.
onboarding.label: Let's do this →

popupMessages.statDangerOrange: Heads up — your {stat} is getting low at {value}. Keep an eye on it before the board starts asking questions.
popupMessages.statDangerRed: 🚨 The board has called an emergency meeting. Your {stat} is critically low at {value}. If it drops to {threshold}, your tenure ends immediately.
popupMessages.investmentSuggestion: Your {stat} is in trouble. Consider using investments to help — check the investment bar for options that boost {stat}.
popupMessages.missionStarLow: Your mission stars are dangerously low. The board expects you to stay true to your stated priorities — losing all your stars means losing their confidence.
popupMessages.missionStarMax: You've earned maximum mission stars. The board sees a leader with clear vision and consistent follow-through. This will significantly boost your year-end evaluation.
popupMessages.geltCrisis: Your gelt is critically low. Some choices may be unavailable until finances improve.
popupMessages.centristClout: Your decision would have earned +{full} clout, but the centrist penalty reduced it to +{reduced}. Organizations closer to the political center build influence more slowly.
popupMessages.communityCloutBonus: ⚡ +{amount} Clout from your position near the community's political center.
popupMessages.communityCloutBonusCentrist: ⚡ +{amount} Clout from your position near the community's political center. You earned {amount} instead of {full} because centrist organizations build influence at half rate.

characterTraits.name: Community Insider
characterTraits.description: You've been in Jewish communal life for decades. People know you — and you know where the bodies are buried.

characterTraits.name: Outsider Voice
characterTraits.description: You came from outside the Jewish communal bubble. You see things others miss — and don't owe anyone favors.

characterTraits.name: Master Fundraiser
characterTraits.description: You have a gift for the ask. Donors take your calls and write bigger checks than they planned.

characterTraits.name: Grassroots Organizer
characterTraits.description: You built power from the ground up. You know how to mobilize people who've never shown up before.

characterTraits.name: Jewish Scholar
characterTraits.description: Your depth of Jewish learning gives you moral authority. When you cite Torah, people listen.

characterTraits.name: Political Operator
characterTraits.description: You understand power. You know who to call, what to trade, and when to hold your fire.

characterTraits.name: Media Savvy
characterTraits.description: You know how to work a story. Journalists take your calls and you rarely come off badly in print.

characterTraits.name: Burnout Survivor
characterTraits.description: You've been through the fire before. You protect your staff because you know what it costs when no one does.

organizations.name: Jewish Voice for Justice
organizations.description: A left-wing advocacy org focused on social justice, Palestinian rights, anti-racism, and progressive domestic policy.
traits: Progressive donor base: bold stances required
Coalition-dependent: allies can turn quickly
Authenticity under constant scrutiny

organizations.name: Am Tzion Institute
organizations.description: A right-wing pro-Israel advocacy org focused on combating left-wing antisemitism and supporting strong US-Israel ties.
traits: Major donor driven: consistency expected
Strong Israel focus: deviations are costly
Credibility at risk on right-wing antisemitism

organizations.name: Jewish Civic Partnership
organizations.description: A centrist advocacy org seeking bipartisan coalitions on antisemitism, Israel, and Jewish continuity.
traits: Bipartisan by design: balance is everything
Moderate donors: no dramatic moves
Credibility through consistency

organizations.name: Lakeview JCC
organizations.description: A community hub providing programs, childcare, fitness, and cultural events for Jews of all backgrounds.
traits: Pluralistic mandate: serve all Jews
Budget driven by membership revenue
Apolitical pressure: controversy is costly

nationalOrganizations.name: American Jewish Action Network
nationalOrganizations.description: A national progressive Jewish advocacy organization working on social justice, voting rights, and Israeli-Palestinian peace at the federal level.
traits: National media: every statement is amplified
Large progressive donor base across 30+ cities
Coalition work with major civil rights organizations

nationalOrganizations.name: Israel-America Security Alliance
nationalOrganizations.description: A national pro-Israel advocacy organization with deep congressional relationships, focused on US-Israel relations and combating left-wing antisemitism.
traits: Major donor network spans Wall Street and Silicon Valley
Congressional relationships on both sides of the aisle
High expectations: any misstep is front-page news

nationalOrganizations.name: Jewish Leadership Council
nationalOrganizations.description: The umbrella body for mainstream American Jewish advocacy, representing hundreds of local organizations and speaking for the communal consensus.
traits: Represents diverse constituency: every decision is contested
Largest platform in Jewish America
Bipartisan credibility is your greatest asset

nationalOrganizations.name: Jewish Community Centers of America
nationalOrganizations.description: The national JCC association supporting 150+ community centers across North America with programming, funding, and crisis response.
traits: Network of 150 local JCCs depending on your leadership
Apolitical brand: politics fractures the network
Security and continuity are the twin mandates

missions.name: Combat Antisemitism from All Directions
missions.description: Your organization fights antisemitism wherever it comes from — far right, far left, and everywhere in between.

missions.name: Counter Antisemitism from the Progressive Left
missions.description: You focus on left-wing antisemitism — in progressive movements, on campuses, and in political discourse.

missions.name: Counter Antisemitism from the Far Right
missions.description: You focus on right-wing and white nationalist antisemitism — the most statistically deadly threat to Jewish Americans.

missions.name: Strengthen US-Israel Relations
missions.description: Your primary mission is securing American political and financial support for Israel.

missions.name: Advocate for Israeli-Palestinian Peace
missions.description: You push for a two-state solution, lifting the blockade, and accountability for both sides.

missions.name: Jewish Social Justice Advocacy
missions.description: You ground your work in Jewish values — tikkun olam, tzedakah — applied to racial justice, immigrants' rights, and economic equality.

missions.name: Jewish Identity and Continuity
missions.description: Your mission is keeping Jews Jewish — through education, culture, day schools, and fighting assimilation.

missions.name: Be the Center of Jewish Community Life
missions.description: You aim to be the place every Jew in your city turns to — for culture, connection, childcare, and crisis.

missions.name: Unite the Jewish Diaspora
missions.description: You work to build bridges across denominations, politics, and geographies.

scenarios.title: Antisemitic Vandalism at the Local University
scenarios.body: Swastikas were spray-painted on the Jewish student center at the nearby university. The local Jewish community is shaken. Your board chair calls you asking what you intend to do. Media requests are already coming in.

choices.text: Issue a strong, unequivocal public statement condemning the attack and calling on university leadership to act.

outcomes.text: Your statement is widely shared. Community members feel seen. Several new donors reach out.
Your comprehensive response is praised by many, but the aggressive stance alienates potential progressive allies. Some donors question whether the confrontational approach is sustainable.

choices.text: Coordinate a coalition response with other civil rights organizations before speaking publicly.

outcomes.text: The coalition statement takes three days. Some community members feel you were too slow. The statement itself is broadly praised.
The intersectional approach builds bridges with other communities, but some in your base feel the Jewish-specific pain was minimized. An op-ed accuses you of 'universalizing antisemitism away.'

choices.text: Organize a campus solidarity vigil and connect affected students to resources.

outcomes.text: Attendance is strong. You receive heartfelt appreciation from students. Less visible nationally but deeply meaningful locally.

The internal community-building approach is warmly received by some, but critics say you're turning inward instead of confronting the real threat. A prominent columnist calls it 'retreat disguised as resilience.'

choices.text: Wait for more information before acting. Issue a brief holding statement.

outcomes.text: The perpetrator turns out to be a current student. Your caution was partly wise, but community members noticed the silence.

advisorQuotes.text: We need to be visible but careful. A wrong word now could define us for years.
The students need to hear from us TODAY. Not tomorrow, not after a committee meeting. Today.
Three donors have already called. They want to see leadership, not hesitation.

scenarios.title: A Major Donor Makes a Restricted Gift Offer
scenarios.body: Harold Weinstein, a longtime real-estate developer and major donor, offers $300,000 — but the gift must fund a specific program tangential to your core work. Several staff members have quietly opposed it on strategic grounds.

choices.text: Accept the full restricted gift and launch the program as Harold envisions it.

outcomes.text: The money stabilizes your budget significantly. But two senior staff members resign in protest over mission drift.

choices.text: Accept the gift but negotiate to modify the program's scope to align better with your mission.

outcomes.text: After difficult conversations, Harold agrees to modified terms. He is slightly less enthusiastic but still supportive.
The donor appreciates your honesty but reduces the gift by half. Your board chair asks whether principled stands are worth the budget impact.

choices.text: Decline the restricted gift and have an honest conversation with Harold about your mission.

outcomes.text: Harold ultimately respects your integrity. His annual gift remains. Word gets around that your organization holds firm on mission.
The compromise satisfies the donor, but word gets out that you negotiated naming rights. Other donors feel slighted, and staff question whether the organization's values are for sale.

choices.text: Accept and immediately find a matching unrestricted donor to protect your core work.

outcomes.text: You accept Harold's gift AND secure a matching unrestricted gift. The new program runs but your core work is fully protected.
You secure the donation, but the donor's public profile attracts scrutiny. A local paper runs a piece questioning whether the gift comes with strings attached. Staff worry about mission drift.

advisorQuotes.text: $300K is transformational money. We can make almost anything work with that.
If we take restricted money for the wrong program, we lose the staff who do our actual work.
Harold has been with us for 15 years. We need to handle this without burning bridges.

scenarios.title: A Request for a Statement on the Latest Conflict
scenarios.body: Following renewed fighting in Gaza, young professionals in your community circulate a letter urging you to issue a statement. Some want condemnation of Israeli military actions. Others want unequivocal solidarity with Israel. Your board is divided.

choices.text: Issue a statement of solidarity with Israel's right to self-defense, without addressing civilian casualties.

outcomes.text: Your right-leaning donors and board members are relieved. Several progressive staff members express deep disappointment.

choices.text: Issue a statement expressing concern for all civilian casualties on both sides, calling for a ceasefire.

outcomes.text: Progressive community members are energized. Several major donors freeze their gifts pending clarification.

choices.text: Issue a carefully balanced statement affirming Israel's security while acknowledging humanitarian concerns.

outcomes.text: Almost everyone finds something to criticize. A few people appreciate the nuance. No one forwards it.

choices.text: Frame your statement entirely through Jewish textual tradition — pikuach nefesh, the sanctity of all human life.

outcomes.text: The statement is widely praised by rabbis across denominations. It sidesteps the political fight and elevates the conversation.
Your carefully worded statement satisfies no one. Progressive members call it cowardly; pro-Israel donors call it weak. The middle ground turns out to be a lonely place.

advisorQuotes.text: Whatever we say will be screenshot'd and shared out of context. Let's be precise.
Our young professionals are watching. Silence is a statement too.
I need to be honest — two of our top five donors have already told me what they expect us to say.

scenarios.title: Staff Burnout Before the High Holiday Season
scenarios.body: It's early September. Your two biggest events of the year require enormous staff effort. Your program director reports three staff are burned out, one has requested leave, and everyone is underpaid compared to similar roles.

choices.text: Offer meaningful raises funded by drawing down reserves, and hire a part-time events coordinator.

outcomes.text: The events go well. Staff morale surges. Your reserves are leaner than your board wanted.
Staff appreciate the new benefits, but the generous package strains the budget more than expected. Some board members grumble about fiscal responsibility.

choices.text: Acknowledge the problem and promise a compensation review after the High Holidays, offering small bonuses now.

outcomes.text: Staff appreciate being heard. The compensation review reveals how underpaid everyone is — creating pressure for next year.
The restructuring helps some teams but displaces others. Two mid-level staffers leave, taking institutional knowledge with them. Rebuilding takes longer than expected.

choices.text: Recruit community volunteers aggressively to fill the capacity gap.

outcomes.text: You find enough volunteers, but the burden falls on your most burned-out staff. One of them quits the week before the gala.

choices.text: Scale back both events and invest in staff recovery. Send a letter to stakeholders explaining why.

outcomes.text: Some donors are disappointed. But your team enters the year rested and turnover hits zero.

advisorQuotes.text: I'm telling you right now — if we don't address comp, I'll lose my best people by December.
The board approved this budget. Drawing down reserves without authorization will be a governance issue.

scenarios.title: Two Board Members at War
scenarios.body: Two of your most influential board members — both major donors — have come into open conflict. The dispute began over programming but has become personal. One walked out of last month's board meeting. Both are calling you asking you to take sides.

choices.text: Convene a facilitated conversation between the two with an outside mediator.

outcomes.text: The mediator earns both parties' trust. Neither is fully satisfied but both agree to remain on the board.
Your transparent approach earns trust from some board members, but the losing faction feels publicly humiliated. One resigns and takes two major donors with them.

choices.text: Meet with each board member individually, listening carefully but refusing to take sides.

outcomes.text: Both feel heard. The conflict simmers but doesn't boil over. You earn a reputation for steadiness.
The quiet resolution preserves board harmony but leaves the underlying issues unresolved. Staff see it as conflict avoidance and lose confidence in leadership.

choices.text: Side with the board member whose position you actually agree with and say so.

outcomes.text: The situation resolves faster, but the other board member reduces her gift and becomes a source of gossip.

choices.text: Quietly engineer a public honor for one of them, making it impossible to continue the feud without looking petty.

outcomes.text: The feud ends quickly. Neither board member figures out how it happened. Pure political craft.
Your mediation works on the surface, but the underlying tensions remain. Both factions feel you sided with the other, and donor confidence wavers as rumors of board dysfunction leak.

advisorQuotes.text: I've seen board conflicts destroy organizations. This needs to be contained fast.
Between them, they represent $180K in annual giving. We can't afford to lose either.

scenarios.title: A Progressive Coalition Asks You to Sign an Embargo Letter
scenarios.body: A broad progressive coalition is circulating a letter demanding an arms embargo on Israel. Many of your staff signed a staff-side version. Some of your major donors have explicitly said they will reduce gifts if you sign anything critical of Israeli military policy.

choices.text: Sign the letter. Your values are your values, and your donors should understand that.

outcomes.text: You sign. Three major donors reduce their gifts. Your progressive coalition relationships deepen. Staff morale surges.

choices.text: Sign a modified version that advocates for accountability but not a full embargo.

outcomes.text: Neither side is fully satisfied. The coalition lists you with an asterisk. Some donors accept the compromise.

choices.text: Decline as an organization but don't prevent staff from signing personally.

outcomes.text: Several staff sign publicly. One donor withdraws citing organizational chaos.

choices.text: Decline and explain your organization focuses on domestic Jewish advocacy, not foreign policy.

outcomes.text: Your progressive allies are deeply disappointed. One partner publicly criticizes you. Moderate donors are quietly relieved.

advisorQuotes.text: Our staff overwhelming supports this letter. If we don't sign, we'll have a credibility crisis internally.
I have three donors who will pull six-figure gifts if we sign anything related to an arms embargo.
This is the kind of issue where there is no safe choice. Only choices with different costs.

scenarios.title: Antisemitism from a Political Ally
scenarios.body: A prominent politician who has co-sponsored your events makes remarks widely characterized as antisemitic — dog-whistling about 'globalists' and dual loyalties. Several Jewish newspapers call on organizations to cut ties. This politician is influential in securing a federal grant your org depends on.

choices.text: Publicly condemn the remarks and cut all ties, regardless of financial and political consequences.

outcomes.text: The condemnation is credible given the relationship. The grant is at risk, but your moral authority on antisemitism is strengthened substantially.

choices.text: Issue a mild statement expressing concern while preserving the relationship.

outcomes.text: Critics are not satisfied. Jewish newspapers run a piece on your tepid response.

choices.text: Reach out privately, demanding a retraction as the price of continued partnership.

outcomes.text: The politician issues a partial clarification. The private pressure eventually leaks, making the episode messier.

choices.text: Argue the remarks have been taken out of context and this politician is a true friend of the Jewish people.

outcomes.text: A significant portion of your own community is alienated. Several rabbis refuse to attend future events.

advisorQuotes.text: Our credibility on antisemitism is on the line. But that grant is 15% of our budget.
If we don't condemn this clearly, everything we say about antisemitism becomes hollow.
I can find replacement funding, but it'll take 6-8 months. Can we survive the gap?

scenarios.title: A Bomb Threat
scenarios.body: At 9:15 on a Tuesday morning, your JCC receives a bomb threat by phone — the third at a JCC in your region this month. You evacuate 84 people including 31 children. Police sweep the building and find nothing. You must decide how to respond.

choices.text: Reopen the next morning with enhanced visible security and a clear, calm communication to families.

outcomes.text: Most families return. Your calm leadership under pressure is noted. Some families temporarily withdraw their children.
Your security-first response is appreciated, but the heightened measures make the JCC feel like a fortress. Attendance drops 15% as families find the atmosphere oppressive.

choices.text: Close for a week while you secure federal security grants and upgrade your infrastructure.

outcomes.text: The closure costs revenue but you reopen with meaningful security improvements that become a model for other JCCs.
The defiant resumption of programming sends a strong message, but a follow-up threat forces an evacuation. Parents are furious you didn't take security more seriously.

choices.text: Reopen immediately and say as little as possible to avoid alarming the community.

outcomes.text: Rumors fill the vacuum. A parent posts on Facebook and the local news picks it up. Families feel uninformed.

choices.text: Host an emergency community meeting that evening, inviting law enforcement and community leaders.

outcomes.text: Turnout is overwhelming. Families feel heard and informed. Several major donors make gifts on the spot.
The community rallies, but the enormous response overwhelms your staff. Several key employees threaten to quit from burnout, and the overtime costs blow through your event budget.

scenarios.title: A Hostile Journalist Is Working on a Story
scenarios.body: A journalist at a Jewish newspaper is working on a critical profile of your organization. She's been calling board members. One board member warns the story could be damaging. You have a week before publication.

choices.text: Call the journalist and offer a full sit-down interview, sharing everything openly.

outcomes.text: The piece runs. It is critical in places but fair. Your openness is noted. The story does less damage than feared.

choices.text: Decline to comment and prepare a rapid response for the day of publication.

outcomes.text: The piece is harsher than expected. Your no-comment is featured prominently.

choices.text: Pitch a counter-story to a more sympathetic outlet to run the same day.

outcomes.text: Both stories run the same day. The dueling narratives create confusion.

choices.text: Use your media relationships to get advance notice of key claims and prepare a surgical rebuttal.

outcomes.text: You rebut two factual errors before publication. The editor runs corrections. The story lands with much less impact.
Your media strategy generates positive coverage, but the reporter's follow-up piece digs into old organizational issues. The net result is mixed — visibility up, but so is scrutiny.

advisorQuotes.text: We've been through bad press before. The key is controlling the narrative, not hiding from it.
Our spring gala is in six weeks. Bad press right now could cost us $50K in ticket sales.

scenarios.title: Campus Divestment Vote
scenarios.body: The student government at the largest university in your area has scheduled a vote on a resolution demanding the university divest from companies doing business with Israel. Jewish students are panicking. The campus Hillel director is begging you to intervene. Several progressive allies in your coalition are publicly supporting the resolution.

advisorQuotes.text: This is a lose-lose. If you fight it loudly, you look like you’re suppressing free speech. If you stay quiet, Jewish students feel abandoned.
Some of these student activists are our people. Young Jews who care about Palestinian rights. We can’t treat them as the enemy.
Our donors are watching this very closely. Three board members have already called me.

choices.text: Launch a full-court press against the resolution: lobby student senators, organize counter-testimony, and mobilize alumni donors to pressure the administration.

outcomes.text: The resolution fails by three votes. Pro-divestment students accuse you of silencing Palestinian voices. Jewish students are relieved but the campus climate worsens.
outcomes.contextNote: Several progressive organizations quietly note your tactics.

outcomes.text: The resolution passes anyway despite your opposition, and your organization is now publicly identified as the face of the losing side.

choices.text: Support Jewish students in testifying about how the resolution affects campus climate, without taking an organizational position on divestment itself.

outcomes.text: Jewish students feel supported. Your nuanced position frustrates both hardliners and progressives but earns respect from the university administration.
Your student support programming is appreciated, but activists on both sides accuse you of trying to sidestep the real issue. A campus newspaper op-ed calls your approach 'performative neutrality.'

choices.text: Organize a counter-programming event: a campus dialogue on antisemitism, Islamophobia, and how divestment debates affect real students on both sides.

outcomes.text: The event draws 200 students and earns a glowing write-up in the campus paper. Several donors are furious you didn’t fight the resolution directly.

choices.text: Stay out of it publicly. The university’s internal politics aren’t your fight. Focus on supporting Jewish students through direct services.

outcomes.text: Some appreciate your restraint. Others call it cowardice. Jewish students on campus feel you weren’t there when they needed you.

scenarios.title: Holocaust Education Under Attack
scenarios.body: A state legislator has introduced a bill to remove Holocaust education from the mandatory curriculum, calling it ‘politically motivated content.’ The bill has surprising bipartisan support from both culture-war conservatives and progressives who want the curriculum to focus on ‘a broader range of genocides.’ You have one week before the committee vote.

advisorQuotes.text: This is not a political issue. This is about historical truth and the obligation of ‘zachor’ — remember. We fight this with everything we have.
We need to frame this as education, not identity politics. The moment this becomes ‘Jews demanding special treatment,’ we lose.

choices.text: Lead a massive public campaign: op-eds, survivor testimony at the statehouse, social media blitz, and a rally on the capitol steps.

outcomes.text: The campaign generates national attention. The bill is tabled indefinitely. Your organization is credited as the driving force behind the victory.
outcomes.contextNote: Three Holocaust survivors testified. The footage goes viral.

outcomes.text: The campaign is intense but the bill passes committee anyway. However, the governor signals a veto thanks to the public pressure you generated.
Your campaign succeeds in getting the curriculum adopted, but a vocal parents' group accuses you of politicizing education. The controversy energizes opponents and drains your staff.

choices.text: Build a broad coalition including educators, veterans groups, and civil rights organizations to oppose the bill through institutional channels.

outcomes.text: The coalition is impressive and the institutional pressure works. The bill dies quietly in committee. Your org isn’t the public face — but insiders know you built it.
The broad coalition succeeds in getting the curriculum passed, but community members feel the specifically Jewish aspects were diluted to please other groups.

choices.text: Focus on a behind-the-scenes approach: meet privately with swing-vote legislators and offer to help them craft an improved curriculum rather than fighting the bill directly.

outcomes.text: Two legislators flip. The bill is amended into an expanded genocide education mandate that actually strengthens Holocaust teaching. A quiet but significant win.
outcomes.contextNote: Your political relationships proved decisive here.

outcomes.text: Your focus on Jewish community education is appreciated internally, but you miss the window to influence the public school curriculum. A rival org fills the gap and gets the credit.

choices.text: Issue a public statement opposing the bill but let the state education association take the lead. Preserve your political capital for fights closer to your core mission.

outcomes.text: The education association’s campaign is disorganized and the bill advances. Community members ask why you didn’t do more.

scenarios.title: Security Partnership with Police Sparks Staff Revolt
scenarios.body: After a string of threats, you’ve been offered a formal security partnership with the local police department — dedicated patrols, direct lines, priority response. Half your staff signs a letter opposing the partnership, citing police violence against communities of color. Two board members who are civil rights attorneys support the staff. Your security consultant says the threats are credible and escalating.

advisorQuotes.text: If we partner with a police department that our Black and Brown neighbors don’t trust, we’re choosing our safety over theirs. That’s not who we are.
Our first obligation is the physical safety of every person who walks through our doors. Full stop.

choices.text: Accept the partnership. Security is non-negotiable and the threats are real. Address staff concerns with a town hall.

outcomes.text: The partnership is implemented. Security improves immediately. Three progressive staff members resign, and a local BLM chapter issues a statement of disappointment.

choices.text: Accept the partnership but also fund a community safety initiative with local civil rights organizations, making it a two-track approach.

outcomes.text: It’s expensive but it works. You get the security you need while demonstrating solidarity. Some on both sides still grumble, but most see the good faith.

choices.text: Decline the police partnership. Invest in private security and community-based safety alternatives instead.

outcomes.text: Staff morale soars. Progressive allies praise your stance. But private security costs twice as much and some congregants feel less safe.

choices.text: Delay the decision pending a community-wide listening session that includes staff, congregants, and neighborhood leaders.

outcomes.text: The listening session is intense but productive. You end up with a modified partnership that most stakeholders can live with, but the two-week delay costs you during a threat spike.
outcomes.contextNote: Your security consultant is unhappy about the delay.

scenarios.title: Interfaith Seder Goes Off the Rails
scenarios.body: Your annual interfaith Passover seder — co-hosted with a prominent church and mosque — was going beautifully until the imam made an unscripted comparison between Palestinians and the Israelites enslaved in Egypt. Half the Jewish attendees walked out. The imam says he was speaking from his heart and won’t retract. The pastor is trying to mediate. Local media is picking up the story.

advisorQuotes.text: Whatever you do, do it before the 6 o’clock news. If we don’t control this story, someone else will.
The Haggadah itself invites us to see ourselves in the story. His reading was hurtful but not unreasonable within the tradition of seder commentary. This is teachable.

choices.text: Issue a public statement condemning the imam’s remarks as offensive and inappropriate. Suspend the interfaith partnership until he retracts.

outcomes.text: Your Jewish community largely supports you. The imam doubles down. The pastor quietly ends the three-way partnership. The interfaith work you built over years is frozen.

choices.text: Meet privately with the imam and pastor to work out a joint statement that acknowledges the pain while preserving the partnership.

outcomes.text: After a tense three-hour meeting, you produce a joint statement. It satisfies almost no one fully, but the partnership survives. The imam agrees to more sensitivity in future events.
The imam refuses to budge. The joint statement falls apart. You’ve invested time and political capital with nothing to show for it.

choices.text: Host a follow-up event: ‘The Exodus Story Through Many Eyes’ — turning the controversy into a moderated public dialogue about shared narratives.

outcomes.text: The event is packed and emotionally raw. Several attendees say it was the most honest interfaith conversation they’ve ever experienced. Some donors think you legitimized the comparison.

choices.text: Say nothing publicly. Let the news cycle move on. Quietly pause interfaith programming for six months.

outcomes.text: The story fades, but so does your interfaith credibility. When you try to restart programming later, neither the church nor the mosque returns your calls.

scenarios.title: A Staff Member’s Personal Posts Cause a Firestorm
scenarios.body: Your communications director — excellent at her job — has been posting passionate pro-Israel content on her personal social media, including sharing IDF videos and calling anti-Zionism a form of antisemitism. A progressive Jewish group screencaps the posts and tags your organization, demanding you ‘address the extremism in your ranks.’ She says it’s her personal account and her personal views.

advisorQuotes.text: If we punish her for pro-Israel views on her own time, what message does that send about who’s welcome here?
The optics are terrible either way. Fire her and we look like we caved to a pressure campaign. Defend her and we own every post she’s ever made.

choices.text: Publicly defend her right to personal expression and refuse to engage with the pressure campaign.

outcomes.text: Your staff respects the principled stand. Conservative donors applaud you. But the progressive group escalates, and two partner organizations quietly distance themselves.
Your defense of the staff member earns loyalty internally, but some progressive community members see it as protecting inflammatory speech. Social media backlash ensues.

choices.text: Implement a new social media policy for all staff that requires a disclaimer separating personal views from organizational positions.

outcomes.text: The policy gives everyone cover. She adds a disclaimer, the critics lose their hook, and you avoid taking a side. Staff grumbles about the new bureaucracy but complies.

choices.text: Ask her to take down the most inflammatory posts as a condition of continued employment. Frame it as protecting the organization.

outcomes.text: She takes down the posts but is deeply hurt. Morale drops as staff worry about being policed on their personal accounts. She starts job hunting quietly.

choices.text: Let her go. Her public persona has become inseparable from the organization’s brand, and the controversy is a distraction you can’t afford.

outcomes.text: The progressive group celebrates. Conservative media picks up the story: ‘Jewish Org Fires Employee for Supporting Israel.’ You’ve made national news for the wrong reason.
outcomes.contextNote: Two other staff members quietly update their LinkedIn profiles.

scenarios.title: How to Release a Damning Antisemitism Report
scenarios.body: Your research team has completed a comprehensive report showing antisemitic incidents in your region increased 47% last year. The data is solid, but the breakdown is politically uncomfortable: incidents from the far right are up 60%, while incidents tied to progressive spaces (campus protests, social media) are up 35%. How you frame and release this report will shape the public conversation.

advisorQuotes.text: Lead with the total number. 47% is the headline. Let journalists pull the breakdown from the appendix. We don’t need to editorialize the data.
Be careful. Every political faction will cherry-pick the numbers that indict the other side. The framing you choose in the press release IS the story.
The truth doesn’t need spin. Release everything, let the data speak, and trust the community to handle uncomfortable truths.

choices.text: Lead with the overall 47% increase. Keep the breakdown in the full report but don’t highlight it in the press release or talking points.

outcomes.text: The 47% figure dominates coverage. The report is cited widely. But within days, partisans on both sides accuse you of burying data that implicates ‘their’ opponents.
The comprehensive report gets media coverage, but experts critique your methodology. A rival organization publishes a rebuttal, and the whole effort looks less authoritative.

choices.text: Release everything prominently — the overall number and the full political breakdown — and hold a press conference framing antisemitism as a threat from all directions.

outcomes.text: The press conference is praised for honesty. Both left and right media quote the data that suits them. Your organization emerges as the credible, nonpartisan source.
The nuance is lost in the news cycle. Headlines read ‘Jewish Org Says Antisemitism Up From Left and Right’ — and both sides feel attacked. Your inbox fills with angry emails.

choices.text: Emphasize the far-right data (60% increase) in the press release, since it represents the larger share of the threat.

outcomes.text: Progressive allies share the report widely. Conservative media accuses you of bias. Several right-leaning donors call to complain.

choices.text: Emphasize the progressive-space data (35% increase) as the underreported story, since media already covers far-right antisemitism.

outcomes.text: Conservative media loves it. You’re invited onto three cable news shows in one day. Progressive allies are furious, saying you’re weaponizing data to attack the left.

scenarios.title: A Rival Jewish Organization Attacks You Publicly
scenarios.body: A competing Jewish organization has published a scathing open letter accusing your organization of ‘failing the Jewish community’ by being too political, too cautious, or too compromised — depending on who you ask. The letter has 40 signatories including three rabbis. It’s trending in Jewish media. Your board is rattled.

advisorQuotes.text: We need to respond, but carefully. Two of those signatories are former donors. This smells like a coordinated campaign, not a genuine critique.
I know the executive director behind this. He’s been trying to poach our donors for two years. This is about market share, not mission.

choices.text: Publish a detailed, point-by-point rebuttal demonstrating your impact with hard data.

outcomes.text: Your rebuttal is thorough and effective. Supporters rally around you. But the public fight between Jewish orgs becomes the story, and funders worry about communal dysfunction.

choices.text: Take the high road. Release a brief statement reaffirming your mission and inviting the other org to collaborate rather than compete.

outcomes.text: The dignified response earns widespread praise. The rival org looks petty. Several of their signatories privately apologize. Your restraint becomes its own story.
The collaboration goes smoothly in public, but behind the scenes the rival org takes credit for joint wins. Your staff feels demoralized by the unequal partnership.

choices.text: Work the phones. Call every signatory personally, understand their concerns, and peel off as many as possible before responding publicly.

outcomes.text: You flip eight signatories who say they were pressured into signing. The letter’s credibility collapses. But it took three days of your time and the rival org now sees you as a threat.
outcomes.contextNote: Your political skills are noticed by people who matter.

outcomes.text: Focusing on your strengths works strategically, but some see it as conceding territory. A board member asks if you're 'retreating rather than competing.'

choices.text: Ignore it completely. Don’t dignify it with a response. Let your work speak for itself.

outcomes.text: Some admire the confidence. Others interpret silence as guilt. The letter circulates for two weeks before dying down. A few donors quietly switch to the rival org.

scenarios.title: A Mega-Celebrity’s Antisemitic Meltdown
scenarios.body: One of the most famous entertainers in the world has gone on an extended antisemitic rant across social media — Jewish control conspiracies, Holocaust denial, the full catalog. It’s everywhere. Every Jewish organization in America is responding. Journalists are calling you for comment. Your young staffers are shaken. A major tech platform is refusing to remove the posts, citing free speech.

advisorQuotes.text: Everyone is going to say the same thing. The question is whether we can say something that actually moves the needle — or just add to the noise.
This is a fundraising moment. People want to DO something with their outrage. Give them that outlet.

choices.text: Issue a strong public condemnation and call on the tech platform to remove the content. Join the chorus of Jewish organizations demanding accountability.

outcomes.text: Your statement joins hundreds of others. It’s the right thing to say but gets lost in the flood. Community members appreciate it but nobody’s mind is changed.

choices.text: Go beyond condemnation: organize a community support event with mental health resources for people processing the trauma of seeing this hatred go mainstream.

outcomes.text: The event draws 150 people, many in tears. A therapist leads a powerful session. The local paper covers it as ‘the Jewish community’s quiet strength in the face of hate.’
The community solidarity event is well-attended, but the positive atmosphere feels hollow to some. 'We celebrated ourselves instead of demanding accountability,' one attendee writes.

choices.text: Use the moment to launch an education campaign targeting non-Jewish audiences — explaining why these tropes are dangerous and how to recognize them.

outcomes.text: The campaign reaches 2 million people online. Three school districts request your educational materials. You’ve turned a crisis into a long-term program.
outcomes.contextNote: This is the kind of response that builds institutional reputation.

outcomes.text: Your educational initiative gets traction, but the celebrity's fans brigade your social media. Staff spend weeks dealing with harassment, and morale takes a real hit.

choices.text: Launch an emergency fundraising campaign: ‘Fight Back Against Hate’ — converting community outrage into financial support for your antisemitism programs.

outcomes.text: The campaign raises $85,000 in 48 hours. But a Jewish journalist writes a piece questioning whether you’re ‘profiting from pain.’ The criticism stings even if it’s unfair.

scenarios.title: State Anti-BDS Legislation
scenarios.body: Your state legislature is considering a bill that would bar state contractors from boycotting Israel. Pro-Israel groups are pushing hard for it. The ACLU and civil liberties organizations oppose it as a free speech violation. Both sides are pressuring you to take a public position. The bill’s sponsor wants you to testify in favor.

advisorQuotes.text: The free speech argument is winning in the courts. Two similar laws have been struck down. Supporting this could put us on the wrong side of a constitutional question.
My clients care about this. Deeply. If we don’t support this bill, I’ll have trouble explaining why they should keep supporting us.

choices.text: Testify in favor of the bill. BDS is discriminatory and the state has a right to set conditions for its contractors.

outcomes.text: Your testimony is powerful. The bill passes with your name attached. Pro-Israel donors are delighted. Civil liberties groups add you to their opposition list.

choices.text: Oppose BDS as a policy but testify against this specific bill on free speech grounds. Separate the issue from the tactic.

outcomes.text: Your nuanced position impresses editorial boards but pleases neither side’s activists. You’re cited in three op-eds as ‘the reasonable middle.’

choices.text: Stay out of the legislative fight entirely. This is a First Amendment question, not an antisemitism question.

outcomes.text: Both sides are frustrated by your silence. Pro-Israel groups see it as betrayal. Civil liberties groups wanted your moral authority on their side. You preserve flexibility but lose influence.

choices.text: Propose amended legislation that targets discriminatory boycotts broadly — not just Israel — making it a general anti-discrimination measure.

outcomes.text: The amendment gains traction. The broader framing neutralizes the free speech objection. Both sides claim partial victory. Your political sophistication is noticed in the statehouse.
outcomes.contextNote: This outcome required real political capital to achieve.

outcomes.text: Your educational approach is praised as thoughtful, but both sides accuse you of fence-sitting. Young adults want bolder action; older donors want a clear pro-Israel stance.

scenarios.title: Pushing for Hate Crime Charges
scenarios.body: A Jewish teenager was beaten outside a synagogue while attackers shouted antisemitic slurs. The DA is considering whether to file hate crime charges or prosecute it as simple assault. She’s asked your organization to make a public statement supporting hate crime enhancement. The attackers are Black teenagers, and some community leaders warn that your involvement could inflame racial tensions.

advisorQuotes.text: Hate crime charges are important. But the optics of a Jewish organization pushing to put Black kids in prison longer — that’s a conversation we need to think through very carefully.
Justice must be pursued regardless of the identity of the perpetrator. That’s not racism — that’s equality before the law.

choices.text: Issue a strong public statement supporting hate crime charges. Antisemitic violence must carry consequences regardless of who commits it.

outcomes.text: The DA files hate crime charges, citing your statement. The victim’s family is grateful. A local NAACP chapter releases a measured statement disagreeing with your approach but acknowledging the hate element.
The DA files charges but the case becomes racially polarizing. A protest is held outside your office. The victim’s family asks everyone to stop using their son as a political symbol.

choices.text: Support the victim’s family publicly but leave the charging decision to the DA without taking a position on hate crime enhancement.

outcomes.text: The family appreciates the support. Some community members wish you’d pushed harder. The DA files simple assault charges. It feels like antisemitic motivation was erased.

choices.text: Support hate crime charges while simultaneously partnering with Black community leaders to frame this as a shared fight against all hate — not a Black vs. Jewish issue.

outcomes.text: The joint framing is powerful. A Black pastor and your rabbi hold a press conference together. The DA files hate crime charges with broad community support. National media covers it as a model for cross-community accountability.
outcomes.contextNote: This was the hardest path but it built something lasting.

outcomes.text: The joint statement with community leaders is well-received, but some in your base feel you diluted the specifically antisemitic nature of the crime. Orthodox leaders express frustration.

choices.text: Stay out of the legal process entirely. Focus on the victim’s recovery and offer community healing programs.

outcomes.text: The healing programs are meaningful for participants. But the attackers receive light sentences and the Jewish community wonders why no one fought for accountability.

scenarios.title: A Jewish Day School on the Brink
scenarios.body: The only Jewish day school in your area is losing enrollment fast — down 30% in three years. The board is asking your organization to lead a community rescue effort. The school is Modern Orthodox in orientation but serves families across denominations. Saving it would cost significant political capital and budget, but losing it would be a devastating blow to Jewish continuity in your region.

advisorQuotes.text: A community without a day school is a community that has decided the next generation will be less Jewish than this one. We cannot let that happen.
I can raise money for this — but it means every other campaign takes a back seat for six months.

choices.text: Launch a major ‘Save Our School’ campaign with your organization as the lead. Commit significant resources and fundraising capacity.

outcomes.text: The campaign raises $400,000 and enrollment stabilizes. You’re hailed as a communal hero. But your other programs suffered during the push, and some constituents feel you prioritized one segment over others.

choices.text: Help the school modernize: broker partnerships with public schools, create a part-time Hebrew enrichment track, and help them appeal to unaffiliated families.

outcomes.text: The modernization plan is controversial among traditional families but attracts new ones. Enrollment slowly ticks up. The school survives in a different form than before.
The alternative education fund gains applicants, but day school families feel abandoned. Orthodox leaders accuse you of redirecting resources away from traditional Jewish education.

choices.text: Offer to absorb the school’s key programs into your JCC/community center programming rather than saving the school as an independent institution.

outcomes.text: You save the educational content but the school closes. Orthodox families are devastated. Your programming grows but the community feels a real loss.

choices.text: Express sympathy but explain this isn’t your organization’s role. The school’s board needs to solve this themselves.

outcomes.text: The school closes at the end of the year. The community mourns. Your name comes up repeatedly in conversations about ‘who could have helped but didn’t.’

scenarios.title: Should Antisemitism Be Part of DEI?
scenarios.body: The county government is revamping its DEI (Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion) training for all employees and has asked your organization to contribute a module on antisemitism. Some progressive DEI practitioners object, arguing that Jews are white and privileged and antisemitism doesn’t belong in an equity framework. Your staff is divided. Your campus coalition partners have asked you not to push this.

advisorQuotes.text: If antisemitism isn’t part of DEI, then DEI is saying we don’t count. That’s exactly the erasure we’re supposed to be fighting.
I want antisemitism in DEI. But if we push too hard right now, we’ll lose the relationships we need for everything else. Timing matters.

choices.text: Accept the county’s invitation and develop the module, even if it strains your progressive alliances. This is a once-in-a-generation opportunity.

outcomes.text: The module is excellent and well-received by county employees. But three partners in your campus coalition publicly distance themselves, calling it a ‘cooptation of equity frameworks.’
The standalone module is well-crafted, but some DEI practitioners feel excluded from the process. Progressive community members question whether you're undermining broader DEI work.

choices.text: Accept but collaborate with other minority groups on a broader module that includes antisemitism alongside other forms of hatred — shared authorship, shared ownership.

outcomes.text: The collaborative module is stronger for its breadth. Your inclusion is seen as legitimate because it’s part of a larger framework. Some donors wish you’d centered Jewish experiences more.
The collaborative module is praised by progressives, but some Jewish community members feel you gave away too much editorial control. The final product mentions antisemitism less than expected.

choices.text: Decline the county’s invitation. Instead, develop your own standalone antisemitism education program that you control completely.

outcomes.text: Your standalone program is good but reaches a fraction of the audience the county training would have. You missed the moment of institutional access.

choices.text: Decline and publicly challenge the premise that DEI should exclude antisemitism — write an op-ed making the case that any equity framework that excludes Jewish experiences is incomplete.

outcomes.text: The op-ed goes viral. It’s shared 50,000 times and sparks a national debate. The county re-invites you. But your progressive allies feel publicly attacked.

scenarios.title: Evangelical Christians Want to Partner
scenarios.body: Christians United for Israel, the largest pro-Israel organization in America, wants a formal partnership with your organization. They’re offering significant resources: shared events, lobbying access, and a $200,000 annual partnership grant. But their theology includes beliefs about Jewish conversion, they oppose LGBTQ+ rights, and many of your progressive members find the alliance deeply uncomfortable.

advisorQuotes.text: The money and political access are real. In this political climate, we need every ally we can get. Ideology is a luxury.
I have three LGBTQ staff members. How do I look them in the eye and tell them we’re partnering with an organization that fights against their rights?

choices.text: Accept the full partnership. Political alliances require accepting that your allies won’t agree with you on everything.

outcomes.text: The partnership brings real resources and political clout. But staff morale drops, three board members resign in protest, and your progressive programming feels hollow.

choices.text: Accept a limited, issue-specific partnership focused solely on combating antisemitism — not broader political advocacy. Draw clear boundaries.

outcomes.text: The limited partnership works better than expected. You get some resources without the ideological baggage. Both sides occasionally test the boundaries.

choices.text: Decline the formal partnership but agree to collaborate on specific shared concerns, like synagogue security, without any organizational branding.

outcomes.text: A practical compromise. You get security collaboration without the public association. CUFI is mildly disappointed but respects the boundary. Your staff is relieved.

choices.text: Decline entirely and issue a statement explaining that your organization’s commitment to LGBTQ+ inclusion and pluralism is incompatible with this partnership.

outcomes.text: Progressive allies applaud. Pro-Israel donors are upset. CUFI redirects their resources to your rivals. Your staff morale is sky-high but your budget feels the loss.

scenarios.title: The Generational Giving Divide
scenarios.body: Your development director presents a sobering analysis: 70% of your funding comes from donors over 65, and they’re not being replaced. Young Jewish donors give to different causes — climate, racial justice, reproductive rights — and see your organization as ‘their parents’ charity.’ She proposes a radical rebrand targeting younger donors, but your legacy donors’ advisory council is threatening to pull support if you change direction.

advisorQuotes.text: We have maybe five years before the demographics make this decision for us. By then it’ll be too late.
These legacy donors built what you have. You owe them loyalty. And frankly, a bird in the hand is worth ten millennials who might donate someday.

choices.text: Launch the rebrand. New visual identity, new programming focused on issues young Jews care about, new digital-first fundraising strategy.

outcomes.text: The rebrand attracts 200 new donors under 40. But 15 legacy donors reduce their giving, and three cut you off entirely. Net revenue drops 12% in year one.
outcomes.contextNote: This is a bet on the future that may take years to pay off.

choices.text: Create a parallel track: a new initiative with its own brand targeting young donors, while keeping the main organization’s identity intact.

outcomes.text: The dual-track approach works but strains your team. Young donors engage with the new initiative while legacy donors stay. You’re essentially running two organizations.

choices.text: Double down on legacy donors. Deepen relationships with your current base and maximize planned giving before it’s too late.

outcomes.text: Legacy donors respond warmly. Three new planned gifts are confirmed, including one seven-figure bequest. But you’ve accepted a slow demographic decline.
The traditional cultivation approach resonates with established donors, but younger community members see it as doubling down on an aging base. Social media criticism follows.

choices.text: Convene a summit of young Jewish leaders and legacy donors to co-design the organization’s future together. Let them find common ground.

outcomes.text: The summit is emotional and productive. A young tech executive and a 78-year-old Holocaust educator discover shared passions. A new giving circle emerges. But it took months of planning.
The intergenerational dialogue reveals deep tensions about organizational direction. Older donors feel lectured; younger participants feel patronized. The facilitator earns their fee.

scenarios.title: A Rabbi’s Sermon Goes Viral
scenarios.body: A local rabbi delivered a fiery Yom Kippur sermon calling out ‘Jews who stay silent on antisemitism because they’re afraid of losing their progressive credentials.’ The sermon was recorded and posted online, where it’s been viewed 3 million times. Conservative media is celebrating her. Progressive Jews are calling it divisive. She’s one of your board members. Journalists are asking for your reaction.

advisorQuotes.text: She’s a board member speaking in her capacity as a rabbi. We can’t control what she says from her bimah. But we need to decide if we’re standing with her or stepping away.
Three million views. Do you know what organizations would pay for that kind of attention? The question is whether we ride the wave or run from it.

choices.text: Publicly embrace the sermon. Share it on your channels with a statement saying this is the kind of moral courage the Jewish community needs.

outcomes.text: The endorsement brings massive engagement. Conservative donors flood your inbox with checks. But progressive members cancel memberships and two partner organizations pull out of joint programs.

choices.text: Say nothing as an organization. The rabbi spoke as a rabbi, not as a board member. No organizational response needed.

outcomes.text: The media moves on. Some think you dodged responsibility. The rabbi is slightly hurt by the silence. Life continues.

choices.text: Issue a nuanced statement: celebrate the rabbi’s passion while noting that fighting antisemitism requires all political alliances, including progressive ones.

outcomes.text: The statement threads the needle skillfully. Moderates appreciate it. The rabbi respects the addition. Partisans on both sides find it wishy-washy.
Your nuanced statement is praised by moderates but satisfies neither camp. The rabbi feels insufficiently supported; progressives feel you legitimized the sermon's framing.

choices.text: Use the moment to host a community conversation about political divisions within the Jewish community — inviting the rabbi and her progressive critics to the same stage.

outcomes.text: The event is standing room only. It’s tense, emotional, and messy — but people leave feeling heard. Three local media outlets cover it as ‘the conversation American Jews need to have.’
The community conversation draws a big crowd but devolves into shouting when the rabbi's supporters and critics face off. Your organization is blamed for not controlling the event.

scenarios.title: The Litmus Test
scenarios.body: Your most important progressive coalition partner — a racial justice organization you've marched with for three years — is circulating a joint statement for all members to sign. The statement calls Zionism 'a form of settler colonialism' and demands signatories pledge to 'center Palestinian liberation in all anti-racism work.' Refusing to sign means expulsion from the coalition. Signing would alienate most of the mainstream Jewish community and several of your own board members. The deadline is Friday.

advisorQuotes.text: This coalition is our lifeblood. If we lose them, we lose access to every progressive space in this city.
Sign that statement and I'll have three resignations on my desk by Monday. Including possibly mine.
There is a difference between criticizing Israeli policy and denying Jewish peoplehood. This statement erases that line.

choices.text: Sign the statement. Your coalition relationships are your most important asset, and you can work to soften the language from inside.

outcomes.text: You sign. The coalition is relieved. But the Jewish Telegraphic Agency runs the story and three board members resign within a week. Your donor base contracts sharply. Progressive allies praise your courage.
outcomes.contextNote: The Federation president calls to say she's 'deeply disappointed.'

outcomes.text: You sign, hoping to moderate from within. But the coalition immediately uses your signature in fundraising emails as proof that 'even Jewish organizations' agree. You've lost control of the narrative.

choices.text: Refuse to sign but publish your own statement explaining why — affirming Palestinian rights while rejecting the framing of Zionism as colonialism.

outcomes.text: Your statement is thoughtful and widely read. The coalition expels you anyway. Some progressive Jews call it 'the bravest thing a Jewish org has done in years.' You lose coalition access but gain unexpected respect.
outcomes.contextNote: Two smaller progressive Jewish groups quietly reach out about forming an alternative alliance.

outcomes.text: Your nuanced statement is dismissed as 'tone policing' by the coalition and 'not enough' by the Jewish establishment. You've managed to anger both sides.

choices.text: Negotiate privately — ask for amended language that distinguishes between the Israeli government and Jewish self-determination. Offer to sign a revised version.

outcomes.text: After tense negotiations, the coalition agrees to soften two sentences. You sign the revised version. Hardliners on both sides are unhappy, but the partnership survives — barely.
They reject your amendments and interpret your negotiation as stalling. You're expelled anyway, and now they're telling people you tried to 'water down' a racial justice statement.

choices.text: Use your grassroots network to organize a counter-letter from progressive Jews of color, LGBTQ Jews, and Mizrachi Jews arguing the coalition's framing erases diverse Jewish experiences.

outcomes.text: The counter-letter gets 200 signatures and goes viral. It reframes the debate entirely. The coalition tables the original statement for 'further discussion.' You've bought time and shifted the conversation.
outcomes.contextNote: Your organizing background made this possible. No other org could have pulled this off.

scenarios.title: Two Mournings, One Calendar
scenarios.body: Three of your younger staff members — all progressive Jews — have proposed that your organization co-host a joint Yom HaZikaron/Nakba Day commemoration with a Palestinian solidarity group. They argue it's consistent with your values of shared grief and mutual recognition. Your older donors and several board members view Nakba Day commemorations as delegitimizing Israel's founding. The event would fall during your spring fundraising season.

advisorQuotes.text: My team poured their hearts into this proposal. If we shut it down, I'm not sure I can keep them.
I have $180,000 in pledges coming in this quarter. If we do this event, I will lose at least a third of that.

choices.text: Approve the joint commemoration. This is exactly the kind of brave, values-driven programming your organization should be doing.

outcomes.text: The event draws 300 people and is emotionally overwhelming. Both Israeli and Palestinian speakers weep. Young Jews are electrified. Three major donors withdraw their spring pledges entirely.
outcomes.contextNote: A photo from the event — a Jewish grandmother and a Palestinian grandmother holding hands — goes viral.

outcomes.text: The event is disrupted by pro-Israel protesters outside and a walkout by Palestinian attendees who felt the framing was too 'both-sides.' Media coverage focuses on the chaos, not the content.

choices.text: Host your own Yom HaZikaron event that includes Palestinian grief narratives, but don't co-brand with the solidarity group.

outcomes.text: The event is moving and thoughtful. Your staff feels heard. The solidarity group calls it 'half-measures' publicly but privately says it's a step forward. Donors grumble but stay.

choices.text: Decline the proposal. Explain that the organization isn't ready for this and the timing is wrong during fundraising season.

outcomes.text: Two of the three staff members who proposed it start quietly job-hunting. Donors are relieved. Your progressive credibility takes a hit in activist circles.
outcomes.contextNote: Word gets around that JVJ 'couldn't even do a Nakba event.'

choices.text: Use your donor relationships to privately secure bridge funding that would replace any lost pledges, then greenlight the event with financial cover in place.

outcomes.text: You find two progressive foundation grants that cover the gap. The event happens without financial catastrophe. Your team is astonished. You've proven values and fiscal discipline aren't mutually exclusive.
outcomes.contextNote: Your fundraising instincts turned a potential disaster into a proof of concept.

scenarios.title: Is Fighting Antisemitism 'Centering Whiteness'?
scenarios.body: A prominent DEI consultant who trains staff at three of your coalition partner organizations has published an article arguing that Jewish organizations' focus on antisemitism 'centers white grievance' and 'diverts resources from communities facing structural racism.' The article names your organization specifically. Your coalition partners are quietly circulating it. Your staff — half of whom are Jews of color — are furious, but for different reasons.

advisorQuotes.text: This article erases every Mizrachi, Sephardic, and Black Jew in our community. But if we come out swinging, we look like we're proving her point.
Do NOT engage publicly. She has 200,000 followers. You have 8,000. The algorithm will eat you alive.
My staff is split down the middle. Half want to write a rebuttal; the other half think the article makes some valid points. I need you to lead on this.

choices.text: Publish a direct rebuttal centering the voices of Jews of color in your organization — making the case that antisemitism IS a racial justice issue.

outcomes.text: Your rebuttal, authored by three Jews of color on your staff, is powerful and personal. It gets shared widely in progressive Jewish spaces. The consultant doubles down but loses credibility. Coalition partners go quiet.
outcomes.contextNote: The piece is cited in a Forward article about the erasure of Mizrachi and Sephardic Jews in DEI frameworks.

outcomes.text: The rebuttal is perceived as defensive. Twitter dunks pile up. Two coalition partners 'pause' joint programming 'to create space for reflection.'

choices.text: Request a private meeting with the consultant to discuss her claims. Bring your Jews-of-color staff members who want to participate.

outcomes.text: The meeting is tense but productive. She agrees to publish a follow-up acknowledging the diversity of Jewish experience. It's not a retraction, but it's something. Your staff who participated feel empowered.
She refuses to meet and tweets that your organization 'tried to silence a Black woman.' The story becomes about the meeting request, not her article.

choices.text: Don't engage with the article directly. Instead, invest in programming that demonstrates — rather than argues — why antisemitism is a racial justice issue.

outcomes.text: You launch a speaker series on Jews of color, Mizrachi history, and intersectional antisemitism. It takes months to show impact but gradually shifts the conversation. The article fades from memory.
outcomes.contextNote: Sometimes the best response is to build rather than argue.

choices.text: Place an op-ed in a major outlet through your media contacts — not a rebuttal, but a proactive piece on why progressive movements need to take antisemitism seriously.

outcomes.text: The op-ed runs in a national outlet and reframes the entire debate. It's shared by several prominent progressives. The consultant's article is now read in the context of yours, not the other way around.
outcomes.contextNote: Your media relationships turned defense into offense.

scenarios.title: When an Ally Says the Quiet Part Loud
scenarios.body: A progressive city council member your organization endorsed and campaigned for — someone you called 'a true ally' in a press release — has been caught on a hot mic at a fundraiser saying 'the Jewish lobby controls this city's housing policy' and 'I'm sick of rich Jews in [neighborhood] blocking affordable housing.' The audio is circulating on social media. She's calling it 'taken out of context' and asking for your support.

advisorQuotes.text: She's on three committees that matter to us. If we go public, we lose a vote on the hate crimes bill next month.
We endorsed her. Our name is on her campaign literature. Every day we stay silent, our credibility erodes.

choices.text: Publicly rescind your endorsement and issue a statement saying antisemitism from allies is even more dangerous than antisemitism from opponents.

outcomes.text: Your statement goes viral in Jewish media. The council member loses her progressive Jewish support base. She's primaried the next cycle. Your credibility on left-wing antisemitism skyrockets — but you've lost a political ally.
outcomes.contextNote: Three other progressive politicians quietly check their language around Jewish issues.

outcomes.text: Your denunciation is praised by the Jewish community but infuriates progressive coalition partners who see it as disproportionate. Two allied organizations accuse you of 'weaponizing antisemitism to silence a woman of color.'

choices.text: Meet with her privately. Demand a genuine public apology and commitment to antisemitism education — but don't rescind the endorsement yet.

outcomes.text: She agrees to a public apology and an antisemitism training. The apology is decent but not great. Your community is split on whether you handled it well or let her off easy.
She gives a hollow apology that satisfies no one, then tells a reporter you 'pressured' her into it. Now you look like you're both covering for her and bullying her.

choices.text: Turn this into a teaching moment — host a public forum on antisemitism in progressive spaces, inviting the council member to participate (not as the accused, but as a learner).

outcomes.text: The forum draws a standing-room crowd. The council member's participation is awkward but genuine. Several progressive leaders say it changed how they think about antisemitism. But online critics call it 'a rehabilitation tour.'

choices.text: Leverage your political connections to broker a deal: she publicly apologizes, funds an antisemitism education initiative, and you keep the endorsement. Quietly make clear this is her one chance.

outcomes.text: The deal works. She funds a $50,000 education initiative. Her apology is specific and heartfelt — your team helped write it. Both sides save face. Pure political craft.
outcomes.contextNote: Your political instincts turned a crisis into an asset.

scenarios.title: The Staff Zionism Schism
scenarios.body: During an all-staff retreat, a heated argument erupted between your communications director — who identifies as a liberal Zionist — and your outreach coordinator — who identifies as a non-Zionist Jew. The outreach coordinator said she 'doesn't feel safe' working alongside someone who 'supports an apartheid state.' The communications director said she's being asked to deny her Jewish identity. Both are in tears. The rest of the staff is frozen. You're standing at the front of the room.

advisorQuotes.text: If we lose either of them, we lose institutional knowledge that took years to build. But this can't fester.
Machloket l'shem shamayim — argument for the sake of heaven. The tradition says both voices must be heard. But 'safety' language shuts down debate.

choices.text: Affirm that your organization welcomes both Zionist and non-Zionist Jews, and establish clear guidelines that personal views on Israel cannot be used to question a colleague's belonging.

outcomes.text: The guidelines calm things temporarily. The communications director feels vindicated. The outreach coordinator feels her concerns were dismissed as 'just opinions.' An uneasy truce holds.
The outreach coordinator resigns two weeks later, saying the policy 'privileges Zionism as a default.' Her departure letter is posted online and goes viral in progressive Jewish circles.

choices.text: Pause the retreat. Bring in an outside facilitator who specializes in Jewish intra-community conflict to lead a structured dialogue over the next month.

outcomes.text: The facilitator is excellent. Over four sessions, both staff members learn to disagree without dehumanizing. The process becomes a model your org shares publicly. It costs real time and money.
The facilitator struggles. The sessions reopen wounds without healing them. Both employees are now performing their conflict for an audience. It's worse than before.

choices.text: Side with the outreach coordinator. In a progressive organization, anti-Zionist voices need to be centered, not just tolerated.

outcomes.text: The communications director resigns immediately. She writes a blistering op-ed about being 'purged' from a Jewish organization for being a Zionist. The piece is picked up nationally. Your progressive base applauds; everyone else is horrified.
outcomes.contextNote: The op-ed is shared by three members of Congress.
