choices.text: Address the room as a Jewish educator — draw on texts about machloket and the tradition of holding multiple truths. Reframe this as a strength, not a crisis.

outcomes.text: Your impromptu teaching on Hillel, Shammai, and the value of disagreement moves the room to tears. Both staff members hug. It doesn't solve the underlying tension, but it defuses the crisis with grace and gives people a framework for moving forward.
outcomes.contextNote: Your Jewish learning turned a workplace crisis into a moment of communal healing.

scenarios.title: Praised by the Wrong People
scenarios.body: Your latest report on antisemitism on college campuses — documenting left-wing harassment of Jewish students — has been praised in a prominent white nationalist newsletter. They're calling your organization 'useful allies' and sharing your data to argue that 'the real threat to Jews comes from the left, not from us.' The report is solid and factual. But it's now being weaponized. A screenshot of the newsletter endorsement is circulating on Jewish Twitter.

advisorQuotes.text: We cannot control who shares our work. But we can control how fast we distance ourselves. The clock is ticking.
Our donors funded this report. If we apologize for it, we're telling them their money produced something shameful.
This is a test of whether we can condemn antisemitism from all sides or only from the left. The community is watching.

choices.text: Issue an immediate statement condemning white nationalism and explicitly rejecting the newsletter's endorsement, while standing by your report's findings.

outcomes.text: Your statement threads the needle. You stand by the data while making clear white nationalists are the greater physical threat to Jews. Most of the community respects the response. A few donors feel you 'gave the left ammunition.'
outcomes.contextNote: A centrist Jewish organization shares your statement approvingly.

outcomes.text: Your condemnation of white nationalism is seen as performative by critics who note your organization rarely focuses on right-wing antisemitism. The statement helps but doesn't fully resolve the credibility question.

choices.text: Announce a follow-up report on right-wing and white nationalist antisemitism, to be published within 90 days, showing you take threats from all directions seriously.

outcomes.text: The announcement changes the conversation. You're now seen as comprehensive, not one-sided. But producing the follow-up report strains your research team and takes resources from planned projects.
outcomes.contextNote: You've committed to a major new research project on a tight timeline.

choices.text: Don't respond to the newsletter specifically. Your report speaks for itself. Responding elevates white nationalists and validates the guilt-by-association game.

outcomes.text: Your donors appreciate the steadiness. But the screenshot keeps circulating. Progressive Jewish orgs start referring to you as 'the org white nationalists love.' The label sticks longer than you expected.
outcomes.contextNote: Silence is always interpreted, never neutral.

choices.text: Call a press conference to condemn the white nationalist endorsement — and use the moment to announce an expanded research agenda covering antisemitism from all ideological sources.

outcomes.text: The press conference is covered by three major outlets. You pivot the story from 'org endorsed by white nationalists' to 'org takes on antisemitism from every direction.' Your media instincts turned a crisis into a rebrand.
outcomes.contextNote: Your media savvy made this possible. Another org would have been buried by this story.

scenarios.title: The Megachurch Offer
scenarios.body: A 12,000-member evangelical megachurch wants to formalize a partnership with your organization: joint advocacy on Israel, shared security resources, and a co-branded 'Stand With Israel' fundraising campaign they estimate will raise $500,000. The catch: the church's website explicitly states its mission includes 'bringing the Jewish people to Christ' and its pastor has preached that Jews who don't accept Jesus face eternal damnation. He insists these are 'theological' positions separate from the political partnership.

advisorQuotes.text: These people pray for our conversion. Their love for Israel is rooted in end-times theology that requires our spiritual destruction. Half a million dollars cannot buy our dignity.
Half a million dollars. Do you understand what that would mean for our programming? We could fund the campus antisemitism initiative for three years.

choices.text: Accept the partnership. Political alliances don't require theological agreement. Israel needs every friend it can get.

outcomes.text: The money flows. The joint campaign is a massive success. But Reform and Conservative rabbis across your city sign a letter calling the partnership 'a betrayal of Jewish dignity.' Three synagogues cancel co-sponsored events with you.

The partnership launches smoothly at first, but a viral video surfaces of the pastor telling his congregation that 'our Jewish friends will one day accept the truth.' Jewish media runs it alongside your logo. The damage is severe.

choices.text: Accept a limited, issue-specific collaboration on Israel advocacy only — no co-branding, no joint fundraising, and a written agreement that conversion activities won't be linked to the partnership in any way.

outcomes.text: The guardrails hold. The collaboration produces solid Israel advocacy work without the theological baggage. Some money comes in, but far less than the full partnership would have generated. Most of the community accepts the compromise.
The church violates the agreement within three months, distributing 'Messianic' literature at a joint event. You're forced to end the collaboration publicly.

choices.text: Decline the partnership entirely and publish a position paper on why Jewish organizations should not partner with groups that seek Jewish conversion, regardless of shared political goals.

outcomes.text: Your position paper is widely shared and becomes a reference document for other Jewish orgs. You've given up half a million dollars but earned deep respect from the denominational world. Orthodox and Conservative leaders praise you unexpectedly.
outcomes.contextNote: Sometimes the money you refuse defines you more than the money you raise.

choices.text: Counter-propose: you'll work with the church only if they publicly remove conversion language from their website and commit to a 'no proselytizing' covenant, using your halachic expertise to draft the covenant terms.

outcomes.text: Remarkably, the pastor agrees — the political alliance matters more to him than the website language. The covenant you draft becomes a national model for Jewish-evangelical partnerships. Your scholarly approach produced something unprecedented.
outcomes.contextNote: Your Jewish learning created a framework no one else could have written.

scenarios.title: Settler Violence and the Donor Dilemma
scenarios.body: A viral video shows Israeli settlers attacking a Palestinian village in the West Bank while IDF soldiers stand by. The State Department condemns it. Your inbox is flooded. Progressive Jewish groups are demanding every Jewish organization speak out. But two of your top five donors are active supporters of settlement organizations, and one sits on the board of a group named in the video. Your silence is becoming conspicuous.

advisorQuotes.text: I'm going to be blunt: those two donors represent 30% of our annual budget. If they walk, we lay off staff.
We can love Israel and still say this is wrong. If we can't condemn violence against civilians, what exactly do we stand for?

choices.text: Issue a statement condemning the settler violence while reaffirming your commitment to Israel's security. Name the incident specifically.

outcomes.text: Your statement is called 'courageous' by moderates and 'a knife in Israel's back' by hardliners. One of the two settlement-supporting donors withdraws immediately. The other calls to say she respects you but needs time.
outcomes.contextNote: The mainstream Jewish press praises your moral clarity.

outcomes.text: Both settlement donors withdraw. The financial hit is devastating. But unexpected mid-level donors step up, moved by your willingness to speak the truth.

choices.text: Condemn violence 'against all civilians' without naming settlers specifically. Call for the Israeli government to investigate and hold perpetrators accountable.

outcomes.text: The careful framing preserves most donor relationships but satisfies almost no one. Critics call it 'mealy-mouthed.' Supporters call it 'responsible.' The story moves on.
outcomes.contextNote: Studied ambiguity has a shelf life.

choices.text: Stay silent on this specific incident. Redirect attention to a new initiative on antisemitism — the real focus of your organization.

outcomes.text: Your donors are relieved. But the silence is deafening. A Forward columnist writes a piece titled 'When Pro-Israel Becomes Pro-Silence.' Your credibility outside your base takes a lasting hit.
outcomes.contextNote: What you don't say defines you as much as what you do.

choices.text: Privately call the settlement-supporting donors before making any statement. Explain what you need to say and why. Give them the chance to stay or go with dignity.

outcomes.text: Your personal calls make the difference. One donor withdraws but does so privately, without public drama. The other actually increases her gift, saying 'I'd rather fund an org that tells me the truth.' You issue a strong statement with your financial base mostly intact.
outcomes.contextNote: Your fundraising relationships gave you room to do the right thing.

scenarios.title: The Senator's Other Friends
scenarios.body: A US Senator who has been your organization's most reliable ally on Israel legislation — sponsoring bills, speaking at your gala, meeting with your board — has been photographed at a private dinner with a known white nationalist commentator. The Senator's office says it was a 'chance encounter at a large event.' But the photos show them seated next to each other in a group of eight. Jewish organizations across the spectrum are being asked to respond.

advisorQuotes.text: This Senator has delivered for us on every single Israel vote for six years. You torch this relationship, you don't get it back.
My staff is asking me why we haven't said anything. Two of them are Holocaust educators. They are not okay.

choices.text: Publicly demand the Senator explain the dinner and condemn white nationalism. Make clear that your support is conditional on standing against antisemitism from all sides.

outcomes.text: The Senator issues a statement condemning white nationalism but never mentions you again. His office stops returning your calls. The Israel legislation you were counting on stalls. But your moral credibility soars.
outcomes.contextNote: Other organizations notice you held your ally accountable. It sets a precedent.

outcomes.text: The Senator retaliates, calling your organization 'ungrateful' in a radio interview. Your pro-Israel donors who value the political relationship are furious with you.

choices.text: Handle it privately. Call the Senator's chief of staff and make clear this cannot happen again. Don't go public unless he refuses to give assurances.

outcomes.text: The Senator privately assures you it won't happen again. The relationship survives. But progressive Jewish groups are now citing your silence as evidence that right-leaning orgs 'only care about left-wing antisemitism.'
outcomes.contextNote: The accusation of a double standard will follow you.

choices.text: Issue a general statement about the importance of all political leaders rejecting antisemitism — without naming the Senator. It signals concern without burning the bridge.

outcomes.text: Everyone sees through it. A reporter asks 'Is this about Senator [X]?' and your spokesperson stumbles. The non-denial denial becomes the story.

choices.text: Use your political connections to broker a joint statement: the Senator condemns white nationalism, your org reaffirms the partnership, and both of you come out looking principled.

outcomes.text: The joint statement is masterfully crafted. The Senator saves face, you demonstrate accountability, and the political relationship actually strengthens. Textbook political operation.
outcomes.contextNote: Your political instincts resolved what would have been a catastrophe for any other org.

scenarios.title: The Prime-Time Invitation
scenarios.body: A major conservative cable news host wants you on his show to discuss campus antisemitism — a topic your organization leads on. The segment would reach millions. But this host has a history of trafficking in replacement theory rhetoric and recently platformed a guest who blamed 'Jewish elites' for immigration policy. Your appearance would be seen as legitimizing his show. Declining means losing an enormous platform to reach Americans who need to hear about antisemitism.

advisorQuotes.text: Three million viewers. You'll never get that audience anywhere else. And if WE don't fill that chair, someone worse will.
If I see our logo next to that host's face, I will have a staff revolt on my hands. I'm not exaggerating.

choices.text: Accept the appearance. Use the platform to talk about antisemitism AND confront the host about his own show's history with antisemitic content.

outcomes.text: You go on and deliver a strong message. You even push back on the host's past segments. The clip goes viral — both sides claim victory. Conservative donors love it. Your staff is deeply divided.
The host cuts your mic mid-sentence when you mention replacement theory. The edited clip makes you look like you're agreeing with everything he says. Your team is devastated.
outcomes.contextNote: You didn't control the edit. You never control the edit.

choices.text: Accept, but demand the segment be live and unedited, with an agreement that you can address the show's history with antisemitic content.

outcomes.text: They agree to live but reject your condition about addressing their history. You go on anyway. The segment is fine — not great, not terrible. It reaches millions but changes few minds.

choices.text: Decline the appearance and issue a public statement explaining that you cannot appear on a show that has platformed antisemitism, even to discuss antisemitism.

outcomes.text: Your decline statement gets modest coverage. Your staff is relieved and proud. Several donors call it 'naive' and 'leaving the field to our enemies.' The host mocks you on air.

choices.text: Decline the cable appearance but use your media contacts to pitch the same campus antisemitism story to three mainstream outlets simultaneously, ensuring the story gets told on your terms.

outcomes.text: Two of the three outlets run the story. Combined reach is smaller than cable, but the coverage is accurate, nuanced, and entirely on your terms. Your staff is thrilled. Donors respect the strategic thinking.
outcomes.contextNote: Your media relationships gave you an alternative no one else had.

scenarios.title: The Bipartisan Squeeze
scenarios.body: A student group at the local university invited a controversial Israeli politician to speak on campus. Left-wing Jewish orgs are demanding you condemn the invitation. Right-wing Jewish orgs are demanding you defend it. Both are publicly calling on you by name — 'Jewish Civic Partnership must take a stand.' Your bipartisan identity means both sides expect you to agree with them. Staying silent will be read as cowardice by everyone.

advisorQuotes.text: This is the centrist trap. Every time you split the difference, both sides call you spineless. But if you pick a side, you lose your brand.
Our board is exactly as divided as the community. Whatever you do, four board members will be happy and four will be furious.
Forget the orgs. What do the students actually need from us right now?

choices.text: Defend the invitation on free speech grounds while criticizing the politician's specific views. Try to hold both principles simultaneously.

outcomes.text: Your statement is called 'the most centrist thing ever written' — some mean it as praise, others as an insult. It satisfies the intellectual middle but energizes no one. Both camps continue attacking you, just slightly less.

The right seizes on your criticism of the politician; the left seizes on your defense of the invitation. Each side quotes only the half that suits them. You've given both camps ammunition against you.

choices.text: Refuse to take a position on the invitation itself. Instead, publish a statement about the broader crisis of political polarization in the Jewish community and call for dialogue.

outcomes.text: The 'meta' approach is praised by editorial boards and mocked by activists. 'They released a statement about releasing statements,' one Twitter user writes. But your framing — that intra-Jewish polarization is the real threat — resonates over time.

choices.text: Sidestep the political debate entirely. Show up on campus with programming: a teach-in, free food, mental health support for stressed Jewish students regardless of their views.

outcomes.text: Students are grateful. Both the left and right Jewish orgs call you 'irrelevant' — but 85 students show up for your teach-in and three say it's the first time they've felt welcome in a Jewish space. Your staff is proud.

choices.text: Invite both the left-wing and right-wing Jewish orgs to co-sign a joint statement you'll draft — one that affirms free speech, acknowledges the pain, and commits to dialogue. Use your organizing network to pressure both into signing.

outcomes.text: Against all odds, you get four organizations to sign. The joint statement is imperfect but unprecedented. A national reporter calls it 'the first time left and right Jewish orgs agreed on anything in 2026.' Your convening power is now your brand.
outcomes.contextNote: Your grassroots network made the impossible possible.

scenarios.title: The IHRA Definition Divide
scenarios.body: Your city council is considering adopting the IHRA working definition of antisemitism. Pro-Israel groups are pushing hard for it; Palestinian rights groups and the ACLU are opposing it, arguing some examples conflate criticism of Israel with antisemitism. Your centrist coalition — carefully built from members of both camps — is fracturing along exactly this fault line. Both sides expect you to lobby the council on their behalf.

advisorQuotes.text: The IHRA definition is imperfect, but it's the only internationally recognized framework we have. The question is whether 'imperfect but real' beats 'ideal but imaginary.'
If we support IHRA, we lose our progressive coalition partners overnight. I've spent two years building those relationships.

choices.text: Support the IHRA adoption. It's the most widely recognized definition of antisemitism and your community needs this protection, even if it's imperfect.

outcomes.text: The council adopts IHRA with your testimony cited prominently. Pro-Israel donors celebrate. Your interfaith and progressive coalition partners withdraw from two joint programs. Your centrist brand is now questioned by the left.
outcomes.contextNote: You've taken a side. The center may not hold.

choices.text: Propose an amended version that adopts the core IHRA definition but drops the most contested Israel-related examples. Try to build a compromise both sides can live with.

outcomes.text: Your amendment is actually considered by the council. Pro-Israel hardliners call it 'gutting the definition.' ACLU types call it 'still problematic.' But three centrist council members rally behind your version. It passes narrowly.
outcomes.contextNote: Centrism as legislative craft, not just vibes.

outcomes.text: Your amended version fails. Neither side was willing to compromise. The original IHRA definition passes without your support, and you're left looking ineffective — unable to influence an outcome you tried to shape.

choices.text: Oppose the adoption and advocate for a different framework — one that addresses antisemitism without the Israel-related controversies. Propose the Nexus Document as an alternative.

outcomes.text: Your advocacy for an alternative framework is intellectually respected but politically outmatched. IHRA passes anyway. Pro-Israel donors are livid. But progressive partners deepen their trust in you significantly.

choices.text: Invoke Jewish textual tradition to draft a new, original framework that draws on IHRA, Nexus, and Jerusalem Declaration — positioning it as emerging from Jewish scholarly consensus rather than political camps.

outcomes.text: Your framework is published with endorsements from three rabbis across denominations. The council delays the vote to consider it. National Jewish media covers it as 'a third way.' It may not win this fight, but it changes the terms of the debate.
outcomes.contextNote: Your Jewish learning produced something no policy shop could have written.

scenarios.title: The Centrist Donor Exodus
scenarios.body: Three of your most reliable moderate donors — who together represent 25% of your annual giving — have scheduled a joint call with you. They're frustrated. 'We gave to you because you were above the fray,' one writes in the meeting request. 'But lately you seem paralyzed. We want to see decisive leadership, not endless balance. If you can't show us a clear direction in the next quarter, we're moving our giving to organizations that actually do things.'

advisorQuotes.text: They're not wrong. Our last three public statements were so balanced they said nothing. Donors want to fund impact, not diplomacy.
If we lurch in any direction to keep three donors, we'll lose the twenty donors who fund us BECAUSE we're centrist.

choices.text: Present the donors with a bold new initiative — pick a concrete issue (campus safety, interfaith coalition, community security) and commit to being the best in the country at it.

outcomes.text: Your pitch works. The donors are energized by the specificity. Two increase their gifts; one holds steady. But the initiative commits you to a resource-intensive program that will stretch your team.
The donors like the idea but want to see results first. They cut giving by half 'until the initiative proves itself.' You have funding for the pilot but not the full program.

choices.text: Thank them for their candor, defend your approach, and explain that centrist leadership means playing a long game that doesn't generate flashy headlines but holds the community together.

outcomes.text: One donor is moved by your conviction and stays. The other two leave, citing 'philosophical differences.' You've lost 15% of annual giving but preserved your identity.
outcomes.contextNote: Integrity has a price tag. Today it's 15% of your budget.

choices.text: Offer the donors a seat at the table — create a 'Strategic Advisory Council' that gives major donors input on organizational direction in exchange for multi-year commitments.

outcomes.text: All three donors sign three-year pledges. But the advisory council quickly becomes a shadow board, pushing you toward positions that serve their interests. Your staff feels the mission drifting.
outcomes.contextNote: Donor access can become donor control.

choices.text: Proactively present a diversified funding plan that reduces dependence on any single donor group — new foundation grants, a grassroots small-donor campaign, and a planned giving program.

outcomes.text: Your comprehensive plan impresses all three donors. One stays, one leaves, one increases — but more importantly, you launch a small-donor campaign that brings in 200 new supporters at the grassroots level. You're less vulnerable now.
outcomes.contextNote: Your fundraising expertise turned a threat into structural change.

scenarios.title: The Bipartisan Bill Gets Hijacked
scenarios.body: You spent eight months helping craft a bipartisan antisemitism awareness bill — co-sponsored by a Democrat and a Republican. It was your signature achievement. Now, hours before the floor vote, the Republican co-sponsor has added a last-minute amendment that includes language about 'anti-Israel activity on campuses' that would effectively classify BDS organizing as antisemitism. The Democrat says she'll pull her co-sponsorship if the amendment stays. Both legislators are calling you.

advisorQuotes.text: Eight months of work. If this bill dies, it's not coming back in this session. Sometimes you take a flawed win over a principled loss.
If we let this amendment through, every progressive org in the city will say we were always secretly pro-IHRA hardliners pretending to be centrist.

choices.text: Publicly oppose the amendment. Call on the Republican to withdraw it to save the bipartisan bill you all worked on together.

outcomes.text: The Republican is furious and withdraws from the bill entirely, calling you 'ungrateful.' The bill dies. Eight months of work, gone. But progressive partners say your stand proved your centrist credentials are real.
outcomes.contextNote: The bill's death makes local news. You're quoted calling it 'a tragedy of partisan politics.'

outcomes.text: The Republican agrees to withdraw the amendment under pressure. The original bill passes. Both legislators credit you. Your reputation as an honest broker is cemented.

choices.text: Quietly accept the amendment. A flawed bill that passes is better than a perfect bill that dies. You can work to amend it later.

outcomes.text: The bill passes with the amendment. Pro-Israel groups celebrate. The Democrat calls you a 'sellout' on MSNBC. Your progressive credibility is badly damaged. Amending the bill 'later' proves impossible.
outcomes.contextNote: 'We'll fix it later' is the most expensive lie in politics.

choices.text: Ask both legislators to delay the vote by two weeks so you can negotiate a compromise amendment that addresses campus antisemitism without the BDS language.

outcomes.text: The Democrat agrees to delay. The Republican refuses. The bill comes to the floor with the amendment and passes on a party-line vote. Your careful centrism was steamrolled by partisan reality.
Both agree to a two-week delay. You spend 14 brutal days negotiating. The final compromise isn't perfect, but both co-sponsors stay on. The bill passes with genuine bipartisan support.

choices.text: Work the phones behind the scenes — call in favors with both legislators' chiefs of staff, offer political cover for the Republican to withdraw the amendment by arranging a separate Israel resolution he can champion.

outcomes.text: The side deal works. The Republican gets his Israel resolution (symbolic, but he can fundraise off it). The amendment is withdrawn. The original bipartisan bill passes cleanly. Pure political craft saved eight months of work.
outcomes.contextNote: Your political instincts saved the bill and kept both sides at the table.

scenarios.title: The 'Enabler' Profile
scenarios.body: A national Jewish magazine has published a long profile of your organization titled 'The Radical Center: How Centrism Enables Extremism.' The piece argues that your refusal to take sides on Israel, antisemitism definitions, and political alliances 'gives cover to both far-left antisemites and far-right nationalists.' It quotes unnamed board members, former staff, and rival org leaders. It's going viral. Your phone hasn't stopped ringing.

advisorQuotes.text: Never punch down at a magazine piece. It lives for two days unless you extend its life by responding. Let it die.
The unnamed board member quotes are what worry me. Someone on our own board is feeding this narrative. That's an internal problem.

choices.text: Write a forceful op-ed rebuttal defending centrism as a philosophy of conviction, not cowardice — with specific examples of what your approach has actually accomplished.

outcomes.text: Your rebuttal is published in the same magazine. It's passionate and well-argued. It extends the story's life by a week but shifts the narrative. Several prominent moderates share it as 'the best defense of Jewish centrism I've read.'
Your rebuttal is seen as defensive and thin-skinned. The journalist tweets 'they proved my point' with a link to your piece. The story now has legs for another two weeks.

choices.text: Ignore the article publicly but address the internal leak. Hold a board meeting to discuss the unnamed quotes and reaffirm organizational unity.

outcomes.text: The board meeting is tense. Two members admit to speaking with the journalist 'off the record.' One resigns. The internal bleeding stops but the article's narrative goes unchallenged publicly.

choices.text: Don't respond to the article at all. Focus your energy on a public initiative that demonstrates the value of centrism through action rather than argument.

outcomes.text: You launch a bipartisan community safety initiative that same week. The article fades. The initiative generates its own press coverage. You've changed the subject through action.
outcomes.contextNote: The best response to criticism is often just doing good work.

choices.text: Reach out to the journalist privately. Offer exclusive access to your next major initiative in exchange for a follow-up piece that shows centrism in action. Turn a critic into an embedded reporter.

outcomes.text: The journalist agrees. Her follow-up piece — 'Inside the Centrist Experiment' — is nuanced and ultimately sympathetic. She sees the compromises up close and writes about them with respect. The two pieces together tell a fuller story.
outcomes.contextNote: Your media instincts turned a hit piece into a long-form profile.

scenarios.title: The Flag in the Lobby
scenarios.body: An Israeli flag has hung in your JCC lobby since the building opened in 1987. A group of younger members — many of them parents of preschoolers — has formally petitioned the board to remove it, arguing it makes non-Zionist and anti-Zionist Jewish families feel excluded from a space meant to serve all Jews. A counter-petition from older members has gathered twice as many signatures, calling the flag 'non-negotiable.' Both groups are threatening to cancel their memberships.

advisorQuotes.text: The flag has been there for 37 years. Every JCC in America has one. Removing it would make national news — and not the kind we want.
I have three preschool families who've told me they're not re-enrolling because of this. That's $45,000 in tuition.
A JCC is a beit am — a house of the people. ALL the people. The question isn't whether you love Israel. The question is whether every Jew who walks through your doors feels welcome.

choices.text: Remove the flag. A JCC must be a welcoming space for all Jews, and if the flag is preventing that, it needs to go.

outcomes.text: The flag comes down. National Jewish media covers it. Your JCC is called 'anti-Israel' by pro-Israel groups and praised as 'brave' by progressives. 40 older members cancel. 15 younger families join. The net is negative — for now.
outcomes.contextNote: The story is picked up by a conservative news outlet and goes national.

outcomes.text: Removing the flag sparks a broader conversation. Other JCCs call asking how you handled it. Within six months, your preschool enrollment is up 20%. The young families who joined become your most engaged members.

choices.text: Create a new 'identity wall' in the lobby featuring flags and symbols from Israel, the diaspora, and Jewish communities worldwide — contextualizing the Israeli flag within a broader story of Jewish peoplehood.

outcomes.text: The identity wall becomes a talking point — some love it, others see it as a dodge. But the petition energy dissipates. Both sides claim partial victory. Your preschool families stay.

Hardliners on both sides hate the compromise. The counter-petition group says you 'demoted' the Israeli flag. The original petitioners say you 'surrounded it with distractions.' But most members appreciate the effort.

choices.text: Keep the flag. It's been there since 1987 and represents a connection to Israel that is part of the JCC's identity. You won't be bullied into erasing it.

outcomes.text: The older members are relieved. The younger petitioners feel unheard. Three preschool families leave for a secular alternative. A local blog writes about the JCC being 'stuck in the past.'

choices.text: Address the community directly — hold a moderated town hall on what the flag means, what the JCC means, and what it means to be a pluralistic Jewish space. Let the community decide together, using your staff support skills to hold the room.

outcomes.text: The town hall is emotional but well-facilitated. Your ability to hold space for 150 people with deep disagreements impresses everyone. The community votes to keep the flag but add a permanent exhibit on diverse Jewish relationships to Israel. Both sides feel heard.
outcomes.contextNote: Your experience managing difficult group dynamics turned a potential disaster into community building.

scenarios.title: Pride Month at the JCC
scenarios.body: Your program director has planned a Pride Month celebration including a family drag queen story hour, a 'Jewish & Queer' film screening, and rainbow decorations throughout the building. Three Orthodox families who use the JCC daily — including two whose children are in the preschool — have told you they will pull their kids and memberships if the programming proceeds. A group of LGBTQ Jewish parents has written to say this programming is 'the reason we chose this JCC.' Your board is meeting tomorrow.

advisorQuotes.text: These LGBTQ families are some of our most active members. If we cancel this, we're telling them they don't belong here.
The Orthodox families aren't just members — two of them are significant donors. And word will spread to their community fast.

choices.text: Proceed with all planned Pride programming. A JCC that serves all Jews means ALL Jews, including LGBTQ families.

outcomes.text: Pride Month is a hit. Attendance is strong. The LGBTQ families are thrilled and several new members join specifically because of the programming. The three Orthodox families leave. The Orthodox community whispers that your JCC isn't for them.

The story makes the local paper. Conservative commentators call it 'another institution captured by the radical left.' Your membership numbers are a wash — gains offset by losses — but the controversy exhausts your staff.

choices.text: Scale back the most visible elements. Keep the film screening and support groups but cancel the drag story hour and reduce the decorations. Frame it as 'appropriate for all our families.'

outcomes.text: The LGBTQ parents are hurt. 'You edited our existence for someone else's comfort,' one writes in an email to the board. The Orthodox families stay. Your staff feels you compromised their values for money.

choices.text: Proceed with programming but add complementary events: a 'Torah and Identity' series, a traditional family Shabbat, and other content that signals the JCC serves the full spectrum of Jewish life.

outcomes.text: The expanded calendar satisfies most members. Pride programming happens alongside traditional programming. One Orthodox family stays; two leave but say they 'appreciate the effort.' Your schedule is brutal — your staff is stretched incredibly thin.
outcomes.contextNote: Pluralism is exhausting to administer.

choices.text: Before the board meeting, personally reach out to the Orthodox families — not to negotiate, but to listen. Acknowledge their discomfort while explaining why the JCC must be inclusive. Your empathy skills might keep them in the building.

outcomes.text: Your personal conversations are genuinely moving. Two of the three families decide to stay, saying 'we don't agree, but we feel heard.' The programming proceeds. Your ability to sit with discomfort — yours and theirs — made the difference.
outcomes.contextNote: Your burnout survivor's empathy did what policy memos couldn't.

scenarios.title: The Event Space Request
scenarios.body: A local chapter of Jewish Voice for Peace — which supports BDS — has submitted a rental application to use your JCC's main event hall for a panel discussion on 'Human Rights in Israel-Palestine.' Your rental policy says the JCC is available to all Jewish community organizations. JVP argues they are a Jewish organization. Your board has never dealt with this question because no one has ever asked before. The application is technically compliant with your written policy.

advisorQuotes.text: If we say yes, half our donors walk. If we say no, we've just declared which Jews count as Jewish enough for our building. I don't like either option.
This is a legal question as much as a political one. If your policy says all Jewish orgs, and they're a Jewish org, denying them could open you to a discrimination claim.

choices.text: Approve the rental. Your policy is clear, and a JCC that decides which Jews are 'Jewish enough' for the building has lost its way.

outcomes.text: The event happens. It's contentious but nonviolent. Pro-Israel donors are incandescent. Two board members resign. But several younger community members say it's the first time they've felt the JCC 'actually practices what it preaches about pluralism.'
outcomes.contextNote: The Federation puts a hold on your annual allocation 'pending review.'

outcomes.text: The event is peaceful and well-attended. Media ignores it. The sky didn't fall. But the precedent is now set, and three other political groups immediately submit rental applications.

choices.text: Deny the specific event but revise your rental policy to require that all events align with the JCC's mission of 'building Jewish community' — creating a principled framework for future decisions.

outcomes.text: JVP calls it 'a political litmus test dressed up as policy.' Your legal counsel says the new policy is defensible. Most members accept the framework. But the question of who defines 'building Jewish community' now haunts every rental application.

choices.text: Reject the rental and be direct about why: your JCC does not host events organized by groups that support boycotts of Israel, period.

outcomes.text: Your donor base exhales. Pro-Israel groups praise you. JVP holds the event at a church instead and makes your rejection the centerpiece of their publicity. A dozen younger members quietly let their memberships lapse.

choices.text: Approve the rental but propose a condition: the JCC co-hosts, adding a second panel with a range of Jewish perspectives on Israel. Turn a one-sided event into a community dialogue, using your grassroots network to ensure diverse attendance.

outcomes.text: JVP reluctantly agrees to the expanded format. The two-panel event is packed, heated, and ultimately productive. Your JCC is talked about for weeks as a space where real dialogue happened. It's exhausting, expensive, and impressive.
outcomes.contextNote: Your organizing skills turned a potential crisis into the most important community event of the year.

scenarios.title: Women-Only Swim Hours Under Fire
scenarios.body: Your JCC has offered women-only swim hours on Sunday mornings for 15 years — originally created to accommodate Orthodox women who observe modesty laws. Muslim women in the neighborhood have also started attending, making it a quiet interfaith success story. Now a male member has filed a formal complaint calling the policy 'gender discrimination.' A local newspaper has picked up the story. The headline reads: 'JCC Bans Men from Pool — Is It Legal?'

advisorQuotes.text: Tzniut is not a policy preference. It's a religious obligation for these women. Ending these hours would effectively ban Orthodox women from swimming here.
The headline is bad. But the story underneath is actually beautiful — Jewish and Muslim women swimming together. We need to reframe this fast.

choices.text: Keep the women-only hours and defend them publicly as a religious accommodation. Make clear the JCC respects the modesty requirements of its diverse members.

outcomes.text: The complaining member cancels his membership and posts a negative review online. But the Orthodox and Muslim women who use those hours send a joint thank-you letter to your board. It's shared widely as a feel-good story.
A second complaint is filed, this time threatening legal action. Your lawyer says you're probably fine under religious accommodation law, but the legal review costs $5,000.

choices.text: Compromise: reduce women-only hours from two hours to one, and add a men-only hour to demonstrate equal treatment.

outcomes.text: Nobody asked for men-only hours. The men-only slot sits empty. The Orthodox women say one hour isn't enough. The complaining member says you still haven't addressed the core issue. You've created a solution that satisfies no one.
outcomes.contextNote: False equivalence is not compromise.

choices.text: Proactively pitch the interfaith angle to media. Invite a journalist to see Orthodox Jewish and Muslim women swimming together. Turn the story from 'JCC bans men' to 'JCC bridges faiths.'

outcomes.text: A local TV station runs a three-minute segment on the interfaith swim hours. It's charming and human. The original complainant looks petty. New membership inquiries spike for a month. The story completely flips.

choices.text: Draft a formal religious accommodation policy grounded in both halacha and civil rights law — creating a legal and moral framework that protects these hours permanently.

outcomes.text: Your policy document is praised by the JCC Association of North America and adopted as a template by three other JCCs. The legal framework makes future complaints nearly impossible. Your scholarly approach built something that will outlast you.
outcomes.contextNote: Your Jewish learning protected a community practice for generations.

scenarios.title: The Preschool Teacher's Posts
scenarios.body: A parent in your JCC preschool has forwarded you screenshots from a teacher's personal Instagram account. The teacher — beloved by kids and parents, three years on staff — has posted a series of stories calling Israel 'a genocidal apartheid state,' sharing graphic images from Gaza, and writing 'Zionism is racism.' The parent says: 'I'm not sending my four-year-old to be taught by someone who hates our people.' The teacher has never brought politics into the classroom.

advisorQuotes.text: She's our best early childhood teacher. The kids love her. She's never said a word about politics in the classroom. If we fire her for her Instagram, what are we?
Seven parents have emailed me since yesterday. This is going to be a tidal wave. We need to act before it becomes unmanageable.

choices.text: Terminate the teacher. A JCC preschool teacher who publicly calls Zionism racism cannot represent your institution, regardless of what happens in the classroom.

outcomes.text: The teacher is fired. Most parents are relieved. Your staff is shaken — several question whether they need to self-censor their own views to keep their jobs. The teacher hires a lawyer. Three younger staff members start job-hunting.
outcomes.contextNote: A labor reporter is asking questions about your termination process.

choices.text: Meet with the teacher privately. Explain the situation. Ask her to make her social media private and separate her personal views from her professional role at the JCC.

outcomes.text: She agrees to make her accounts private and is grateful you didn't fire her. Most parents accept the resolution. Two parents pull their kids anyway, calling it 'insufficient.' Your staff's trust in you as a fair employer deepens.
She refuses, saying it's a free speech issue and she won't be silenced. Now you're back to square one, but with the added complication that you tried to negotiate and failed.

choices.text: Do nothing about the teacher but institute a new social media policy for all staff going forward. Address the systemic question without singling out one employee.

outcomes.text: The policy satisfies your lawyers. But the complaining parents see it as dodging the question. 'We don't want a policy — we want action,' one emails the board. Three families withdraw from the preschool.

choices.text: Handle the conversation with the teacher yourself — with the care and empathy of someone who's navigated institutional crisis before. Explain what's at stake, listen to her perspective, and collaboratively develop a resolution both sides can live with.

outcomes.text: Your one-on-one conversation is compassionate and direct. She didn't realize the impact of her posts on preschool families. She volunteers to make her accounts private and writes a personal note to concerned parents — not retracting her views, but acknowledging the complexity. Parents are moved. Staff is relieved.
outcomes.contextNote: Your experience with burnout and institutional care made this conversation possible.

scenarios.title: AI-Generated Antisemitism Goes Viral
scenarios.body: A sophisticated AI-generated video depicting Jewish community leaders in your city plotting to control local government has gone viral on TikTok and X. The video is a deepfake — completely fabricated — but it's been viewed 3 million times and local news stations are asking if you have a comment. Your staff has received threatening messages referencing the video. The ADL has issued a national statement, but your community wants to hear from you specifically.

choices.text: Launch an aggressive public campaign exposing the deepfake. Partner with tech companies to get it removed. Hold a press conference with the community leaders depicted in the video.

outcomes.text: Your press conference gets significant coverage and the major platforms remove the video within 48 hours. But the Streisand effect kicks in — clips of the deepfake are now everywhere as people share it to show what you're fighting against. Your staff appreciates the strong response but the budget hit stings.
outcomes.contextNote: The video removal is a win, but copies persist on fringe platforms.

outcomes.text: The platforms are slow to act and your press conference is overshadowed by a news cycle that moves on. Some community members feel you drew more attention to the video than it deserved. Staff morale suffers from the threatening messages that continue.

choices.text: Focus internally. Brief your board, increase security protocols, provide staff with mental health support, and quietly work with law enforcement to identify the video's source.

outcomes.text: Your staff feels genuinely cared for and the security upgrades provide peace of mind. But the community notices your silence and several board members are frustrated that you didn't speak out. A local columnist writes that Jewish organizations are 'hiding from the conversation about AI-enabled hate.'
outcomes.contextNote: Law enforcement opens an investigation but warns it could take months.

choices.text: Build a broad coalition with Muslim, Black, and LGBTQ organizations — all targeted by similar AI deepfakes — and issue a joint statement calling for federal regulation of AI-generated hate content.

outcomes.text: The coalition statement gets national attention and a congressional office reaches out about potential legislation. Your progressive donors love it. But Orthodox community members feel the Jewish-specific nature of the attack was diluted, and two conservative board members express concern about the political company you're keeping.
outcomes.contextNote: The coalition opens doors to new funding sources but also new political obligations.

outcomes.text: The coalition struggles to agree on language. The Muslim organization wants to include Islamophobia in the statement; the LGBTQ group wants to address trans-targeting deepfakes. Three weeks pass before anything is released, and by then the moment has passed. Your community wonders why you outsourced the response.

advisorQuotes.text: This is a 24-hour problem if we move fast, a 24-day problem if we don't. Get ahead of it.
I'm worried about staff safety first. Everything else is secondary until our people feel secure.
We're not the only community being targeted by this technology. There's power in solidarity.

scenarios.title: Holocaust Memorial Under Revision
scenarios.body: The city council has proposed redesigning the downtown Holocaust memorial to include panels recognizing the Armenian genocide, the Rwandan genocide, and the enslavement of African Americans. The proposal has the support of the mayor and several interfaith groups. Some Holocaust survivors' families are devastated — they say it erases the specificity of the Shoah. Others in your community see it as an opportunity for broader relevance. The council wants your organization's official position before next month's vote.

choices.text: Oppose the redesign publicly. The Holocaust memorial exists to remember a specific genocide. Diluting it dishonors survivors and their families. Issue a formal statement and organize community testimony at the council hearing.

outcomes.text: Your testimony is powerful and several council members reconsider. Holocaust survivors' families are deeply grateful. But the mayor's office is frustrated with your opposition, and interfaith partners feel you prioritized Jewish memory over universal human rights. A prominent Black pastor publicly questions whether Jewish organizations truly care about other communities' suffering.
outcomes.contextNote: The council delays the vote by 90 days. The debate will continue.

outcomes.text: The council proceeds despite your opposition. Your stance is characterized in the local press as insular and self-interested. Younger community members are divided — some respect the principled stand, others are embarrassed.

choices.text: Support the redesign with conditions: the Holocaust panels must remain central and undiminished, with additional panels placed in a separate but connected area. Work with the council to ensure respectful implementation.

outcomes.text: The compromise is well-received by the council and most interfaith partners. Some survivors' families feel betrayed — 'You negotiated with our memory,' one writes in a letter to the editor. Your development director warns that two major donors who are children of survivors have gone quiet.
outcomes.contextNote: The mayor thanks you personally, which may open doors later.

outcomes.text: The compromise works. The redesigned memorial opens six months later with your organization acknowledged as a key partner. Attendance at the memorial doubles. Several new donors approach you, impressed by your leadership.

choices.text: Enthusiastically endorse the expanded memorial. Frame it as an evolution of Holocaust remembrance — 'Never Again means never again for anyone.' Lead the fundraising effort for the new panels.

outcomes.text: Progressive allies are thrilled and the interfaith community rallies around you. But the backlash from within the Jewish community is fierce. The Orthodox council calls your position 'a betrayal of the dead.' Three survivor families publicly withdraw their support. Conservative and older donors are furious.
outcomes.contextNote: A national Jewish publication runs an op-ed calling your position 'well-intentioned but historically irresponsible.'

choices.text: Stay out of it. The memorial is a city project, not a Jewish organizational issue. Focus your energy on your own programming and let the community members testify individually.

outcomes.text: The council interprets your silence as indifference. Both sides of the debate are disappointed — those opposing the redesign wanted your institutional weight, and those supporting it wanted your moral authority. Your board chair is privately irritated that you avoided a leadership moment.
outcomes.contextNote: Sometimes not choosing is itself a choice — and the community notices.

advisorQuotes.text: The Torah commands us to remember. But it also commands us to see the stranger's suffering. This is not a simple question.
Whatever we decide, we need to hear from the survivors' families first. Their voice matters more than ours here.
The mayor is watching. Our position on this will affect every city hall ask for the next three years.

scenarios.title: Day School on the Brink
scenarios.body: Hillel Academy, the only Jewish day school in your area, has announced it will close at the end of the school year unless it raises $1.2 million in the next 90 days. Enrollment has dropped 40% over five years. The school's board chair — a major donor to your organization — is asking you to lead the rescue effort. Eighty-three families and two dozen teachers are affected. The building itself is a community landmark.

choices.text: Lead an emergency fundraising campaign. Commit significant organizational resources — staff time, donor lists, event infrastructure — to saving Hillel Academy. Make it your top priority for the quarter.

outcomes.text: Your campaign raises $800,000 — impressive but short of the goal. The school negotiates a scaled-down program that keeps doors open for one more year. The community credits your leadership. But your own programming suffered during the push — two planned events were canceled and your development director is burned out.
outcomes.contextNote: The school's long-term viability remains uncertain.

outcomes.text: The campaign falls short at $500,000. The school closes anyway. Families are grateful you tried, but some feel you gave them false hope. Your staff is exhausted from a fight you ultimately lost.
outcomes.contextNote: The school building is sold to a developer within six months.

choices.text: Facilitate a merger discussion between Hillel Academy and the Conservative synagogue's supplementary school. Propose a hybrid model that preserves some day school programming within an existing institution.

outcomes.text: The merger talks are productive but politically fraught. Orthodox families won't send their kids to a Conservative institution. The supplementary school resents being asked to absorb another program's problems. After weeks of negotiation, a compromise emerges that satisfies no one completely but preserves Jewish education in the community.
outcomes.contextNote: The hybrid model launches with 35 students — a third of the original enrollment.

outcomes.text: The merger talks collapse when the synagogue board refuses. Both institutions blame each other. Your role as mediator is appreciated by some but resented by the school's board chair, who feels you didn't fight hard enough for a full rescue.

choices.text: Express sympathy but stay out of the operational rescue. The day school is a separate institution with its own board. Offer to help affected families find alternatives and provide transition support for teachers.

outcomes.text: The school closes. Families are devastated and some blame you for not stepping up. The board chair — your major donor — reduces his annual gift by half. But your organization's programs remain strong, your staff isn't stretched, and several families redirect their involvement to your programming.
outcomes.contextNote: Six months later, three former day school families become active volunteers in your programs.

advisorQuotes.text: If we lead this campaign, we're pulling resources from our own annual fund. That's a real cost.
Jewish education is the foundation of Jewish continuity. If we don't fight for it, who will?

scenarios.title: The Tenured Problem
scenarios.body: Dr. Richard Harmon, a tenured political science professor at the state university, has a documented history of antisemitic statements in his lectures — comparing Israeli policies to Nazism, questioning the 'outsized influence of the Jewish lobby,' and reportedly telling a Jewish student to 'check her Zionist privilege.' The university's DEI office investigated and found 'insufficient evidence of discriminatory conduct.' Three Jewish students have now filed formal complaints, and their parents are calling your office daily.

choices.text: Go directly to the university president. Demand a formal review of Dr. Harmon's conduct and threaten to mobilize donors and alumni if the university doesn't act.

outcomes.text: The president agrees to a new review under pressure. Dr. Harmon is placed on a 'teaching improvement plan' — not fired, but monitored. Jewish students feel heard. But Dr. Harmon gives an interview claiming he's being silenced for 'criticizing Israel,' and the ACLU files a friend-of-the-court brief supporting his academic freedom. You're now at the center of a free speech debate you didn't want.
outcomes.contextNote: The case becomes a national talking point in the academic freedom debate.

outcomes.text: The president pushes back, calling your approach 'heavy-handed.' The university issues a statement about academic freedom. You've burned political capital and the professor is now more emboldened, not less.

choices.text: Work with the students to document everything carefully. Help them file complaints through proper channels — Title VI, the Department of Education's Office for Civil Rights. Build the legal case methodically.

outcomes.text: The OCR investigation takes six months but produces a finding of 'hostile environment.' The university is required to implement training and monitoring. It's not the dramatic victory some wanted, but it creates lasting institutional change. The students are exhausted but vindicated.
outcomes.contextNote: The OCR finding sets a precedent that other campuses will reference.

outcomes.text: The OCR investigation drags on and ultimately finds 'insufficient evidence' at the federal level. The students feel the system failed them. Parents are furious that you chose the slow institutional route instead of going public.

choices.text: Organize a public teach-in on campus antisemitism. Invite scholars, students, and community members. Don't name Dr. Harmon specifically — make it about the broader environment, not one professor.

outcomes.text: The teach-in draws 200 people and generates thoughtful media coverage. Several non-Jewish faculty members attend and privately express support. But the students who filed complaints feel you avoided the real issue — 'We don't need awareness, we need accountability,' one writes in the campus paper.
outcomes.contextNote: The teach-in becomes an annual event, building long-term campus relationships.

outcomes.text: Dr. Harmon shows up at the teach-in and dominates the Q&A, reframing the conversation around 'Zionist censorship of Palestinian voices.' The event becomes a spectacle. Your community is embarrassed.

choices.text: Quietly offer the students legal representation and connect them with national advocacy organizations that specialize in campus antisemitism. Stay behind the scenes — let the students lead.

outcomes.text: The students, now backed by experienced advocates, file a high-profile Title VI complaint. The case gets national attention and your organization is credited behind the scenes. You've empowered the next generation without making it about your institution.
outcomes.contextNote: The students become vocal advocates for Jewish campus life after graduation.

advisorQuotes.text: If you have the clout, use it. The president will listen to power, not to process.
Be careful about making this about one professor. The free speech crowd will eat us alive.
The students should own this fight. Our job is to support them, not replace them.

scenarios.title: The Definition War
scenarios.body: A national coalition of Jewish organizations is circulating a public declaration that anti-Zionism is a form of antisemitism. They want your signature before a coordinated press release next week. The declaration has already been signed by 30 organizations. Progressive Jewish groups are organizing a counter-statement arguing the declaration 'silences legitimate criticism of Israeli policy and alienates young Jews.' Both sides are pressuring you to take a position. Your board is split almost evenly.

choices.text: Sign the declaration. Anti-Zionism denies Jews the right to self-determination and is functionally indistinguishable from antisemitism in its consequences. Stand with the mainstream Jewish community.

outcomes.text: Your signature is welcomed by the coalition and prominently featured in the press release. Major donors are pleased — several increase their annual commitments. But your young professionals group loses a third of its members overnight. Three progressive staff members request a meeting to express their 'deep discomfort.' A campus Hillel director publicly distances herself from your position.
outcomes.contextNote: The declaration receives national media coverage. Your name is now associated with one side of this debate permanently.

outcomes.text: The declaration becomes a lightning rod. Academics, progressives, and some Jewish voices push back hard. Your organization is protested at a community event. The Orthodox community and older donors stand firmly behind you.

choices.text: Decline to sign. Issue your own nuanced statement acknowledging that anti-Zionism can be antisemitic in some forms but that the declaration is too broad and risks chilling legitimate discourse about Israeli policies.

outcomes.text: Your nuanced statement is praised by academics, some rabbis, and progressive allies. But it satisfies neither side fully — the coalition sees you as a defector, and progressives say you didn't go far enough. Your board chair calls it 'the bravest and loneliest statement we've ever made.'
outcomes.contextNote: Several smaller organizations privately tell you they wish they'd taken your approach.

outcomes.text: The nuance is lost in the media coverage. Headlines reduce your position to 'Jewish org refuses to call anti-Zionism antisemitism.' Conservative donors are alarmed. Your development director warns of a potential 15% drop in major gifts.

choices.text: Sign the counter-statement. Anti-Zionism is a political position, not a form of bigotry. Conflating the two endangers both free speech and the fight against actual antisemitism.

outcomes.text: Progressive Jewish groups celebrate your courage. Young Jews flood your social media with support. But the mainstream Jewish establishment is furious. You are quietly removed from two communal planning committees. Four major donors — representing 20% of your annual revenue — suspend their giving.
outcomes.contextNote: Your decision makes national Jewish media. There is no going back from this position.

choices.text: Stay silent. Don't sign either statement. Tell both sides you're 'still deliberating' and hope the news cycle moves on.

outcomes.text: Both sides interpret your silence as cowardice. The coalition publicly lists you as a 'non-signatory' — implying you're soft on antisemitism. The counter-statement organizers list you as 'declined to participate.' Your board is divided on whether silence was wisdom or weakness. Staff morale drops as they field calls from angry community members on both sides.
outcomes.contextNote: In a community this small, there is no neutral ground. Everyone noticed your absence.

advisorQuotes.text: This is a question about truth, not politics. We must say what we believe, even if it costs us.
I've already had three calls from donors this morning. They want to know where we stand. And by 'where we stand,' they mean they want us to sign.
If we sign that declaration, we lose every young Jew under 30 who still cares about this organization. I'm not exaggerating.

scenarios.title: An Unlikely Partnership
scenarios.body: The Islamic Center of Greater Metro has proposed a formal partnership with your organization: shared programming, joint advocacy on hate crimes legislation, and a co-sponsored community dialogue series. Their imam is widely respected and the offer is genuine. But the Islamic Center has also hosted speakers critical of Israel, and two of your board members have already expressed concerns about 'legitimizing anti-Israel voices.'

choices.text: Accept the partnership fully. Co-host events, issue joint statements, build real institutional ties. This is what interfaith work looks like when it's serious, not performative.

outcomes.text: The partnership launches with a packed joint event. Local media covers it as a feel-good story. Progressive donors are enthusiastic. But when the Islamic Center posts about Palestinian solidarity on social media the following week, your phone rings off the hook. Orthodox community members publicly question your judgment.
outcomes.contextNote: The partnership is real but requires constant navigation of the Israel question.

outcomes.text: The partnership becomes a model for interfaith cooperation in the region. A foundation approaches you with a $50,000 grant for joint programming. The political costs are real but the institutional benefits compound over time.

choices.text: Accept a limited partnership focused exclusively on hate crimes advocacy. Keep joint programming off the table until you've built more trust. No joint statements on anything related to the Middle East.

outcomes.text: The limited partnership works. You co-author testimony on a state hate crimes bill that passes. Neither side is fully satisfied — the imam wanted deeper ties, your board wanted none at all — but the relationship is functional and growing slowly.

choices.text: Decline the formal partnership. Express appreciation but explain that your board isn't ready. Offer to attend each other's public events as guests, not partners.

outcomes.text: The imam is disappointed but gracious. Your conservative board members are relieved. But when a mosque in the suburbs is vandalized two months later, the Islamic Center doesn't call you — they call another Jewish organization. The missed opportunity stings.
outcomes.contextNote: Relationships not built are doors that close.

advisorQuotes.text: The Talmud teaches that we are responsible for one another. It does not specify which 'one another.'
I need to know: has anyone vetted what the imam has said about Israel? Because my donors will.

scenarios.title: The Tainted Gift
scenarios.body: Marcus Feldstein, your second-largest annual donor at $200,000 per year, has been indicted by the SEC for cryptocurrency fraud. His name is in every news outlet. His foundation — which funds your youth programming — is expected to be frozen by the courts. He's called you personally asking you to issue a statement of support, calling the charges 'politically motivated.' Your development director says losing his gift would create a $200,000 hole in next year's budget.

choices.text: Cut ties immediately and publicly. Return any recent donations that may be tainted. Issue a statement about your commitment to ethical fundraising. Take the financial hit.

outcomes.text: The community respects your integrity. Two donors increase their gifts, citing your ethical stand. But the $200,000 gap is real — you'll need to cancel or scale back youth programming unless you find replacement funding fast. Your development director is already working the phones.
outcomes.contextNote: Doing the right thing has a price tag.

choices.text: Say nothing publicly. Quietly distance from Feldstein without a formal announcement. Don't return donations — they were given legally at the time. Hope the story doesn't reach your community.

outcomes.text: It works for three weeks. Then a reporter asks if your organization has received donations from Feldstein's foundation. Your 'no comment' becomes the headline. The story is now worse — you look like you were trying to hide the connection.
The story fades without reaching your community. Feldstein's case drags on for years. You quietly phase out the relationship. No one notices.

choices.text: Issue a carefully worded statement expressing support for Feldstein's family while noting that your organization 'takes all legal and ethical matters seriously.' Don't return donations. Don't cut ties. Thread the needle.

outcomes.text: The statement is criticized from both sides. Ethics watchdogs say you're enabling. Feldstein feels you didn't go far enough in his defense. Your staff is uncomfortable with the ambiguity. But the money keeps flowing — for now.
outcomes.contextNote: The middle ground is often the loneliest ground.

advisorQuotes.text: $200,000 is not a number we can replace quickly. I need you to understand what cutting ties actually means for our programs.
Our reputation is worth more than any single gift. If the money is dirty, we can't keep it.

scenarios.title: The Missing Generation
scenarios.body: Your annual community survey reveals a stark finding: participation by Jews under 30 has dropped 35% in two years. Exit interviews paint a consistent picture — young Jews feel the organized Jewish community is 'out of touch,' 'too focused on Israel,' and 'run by and for older wealthy donors.' A group of young Jewish professionals has started their own independent havurah that meets in apartments and coffee shops. They're not hostile to your organization — they just don't see the point of it.

choices.text: Invest heavily in new programming designed by and for young adults. Hire a young professionals coordinator, launch a fellowship, and give the under-30 cohort real decision-making power — including a board seat.

outcomes.text: The new programming attracts 40 young adults in the first month. Energy is high. But your older members feel displaced — 'We built this organization and now we're being pushed aside for TikTok programming,' one board member says. Two veteran donors reduce their giving, arguing you're chasing trends.

The programming launch fizzles. The young professionals coordinator quits after four months, frustrated by institutional bureaucracy. The independent havurah is still thriving. You've spent money and political capital with little to show for it.

choices.text: Reach out to the independent havurah. Don't try to absorb them — partner with them. Offer your resources (space, funding, institutional support) in exchange for nothing except a loose affiliation.

outcomes.text: The havurah leaders are cautiously interested. They accept meeting space and a small programming grant. They don't want your logo on their events, but they start sending members to your larger community gatherings. It's a slow bridge, not an immediate win.
outcomes.contextNote: The best relationships start without expectations.

choices.text: Commission a deeper study. Before changing anything, understand exactly what's happening and why. Bring in an outside consultant to do focus groups, interviews, and a strategic plan.

outcomes.text: The consultant's report is thorough and damning: your programming is designed for a community that no longer exists. The recommendations require a fundamental organizational transformation that your board isn't ready for. The report sits on a shelf. Meanwhile, another 15% of young members drift away.
outcomes.contextNote: Studies that gather dust are worse than no study at all.

advisorQuotes.text: Stop asking why they're leaving and start asking what they're building without us. That's where the answer is.
We can't program our way out of a relevance crisis. This is about institutional culture, not event calendars.

scenarios.title: Safety at What Cost?
scenarios.body: Your organization has been awarded a $150,000 federal security grant for facility hardening — cameras, reinforced doors, panic buttons. The grant requires a formal partnership with local police, including regular patrol coordination and information sharing. Your security committee is thrilled. But several staff members and community members of color have raised concerns: the local police department has a documented pattern of racial profiling, and a formal partnership could alienate members who don't feel safe around police.

choices.text: Accept the grant and the police partnership. Security is non-negotiable in the current threat environment. The grant money is desperately needed and you can't protect your community without law enforcement cooperation.

outcomes.text: The security upgrades are completed within two months and community members report feeling safer. But three staff members of color request transfers, and your DEI committee sends a formal letter of concern. A local racial justice coalition removes you from their steering committee.
outcomes.contextNote: Physical safety and emotional safety are not always the same thing.

choices.text: Accept the grant but negotiate modified terms: private security firm instead of police patrols, no information sharing agreement, police contact only for active emergencies. It may cost more but preserves community trust.

outcomes.text: The funder agrees to modified terms after extensive negotiation. The security upgrades proceed with a private firm. It costs more out of pocket but avoids the police partnership controversy. Your DEI committee is relieved. The security committee grumbles that you're 'prioritizing politics over safety.'

The funder rejects your modifications — the police partnership is a non-negotiable condition. You're back to square one, now with the added embarrassment of having tried to negotiate around the requirement.

choices.text: Decline the grant. Issue a statement explaining that while security is a priority, you cannot partner with a police department that has failed communities of color. Pursue alternative security funding.

outcomes.text: Progressive allies applaud your stand. Your staff of color feel seen and valued. But conservative community members are livid — 'You turned down $150,000 for security because of politics?' The synagogue across town accepts the same grant and gets the upgrades you didn't. Your facility remains vulnerable.
outcomes.contextNote: Alternative security funding will take at least a year to secure.

advisorQuotes.text: We can't be the organization that turned down security money. Not after Pittsburgh. Not after Poway.
Ask the Black and brown members of our community how they feel about police patrols in our building. Then decide.

scenarios.title: The Reckoning
scenarios.body: Rabbi Sarah Winters, a beloved figure in the community who has served as scholar-in-residence at your organization's annual retreat for eight years, has been publicly accused of sexual harassment by three former students at the rabbinical seminary where she teaches. The accusations span a decade. Rabbi Winters denies all allegations and has retained a lawyer. Your annual retreat is in six weeks and she's the keynote speaker. Tickets are already sold. Three local rabbis have called you today — two asking you to stand by her until the process plays out, one asking you to cut ties immediately.

choices.text: Remove Rabbi Winters from the retreat immediately. Issue a statement that your organization takes all allegations of sexual misconduct seriously and that you stand with survivors. Find a replacement speaker.

outcomes.text: Survivors' advocacy groups praise your swift action. Most community members support the decision. But Rabbi Winters' supporters — and there are many — accuse you of a rush to judgment. Two board members who are close friends of the rabbi resign. The replacement speaker draws half the expected attendance.
outcomes.contextNote: The seminary later suspends Rabbi Winters pending its own investigation.

outcomes.text: The decision triggers a community-wide debate about due process vs. believing survivors. Your organization becomes the focal point of an argument you didn't start. Staff are emotionally exhausted from fielding calls from both sides.

choices.text: Keep Rabbi Winters on the program but add a panel on ethics and accountability in Jewish leadership. Frame it as a 'both/and' — you can engage with her scholarship while the process plays out, and use the moment for broader community conversation.

outcomes.text: The panel is well-attended but the presence of Rabbi Winters overshadows it. Several attendees walk out of her keynote in protest. A local news crew shows up. The 'both/and' approach is mocked on social media as 'having your cake and platforming an alleged harasser too.'

Against the odds, the retreat is productive. The ethics panel generates real conversation. Rabbi Winters delivers a powerful but chastened keynote. Several attendees say it was the most meaningful retreat in years — though the controversy hangs over everything.

choices.text: Cancel the retreat entirely. The situation is too fraught and any decision about Rabbi Winters will be controversial. Refund tickets and reschedule for next year when there's more clarity.

outcomes.text: The cancellation is seen as an abdication of leadership. 'They couldn't even make a decision,' one board member tells a colleague. The refunds cost you $15,000 in non-recoverable deposits. Both sides of the debate think you were a coward — just for different reasons.
outcomes.contextNote: Leadership is making hard calls. This wasn't one.

advisorQuotes.text: Whatever we do, we need to be able to look survivors in the eye and say we acted with integrity.
Jewish law requires us to hear both sides. Removing her before any finding is a violation of that principle.
If we keep her and something else comes out, we're done. The risk calculus here is clear.

scenarios.title: Staff Rebellion
scenarios.body: Four of your six program staff members have signed a letter refusing to promote or coordinate Birthright Israel trips, calling the program 'a propaganda tool that whitewashes the occupation.' They're not quitting — they love their other work — but they won't touch anything Birthright-related. Your organization has been a Birthright campus partner for 12 years. The Birthright office is asking why your applications have stalled.

choices.text: Tell staff that promoting organizational partnerships is part of their job. If they can't fulfill their duties, they need to decide whether this is the right workplace for them. Birthright stays.

outcomes.text: Two staff members comply resentfully. Two resign, including your most talented program coordinator. The office atmosphere is toxic for weeks. Birthright is preserved, donors are satisfied, but you've lost institutional knowledge and trust that took years to build.
outcomes.contextNote: Replacing the staff members will take months and cost more than keeping them.

outcomes.text: All four comply after a tense meeting. No one quits, but no one forgets either. The letter circulates on social media through anonymous staff accounts. Jewish Twitter has opinions.

choices.text: Drop the Birthright partnership. Your staff's conscience matters and you can connect young Jews to Israel through alternative programs that allow for more critical engagement.

outcomes.text: Staff morale soars. Progressive young Jews applaud the decision. But Birthright's national office is furious and your name is quietly removed from partnership lists. Conservative donors see this as a rejection of Israel itself. The Orthodox council issues a statement calling your decision 'a capitulation to anti-Israel extremism.'
outcomes.contextNote: The decision makes national Jewish media.

choices.text: Find a compromise: maintain the Birthright partnership but add alternative Israel programming that includes Palestinian perspectives. Let staff choose which programs they work on.

outcomes.text: The compromise holds — barely. Staff appreciate the alternative programming. Birthright stays but loses some energy.
