The new Israel programming draws a different audience than expected: older community members who want to understand 'what the kids are thinking.' It costs more to run two tracks but preserves internal peace.
outcomes.contextNote: Compromise often means no one is happy, but everyone can live with it.

advisorQuotes.text: These are our best people. If we force their hand, we lose them. And we can't afford to lose them.
Birthright is a litmus test for the donor community. Dropping it sends a message far beyond the program itself.

scenarios.title: When Allies Go Wrong
scenarios.body: Congresswoman Diana Torres has been your organization's strongest political ally for five years — sponsoring legislation on antisemitism, attending your events, and securing federal funding for your programs. Last night, at a private fundraiser (recorded by a guest), she said that 'Jewish donors have too much influence in American politics' and made a joke about 'controlling the media.' The audio is everywhere. She's issued a non-apology calling the comments 'taken out of context.' Her office is asking if you'll stand by her.

choices.text: Condemn the remarks unequivocally and publicly. Call on Congresswoman Torres to apologize and undergo antisemitism awareness education. Make clear that allyship doesn't excuse antisemitism.

outcomes.text: Your statement is widely shared and praised. 'This is what principled leadership looks like,' writes a columnist. But Torres' office goes cold — the federal grant you were expecting is suddenly 'under review.' A political consultant warns that you've made a permanent enemy in someone who still has significant power.
outcomes.contextNote: Political capital is real, and you just spent some.

outcomes.text: Torres refuses to apologize and doubles down, claiming you're overreacting. She pivots to attacking 'the pro-Israel lobby' and your organization is now the face of the story. You're dragged into a news cycle you can't control.

choices.text: Handle it privately. Call Torres directly. Tell her the comments were unacceptable and that you need a real apology — not for the cameras, but because the relationship depends on it.

outcomes.text: Torres listens. She issues a fuller apology the next day, specifically naming antisemitic tropes and committing to education. The relationship is strained but intact. Community members who wanted public fireworks are disappointed, but the substantive outcome is better than a public war.
outcomes.contextNote: Private pressure sometimes achieves what public shaming cannot.

outcomes.text: Torres says she'll 'think about it' and then does nothing. Your private approach failed and now you look weak for not speaking publicly. The community wonders why you're protecting a politician who traffics in antisemitic tropes.

choices.text: Issue a measured statement expressing 'deep concern' about the comments while acknowledging Torres' record of support. Call for dialogue, not punishment.

outcomes.text: 'Deep concern' becomes a punchline. Social media mocks your tepid response. 'Imagine if she'd said this about any other minority,' one commentator writes. Torres takes your mild response as tacit support and continues to invoke your relationship in her defense. You've pleased no one.

advisorQuotes.text: She has three years left in her term and she chairs the subcommittee that funds us. Think carefully about making an enemy.
If we don't condemn this, we become the organization that gave a pass to antisemitism because we liked the politician. That's our legacy.

scenarios.title: Statement on Gaza
scenarios.body: A major escalation in Gaza has dominated the news for two weeks. Israeli airstrikes have killed over 200 Palestinians including children. Hamas rockets have reached Tel Aviv. Two Israeli civilians are dead. Your inbox has 400+ unread emails. Half demand you condemn Hamas unequivocally. Half demand you condemn the Israeli military response. Your board chair says you must say something this week. Your development director says whatever you say will cost you donors. She's right.

choices.text: Issue a statement focused on Israel's right to self-defense. Condemn Hamas terrorism. Express sorrow for civilian casualties on both sides but emphasize that Hamas bears responsibility for operating in civilian areas.

outcomes.text: Conservative donors and Orthodox community members rally behind you. AIPAC shares your statement approvingly. But progressive members are devastated — 20 young professionals resign from your mailing list in 48 hours. Your social media becomes a battleground. Two staff members request meetings to discuss their 'moral distress.'
outcomes.contextNote: You are now publicly positioned on one side of the most divisive issue in Jewish life.

choices.text: Issue a statement mourning all loss of life. Call for an immediate ceasefire. Don't assign blame. Emphasize the need for a political solution that ensures security for Israelis and dignity for Palestinians.

outcomes.text: The ceasefire call is praised by J Street and some Reform rabbis. But the 'both sides' framing infuriates everyone with strong feelings — pro-Israel voices say you're equating a democracy with a terror group, pro-Palestinian voices say you're whitewashing a military assault. The statement satisfies almost no one.
outcomes.contextNote: In a community this polarized, the center is often the most dangerous place to stand.

outcomes.text: The statement opens space for a community conversation you host the following week. 80 people show up. It's painful and heated but real. Several participants say it's the first time they felt safe expressing doubt in an institutional Jewish setting.

choices.text: Issue a statement focused on Palestinian suffering. Call the military response disproportionate. Demand the U.S. condition military aid on human rights compliance. Stand with the progressive Jewish movement.

outcomes.text: Progressive allies and young Jews celebrate your moral clarity. But the backlash is severe. You lose six major donors within a week. The local federation distances itself from you publicly. Orthodox and Conservative community leaders issue a joint statement condemning your position. You are now a pariah in half the Jewish communal world.
outcomes.contextNote: Courage and isolation often look the same.

choices.text: Don't issue a statement. Focus on providing pastoral care — organize havdalah gatherings, create space for processing, bring in trauma counselors. Let others make political statements; you'll focus on the human dimension.

outcomes.text: The pastoral approach is appreciated by many but seen as evasive by others. 'Havdalah is lovely but it's not leadership,' one board member says. Your silence is interpreted differently by different audiences — some see wisdom, others see cowardice. Staff appreciate not being in the crossfire.
outcomes.contextNote: Sometimes the bravest act is to hold space instead of holding a microphone.

advisorQuotes.text: Every word we say will be weaponized by someone. Say what is true, not what is safe.
I have donors on both sides of this. Whatever we say, plan for a 15% revenue hit. The only question is which 15%.
Our young people need us to say something real. Not corporate, not legal, not safe. Real.

scenarios.title: The Banned Speaker
scenarios.body: The state university has disinvited an Israeli diplomat from speaking on campus after student government passed a resolution calling her visit 'an endorsement of apartheid.' The diplomat was invited by the campus Hillel. Jewish students are outraged. The ACLU is considering weighing in on free speech grounds. The university president says the decision was made for 'safety concerns' but the student government resolution makes the real motivation clear.

choices.text: Condemn the disinvitation as a violation of free speech and academic freedom. Demand the university reinstate the event. Offer your facility as an alternative venue if the university refuses.

outcomes.text: The university president reverses the decision under pressure, citing free speech principles. The diplomat speaks to a packed room with heavy security. The event is disrupted by protesters but proceeds. You're credited with leading the fight. Conservative media covers you approvingly; progressive outlets less so.
outcomes.contextNote: The free speech argument proved more effective than the pro-Israel argument.

outcomes.text: The university doubles down. Your offer to host draws protesters to your building. The diplomat ultimately speaks at your venue to 60 people instead of 600. The story becomes about your organization, not about free speech or Israel.

choices.text: Support the Jewish students' right to hear diverse speakers but don't make this about Israel specifically. Frame it as a free speech issue that affects all communities.

outcomes.text: Your free-speech framing attracts unlikely allies — conservative faculty, libertarian students, and even some progressive organizations concerned about censorship precedent. A broader coalition letter carries more weight than a Jewish-only response. The students feel supported without the issue being weaponized.

choices.text: Stay out of it. This is a campus matter for Hillel and the students to handle. Your organization intervening would be overstepping and could inflame the situation.

outcomes.text: The students are disappointed by your absence. The campus Hillel director asks pointedly what your organization actually does if not stand up for Jewish students. The story fades but the relationship with campus Jewish life is damaged.

advisorQuotes.text: Frame this as free speech, not as Israel. You win more allies and avoid the Israel debate entirely.
The students need us now, not after a committee meeting. Show up.

scenarios.title: The Membership Question
scenarios.body: A longtime board member has proposed that your organization formally adopt an inclusive membership policy welcoming intermarried families — including those where the non-Jewish spouse has not converted — as full community members with voting rights and program access. Currently, your policy is ambiguous. The Orthodox members of your board call the proposal 'a betrayal of Jewish continuity.' Several intermarried families who are already active members say the current ambiguity makes them feel like 'second-class Jews.'

choices.text: Adopt the inclusive policy. Full membership for intermarried families, no asterisks. Issue a public statement welcoming all families who want to participate in Jewish community life.

outcomes.text: Intermarried families are overjoyed. Fifteen new family memberships come in the first month. Progressive rabbis praise your decision. But three Orthodox families withdraw, and the local Orthodox rabbi calls the policy 'a step toward the dissolution of Jewish identity.' Your organization is now seen as taking a side in one of Judaism's most sensitive debates.
outcomes.contextNote: The new families bring energy, children, and fresh perspectives.

choices.text: Create a 'community friend' category for non-Jewish spouses — full program access but no voting rights on matters of Jewish religious practice. Voting rights on operational matters.

outcomes.text: The compromise is seen as reasonable by most but loved by no one. Intermarried families appreciate the gesture but notice the distinction. 'Community friend,' one spouse says, 'is just a polite way of saying I don't fully belong.' Orthodox members are somewhat mollified but remain wary.
outcomes.contextNote: The category creates a new administrative burden that no one anticipated.

choices.text: Table the proposal. Say you need more time for community input. Form a committee to study the issue and report back in six months.

outcomes.text: Everyone knows what 'forming a committee' means — nothing will change. The board member who proposed the policy resigns in frustration. Two intermarried families that were on the fence about joining decide to go elsewhere. The committee meets twice and produces a 30-page report that recommends... further study.
outcomes.contextNote: Committees are where courage goes to die.

advisorQuotes.text: This is not just a policy question. It's a theological question. Who is a Jew? How do we define community? These deserve more than a board vote.
The intermarried families are already here. They're volunteering, donating, sending their kids to our programs. The policy should reflect reality, not ideology.

scenarios.title: The Numbers Are In
scenarios.body: The FBI's annual hate crime report shows a 37% increase in antisemitic incidents nationwide — the highest in 40 years. Your organization has been tracking local data that tells the same story. Media outlets are calling for comment. This is an opportunity to raise your profile, drive fundraising, and advance your mission. But how you frame the data matters: do you emphasize the threat, the community's resilience, or the systemic failures that allow hate to grow?

choices.text: Lead with alarm. Hold a press conference emphasizing the severity of the threat. Use the data to argue for increased security funding, hate crime legislation, and community vigilance.

outcomes.text: The press conference generates significant media coverage. Two legislators reach out about co-sponsoring hate crime legislation. Donations spike 20% in the week following. But some community members feel you're fearmongering, and parents call worried about sending their kids to Jewish programs.
outcomes.contextNote: Fear is an effective fundraising tool. It's also corrosive to community well-being.

choices.text: Frame the data in context. Yes, incidents are up, but Jewish community life is thriving. Emphasize resilience, the strength of communal institutions, and specific actions community members can take.

outcomes.text: The resilience framing is well-received by community members who are tired of fear-based messaging. Local rabbis incorporate your talking points into Shabbat sermons. But the media is less interested in resilience than in alarm, and your coverage is limited. A national Jewish org that led with fear raises three times what you do.

choices.text: Use the data to build coalitions. Frame antisemitism as part of a broader hate epidemic and push for comprehensive civil rights legislation that addresses all forms of bias.

outcomes.text: The coalition approach attracts new allies and a foundation grant for cross-community programming. But critics say you're diluting the Jewish-specific nature of the threat. An op-ed in a Jewish newspaper asks: 'Why can't Jewish organizations just focus on Jews?'

advisorQuotes.text: The data is only useful if we control the narrative. Let's get ahead of it.
This is a fundraising moment. I'm not being cynical — the community needs to understand the threat, and they show they understand by giving.

scenarios.title: Guards or Welcome Mats?
scenarios.body: After a suspicious individual was spotted photographing your partner synagogue's exits, the security committee wants armed guards at all community events. The rabbi opposes it — 'A synagogue with armed guards sends a message of fear, not faith.' Several families with young children say visible weapons make them uncomfortable. Other families say the absence of visible security makes them nervous. The congregation is split and looking to you for guidance since you coordinate community-wide security programming.

choices.text: Recommend armed security at all community events. Trained professionals, concealed carry where possible, visible at entrances. Safety is non-negotiable.

outcomes.text: Armed guards are deployed within two weeks. Most community members adapt quickly. But two families with young children stop attending Shabbat services. The rabbi's sermons take on a melancholic tone. 'We are doing what is necessary,' she says, 'but something has been lost.'

choices.text: Recommend enhanced security that isn't visible: better locks, panic buttons, security cameras, trained volunteer ushers. Reject the 'fortress mentality.'

outcomes.text: The discreet approach is implemented. The rabbi is relieved. Families with children return to regular attendance. But the security committee members who pushed for armed guards are frustrated — 'You're choosing aesthetics over lives.' When a JCC in another state has a security incident, they send you the article without comment.
outcomes.contextNote: Invisible security requires constant vigilance and maintenance.

choices.text: Propose a hybrid: concealed-carry trained staff (volunteers who are already members and licensed), plus enhanced physical security. No visible outside guards.

outcomes.text: The hybrid approach is accepted by most. Three congregation members volunteer for concealed-carry training. The rabbi is uncomfortable but acknowledges the compromise. The security committee is partially satisfied. It's not perfect, but it's workable.

advisorQuotes.text: After Pittsburgh, I don't want to be the person who argued against security. I can't live with that responsibility.
Children shouldn't grow up thinking synagogue is a place that needs soldiers. That's not the Judaism I want to pass on.

scenarios.title: The Viral Staffer
scenarios.body: Your communications coordinator, Maya Chen, posted a personal Instagram story showing her at a pro-Palestinian protest over the weekend holding a sign that reads 'End the Occupation.' The post has been screenshot and shared widely. Conservative community members are demanding she be fired. Progressive members are defending her right to personal political expression. Maya is an excellent employee — she's been with you for three years and her work is outstanding. She's also scared.

choices.text: Terminate Maya's employment. Your organization cannot have a communications coordinator whose personal views directly contradict your stakeholders' expectations. The community trust damage is too severe.

outcomes.text: Maya is fired. Conservative donors and community members are satisfied. But the rest of your staff is shaken — several begin updating their LinkedIn profiles. A group of young Jewish professionals starts a petition calling the firing 'McCarthyist.' Maya's story is picked up by Jewish Currents and The Forward.
outcomes.contextNote: Your remaining staff now know that personal political expression can cost them their jobs.

choices.text: Keep Maya but have a firm conversation about social media boundaries. Implement a new personal social media policy for all staff. Issue a statement that employees' personal views don't represent the organization.

outcomes.text: Maya agrees to the new policy. Most community members accept the 'personal views' distinction. But the most vocal critics aren't satisfied — they wanted consequences, not policies. The new social media policy creates a chilling effect on all staff expression, which some see as necessary and others see as oppressive.
The policy works well and actually clarifies expectations for everyone. Maya is grateful and becomes an even more dedicated employee. The controversy fades.

choices.text: Publicly defend Maya's right to personal political expression. Frame it as a values issue — your organization believes in free speech even when it's uncomfortable. Make no policy changes.

outcomes.text: Progressive staff and young community members are energized by your stand. Maya is deeply loyal. But three board members threaten to resign and your largest donor calls to express 'grave concern about the organization's direction.' You've taken a position that goes beyond the specific incident into broader political territory.
outcomes.contextNote: Defending free speech for your employees costs different things than defending it in the abstract.

advisorQuotes.text: If we fire her for attending a protest, what does that say about who we are? Is this the organization we want to be?
My donors don't care about her rights. They care about the image of this organization. And right now, that image is damaged.
We need a policy, not a political statement. Handle this professionally, not ideologically.

scenarios.title: The Reparations Question
scenarios.body: Your organization is part of a racial justice coalition that has been working on housing discrimination and criminal justice reform. The coalition has now voted to endorse a federal reparations study bill and is asking all member organizations to sign the endorsement letter. The bill doesn't commit to reparations — only to studying them. But signing puts your organization on record in a politically charged debate that has nothing to do with your core Jewish mission.

choices.text: Sign the endorsement. Your organization's commitment to racial justice should extend to difficult conversations. A study isn't a commitment to a specific policy — it's a commitment to learning.

outcomes.text: Coalition partners are grateful and your relationship deepens. Progressive donors applaud. But conservative Jewish voices are scathing: 'Since when is a Jewish organization's job to endorse reparations?' Several older donors — including Holocaust survivors who drew their own parallels to German reparations — are confused and upset.
outcomes.contextNote: The Jewish community's own reparations history makes this issue more complicated than it appears.

choices.text: Decline to sign but remain in the coalition. Explain that this issue is outside your organizational mandate. Support the coalition's other work and offer to contribute to the broader racial justice agenda in areas closer to your mission.

outcomes.text: The coalition accepts your position but with visible disappointment. 'You're with us when it's easy,' one coalition leader says. The relationship cools but doesn't break. Your board is relieved. Your progressive staff members are quietly frustrated.

choices.text: Leave the coalition. This is a bridge too far and the trajectory of the coalition's demands will only get more difficult. Redirect your racial justice work to direct service programs that don't require political endorsements.

outcomes.text: Conservative community members approve. But the coalition publicly names you as an organization that 'chose comfort over justice.' The story is covered in local media. Your progressive staff members are demoralized and two begin job searching. The direct service programs you launch are good work, but they lack the systemic impact of coalition advocacy.
outcomes.contextNote: Doors that close are hard to reopen.

advisorQuotes.text: Our coalition partners showed up for us after the synagogue shooting. They're asking us to show up for them now. That's what solidarity means.
This is not our fight. We have a hundred things to do that are clearly in our lane. Why are we venturing into someone else's?

scenarios.title: Bread and Boundaries
scenarios.body: The regional food bank has approached your organization to partner on a hunger relief program in a low-income neighborhood with a significant Jewish population. The partnership would dramatically expand your reach — serving 400 families monthly instead of 80. But the food bank's inventory isn't kosher, and they can't guarantee separate handling. Your Orthodox constituents who rely on kosher food assistance would be left out. The food bank says it's all or nothing — they can't run two parallel systems.

choices.text: Accept the partnership. Serve all 400 families. Separately fundraise for a small kosher food pantry to supplement what the partnership can't provide for Orthodox families.

outcomes.text: The expanded program is a massive success — 400 families served, media coverage, volunteer engagement soaring. The supplementary kosher pantry serves 30 Orthodox families but is a logistical headache. Orthodox leadership appreciates the effort but feels it was an afterthought.
outcomes.contextNote: The program becomes your most visible community initiative.

choices.text: Decline the partnership. Instead, invest in expanding your existing kosher food program. Serve fewer families but maintain the religious standards that define Jewish communal feeding.

outcomes.text: Your kosher program expands from 80 to 120 families — meaningful but far from the 400 the partnership would have reached. Orthodox community members are grateful. Others wonder why you turned down an opportunity to help hundreds of hungry people over a dietary requirement that most of them don't observe.
outcomes.contextNote: The food bank partners with a church instead.

choices.text: Counter-propose: accept the partnership but negotiate a kosher section within the food bank's system. Offer to fund the additional infrastructure needed. Make it work for everyone.

outcomes.text: The food bank agrees after extensive negotiation. The kosher section is small but real — 60 families served with proper kosher handling. The broader partnership serves 340 others. It costs more than either alternative alone, but it's genuinely inclusive. Your staff is exhausted from the logistics but proud.
The food bank rejects the counter-proposal — the kosher infrastructure is too complex for their operation. You're back to the original choice: all or nothing.

advisorQuotes.text: Feeding the hungry is a mitzvah. Keeping kosher is a mitzvah. When two commandments conflict, we must find the way that honors both.
400 families. That's 400 families. We have to say yes to this.

inboxScenarios.subject: RE: Celebrity donation offer — your thoughts?
inboxScenarios.body: Just got off the phone with management for Elijah Cole — the actor who was dropped by his agency last month after those antisemitic comments. He's offering $75,000 to any Jewish organization that will publicly accept it. The board is split. Some see it as a redemption arc; others think this is a PR disaster waiting to happen. What do you want to do?

choices.text: Accept the gift and issue a statement saying we believe in redemption and accountability.

outcomes.text: The story gets coverage. Half the community is moved; half is furious. The $75k comes in.

choices.text: Decline publicly and explain that accepting would compromise our credibility on antisemitism.

outcomes.text: You are praised by those who care most about the antisemitism issue. You lose the $75k. Your moral clarity is noted.

choices.text: Accept quietly, with no press statement.

outcomes.text: The donation is discovered by a journalist two months later. The story is worse than if you'd been transparent.

choices.text: Decline for now but invite him to engage with our programming before revisiting.

outcomes.text: He attends two events. The community is cautiously open. He donates $25k with no strings attached six months later.

advisorQuotes.text: $75K is $75K. And the redemption angle could actually play well.
Accepting money from someone who attacked our community? I can't explain that to my team.

expiredOutcome.text: You never responded to the board chair's email about Elijah Cole's donation offer. He accepted another organization's invitation and publicly thanked them. Your board chair is frustrated by the silence.
expiredOutcome.contextNote: Failing to respond to time-sensitive opportunities has real costs in Jewish communal life.

inboxScenarios.subject: Urgent: Intern social media post going viral
inboxScenarios.body: Our summer intern just posted a thread on X comparing our organization's work to 'Jewish supremacy' and tagging several journalists. It's getting traction. She says it's her personal account but she identifies herself as an intern here in her bio. HR is asking what to do. She's been one of our best interns.

choices.text: Terminate the internship immediately and issue a statement distancing the org.

outcomes.text: The decision is defensible but generates a secondary story about workplace culture. She becomes a minor cause célèbre.

choices.text: Issue a private reprimand and update your social media policy with clearer guidelines.

outcomes.text: She stays, removes the post, and continues to do excellent work. The policy update was overdue anyway.

choices.text: Defend her publicly as a private individual exercising free expression, while clarifying your org's position.

outcomes.text: Staff morale surges. Some donors are furious. A complicated outcome.

choices.text: Say nothing publicly and handle it in a quiet internal conversation.

outcomes.text: It doesn't pass. A journalist calls for comment. Your silence becomes the story.

expiredOutcome.text: The viral social media post continued spreading while you said nothing. A journalist wrote a piece about your organization's 'non-response' that was shared widely. The intern eventually deleted the post on her own, but the damage was done.
expiredOutcome.contextNote: In the age of social media, silence is rarely neutral — it's interpreted as either complicity or incompetence.

inboxScenarios.subject: Grant opportunity — strings attached
inboxScenarios.body: The Hartmann Foundation is offering us a $150,000 unrestricted grant. The catch: they want us to co-sign a letter that takes a position on Israel we've intentionally stayed neutral on. The foundation has been pushing several organizations to take this position in exchange for funding. The letter is not extreme, but it would be our first formal position on the issue.

choices.text: Accept the grant and co-sign the letter. $150k is too important to pass up.

outcomes.text: The money comes in. Board members who valued our neutrality are upset. You've set a precedent that money buys positions.

choices.text: Decline and tell the foundation we don't take positions in exchange for funding.

outcomes.text: You lose the $150k but preserve your independence. The foundation respects the answer and may return with no strings.

choices.text: Negotiate: accept the grant but water down the letter to a vague statement of values.

outcomes.text: Both sides feel they got something. Critics notice the correlation between the gift and the letter.

choices.text: Decline and publish a statement about donors trying to buy positions, without naming the foundation.

outcomes.text: The statement goes modestly viral in Jewish professional circles. Several foundations contact you impressed by your integrity.

advisorQuotes.text: The Hartmann Foundation gives to everyone. If we say no, they'll just fund our competitors.
Once you let a donor buy a position, you never get that independence back.

inboxScenarios.subject: Inbound from Rep. Baumann's office
inboxScenarios.body: Just got a call from Congressman Baumann's chief of staff. The Congressman wants to attend our next major event and give remarks — he'll bring press. He's been a strong ally on Israel and antisemitism, but he voted against the Voting Rights Act last year, which is a red line for several of our coalition partners. Do we invite him?

choices.text: Invite him — he's been a strong ally on our core issues and we don't demand perfection.

outcomes.text: He attends, the event gets press coverage. Two coalition partners quietly pull back from joint programming.

choices.text: Decline and explain that the Voting Rights vote makes it impossible to host him at this time.

outcomes.text: Coalition partners are gratified. Several of the Congressman's donors quietly make smaller gifts to your org.

choices.text: Invite him but don't publicize his attendance in advance.

outcomes.text: He attends. A journalist tweets his presence live. The 'quiet' strategy is immediately exposed and criticized.

choices.text: Counter-propose a private meeting instead of a public appearance — build the relationship without the optics.

outcomes.text: He agrees. The private relationship proves valuable six months later when you need an urgent congressional ask.

inboxScenarios.subject: FWD: Concerns re: your leadership
inboxScenarios.body: I'm forwarding this reluctantly. A group of five board members has sent a letter to the full board raising concerns about your leadership style and recent decisions. They're not calling for termination but they want a formal performance review moved up from June to next month. I'm with you, but you need to know this is happening.

choices.text: Request a meeting with the full board to address concerns head-on before the formal review.

outcomes.text: Your directness earns respect. The review happens quickly and concludes without formal action.

choices.text: Work through the Vice Chair to understand the specific concerns before responding formally.

outcomes.text: You learn the concerns are mostly about communication style, not substance. An easy fix once you know.

choices.text: Push back firmly — the review timing is inappropriate and this looks like an attempt to destabilize leadership.

outcomes.text: The pushback causes the five members to dig in. The conflict becomes an organizational drama that distracts everyone.

choices.text: Use your political relationships to identify who is driving this and quietly address their specific concerns.

outcomes.text: Two of the five come around quickly once their specific concerns are addressed. The formal review becomes a formality.

advisorQuotes.text: I know these five. Two are genuinely concerned; three are playing politics. Handle accordingly.
The staff already knows something is happening. If you don't address this fast, rumors will fill the vacuum.

expiredOutcome.text: You didn't respond to the board revolt, and the five members interpreted your silence as arrogance. They successfully moved up the performance review and framed it publicly as a 'leadership accountability measure.' The review was painful.
expiredOutcome.contextNote: Board dynamics move fast when left unmanaged. In Jewish organizations, silence is almost never interpreted charitably.

inboxScenarios.subject: CONFIDENTIAL: Something isn’t right with the accounts
inboxScenarios.body: I need to talk to you urgently. I found discrepancies in our event revenue — roughly $23,000 is unaccounted for over the last eight months. The pattern points to our bookkeeper, Janet, who’s been with us for twelve years. I haven’t told anyone else. What do you want me to do?

advisorQuotes.text: If this is real, the board needs to know immediately. We have fiduciary responsibilities.

expiredOutcome.text: You didn’t respond to Miriam’s urgent email. She went to the board chair directly. The board launched an investigation without your knowledge, and your leadership looks absent at best, complicit at worst.
expiredOutcome.contextNote: The board chair is furious you ignored this.

choices.text: Immediately hire a forensic accountant, place Janet on paid leave, and inform the board chair confidentially. Follow the process.

outcomes.text: The forensic audit confirms the discrepancies. Janet is terminated and the funds are partially recovered. The board commends your decisive handling. Staff is shaken but trusts the process.

choices.text: Talk to Janet directly first. She’s been a trusted employee for twelve years — she deserves a chance to explain before you escalate.

outcomes.text: Janet breaks down and admits to taking the money during a personal crisis. She offers to repay. You now face an impossible choice — and if this leaks, your initial handling looks like a cover-up.
outcomes.contextNote: Your board chair hears about the conversation from another staff member two days later.

outcomes.text: Janet denies everything and lawyers up. By confronting her first, you’ve given her time to cover tracks. The forensic accountant you hire later finds less evidence.

choices.text: Ask Miriam to quietly gather more evidence before taking any action. You need the full picture before making accusations.

outcomes.text: Miriam finds more discrepancies — it’s closer to $35,000. The additional evidence makes the case airtight, but the delay means more money went missing. The board questions why you waited.

choices.text: Ask Miriam if she’s absolutely sure. Twelve years of trust is not nothing. Could there be an accounting error?

outcomes.text: Miriam is sure and is now worried you won’t act. She documents everything and sends it to the board treasurer as a CYA measure. You’ve lost control of the situation.

inboxScenarios.subject: Invitation: University Task Force on Campus Climate
inboxScenarios.body: I’m writing to invite your organization to join a new university task force on campus climate. The task force will make binding recommendations on speech codes, event policies, and bias reporting. We want Jewish community representation. However, I should note that the SJP (Students for Justice in Palestine) chapter has also been invited, and the task force requires consensus-based decision-making.

advisorQuotes.text: A seat at the table is always better than shouting from outside. Even if SJP is there, you’ll have veto power on consensus items.

choices.text: Accept. A seat on this task force gives us real institutional power to shape campus policies on antisemitism.

outcomes.text: The task force is grueling but productive. You secure a bias reporting system that explicitly includes antisemitism. Working alongside SJP is uncomfortable but leads to a surprising mutual understanding on some issues.
The task force becomes deadlocked on Israel-related speech. SJP blocks your antisemitism proposals and you can’t reach consensus. Six months of work produces a watered-down report.
outcomes.contextNote: But your presence prevented worse outcomes.

choices.text: Accept but only if the task force adopts the IHRA definition of antisemitism as its working framework.

outcomes.text: The university declines your condition. SJP issued a statement calling your demand ‘an attempt to silence Palestinian voices.’ You’re off the task force before it begins.

choices.text: Decline. The consensus requirement means SJP can block everything. This is a trap dressed up as inclusion.

outcomes.text: The task force proceeds without you. Its recommendations include a speech code that many Jewish students find chilling. You’re left criticizing from the outside.

choices.text: Accept and privately coordinate with sympathetic faculty members to build a majority bloc before the first meeting.

outcomes.text: Your behind-the-scenes coalition-building pays off. The task force produces strong recommendations on antisemitism and campus safety. Your political skills impressed the university president.
outcomes.contextNote: The president begins consulting you on other campus issues.

inboxScenarios.subject: Documentary access request — American Jewish community
inboxScenarios.body: I’m an award-winning documentary filmmaker working on a film about the American Jewish community’s internal debates over Israel, antisemitism, and identity. I’d like to embed with your organization for three months — filming board meetings, staff conversations, and community events. Full editorial control stays with me, but I’ll share a rough cut before release. This could be the most important film about American Jews in a decade.

advisorQuotes.text: This is either the best PR opportunity we’ll ever get or the worst. There’s no middle ground with documentary filmmakers.

choices.text: Grant full access. This is a chance to show the world the complexity and humanity of Jewish communal life.

outcomes.text: The filmmaker captures beautiful and raw moments. The rough cut is powerful but includes an uncomfortable board argument about Israel. The film premieres at a major festival to critical acclaim.
The filmmaker captures a staff member’s off-the-record criticism of a major donor. The rough cut makes your organization look dysfunctional. She refuses to cut the scene.
outcomes.contextNote: You have no editorial control. That was the deal.

choices.text: Grant limited access: public events and on-the-record interviews only. No board meetings, no internal conversations.

outcomes.text: The filmmaker accepts the terms but the resulting footage feels sanitized. Your segment of the film is forgettable.

choices.text: Decline but offer to connect her with community members who can participate as individuals, not as representatives of your organization.

outcomes.text: The filmmaker moves on to another organization. The film comes out and your rivals get the spotlight. Several community members ask why you passed on the opportunity.

choices.text: Accept only if she agrees to a ‘sensitivity review’ — not editorial control, but the right to flag factual errors and request context be added.

outcomes.text: She agrees to the sensitivity review. The film is nuanced and compelling. Your organization is portrayed as principled but human. It wins an audience award.

inboxScenarios.subject: Open letter: We need you to disavow Zionism
inboxScenarios.body: We are the Jewish Students for Action chapter at State University. We are writing to demand that your organization publicly disavow Zionism as a political ideology and acknowledge that it has caused harm to Palestinian people. We believe this is necessary for your organization to credibly fight antisemitism. We plan to publish this letter with your response (or non-response) on Friday.

advisorQuotes.text: These are Jewish kids. Our kids. Even if we disagree with them completely, we need to treat this with care.
Zionism is integral to Jewish identity for the vast majority of Jews worldwide. Disavowing it would be disavowing our community.

expiredOutcome.text: You didn’t respond. The students published the letter with the note ‘Organization declined to respond.’ Both sides interpreted your silence unfavorably — as cowardice or as tacit agreement.

choices.text: Respond firmly: Zionism — Jewish self-determination — is not something we will disavow. Period. Publish the response before they publish theirs.

outcomes.text: Your response is unambiguous and widely shared. Most of the Jewish community applauds. The students call you ‘part of the problem.’ Conservative media celebrates you.

choices.text: Respond thoughtfully: explain the diversity of Zionist thought, reject the binary framing, and invite the students to a dialogue about what a just future looks like.

outcomes.text: Your nuanced response doesn’t make headlines but earns respect from educators and moderates. Three of the student signatories quietly accept the dialogue invitation.

choices.text: Don’t respond to the letter directly. Instead, publish your own statement about the organization’s values — what you stand for, not what you’re against.

outcomes.text: The positive framing works for your base but the students publish the exchange as evidence of evasion. ‘They wouldn’t even answer the question’ trends locally.

choices.text: Reach out directly to the student leaders before Friday. Meet with them in person. Have an honest, off-the-record conversation.

outcomes.text: The meeting is raw and emotional. You don’t change their minds, but they agree to soften the letter and delay publication. One student later joins your campus advisory board.

inboxScenarios.subject: FWD: Delicate situation with the Abramowitz family
inboxScenarios.body: David Abramowitz — our second-largest donor — just called me in a panic. His 19-year-old daughter was arrested at a pro-Palestinian protest last night. She’s fine, but it’s in the news and the family is mortified. David is asking us to issue a statement saying we ‘respect diverse viewpoints within the Jewish community.’ He’s never asked us for anything personal before. His annual gift is $150,000.

advisorQuotes.text: $150,000 a year. That’s two staff positions. I’m not saying we sell our soul, but we should find a way to help him.
We can’t issue organizational statements to bail out donors’ personal family situations. That’s a line we can’t uncross.

choices.text: Issue the statement David requested. It’s vague enough to be unobjectionable and it helps a loyal donor in a moment of personal crisis.

outcomes.text: David is deeply grateful. The statement passes without much notice — but your board chair and two other board members privately express concern about the precedent.
outcomes.contextNote: Other donors may expect similar favors in the future.

choices.text: Call David personally. Express support for his family, but explain you can’t issue organizational statements for personal situations. Offer to help in other ways.

outcomes.text: David is disappointed but respects your honesty. He doesn’t reduce his gift. Your board chair thanks you for holding the line. The situation blows over in a week.

choices.text: Don’t issue a public statement, but quietly connect David’s daughter with a mentor from your young leadership program. Turn crisis into engagement.

outcomes.text: David is touched by the personal gesture. His daughter has a meaningful conversation with the mentor. Three months later, she joins your campus committee — and David increases his gift.

choices.text: Issue a broader statement about the importance of intergenerational dialogue in the Jewish community — not mentioning the Abramowitz family but giving David something to point to.

outcomes.text: The statement is well-crafted and resonates broadly. David shares it on his social media. The community reads it as genuine leadership, not donor management.

inboxScenarios.subject: Confidential: Something wrong at the Federation
inboxScenarios.body: An anonymous email claims that the local Jewish federation has been inflating program participation numbers to secure foundation grants. The email includes what appear to be internal spreadsheets. If true, this is fraud. If false, acting on it could destroy a partner relationship. The email asks you to 'do the right thing.'

choices.text: Forward the materials to the federation's board chair with a request for an explanation. Give them the chance to address it internally first.

outcomes.text: The board chair thanks you for the heads-up and launches a quiet internal review. Two months later, the federation's ED resigns 'for personal reasons.' The numbers were inflated. Your relationship with the federation is awkward but intact.

The board chair is furious you're 'spreading anonymous accusations' and accuses you of trying to undermine the federation. The relationship freezes. Later, the allegations turn out to be true — but by then, you've been painted as the enemy.

choices.text: Report it to the relevant foundation anonymously. Let the funders investigate. Keep your name out of it.

outcomes.text: The foundation launches an audit. The federation scrambles. Your anonymity holds — for now. The investigation finds irregularities and the federation faces consequences. You did the right thing, but you'll always wonder if they'll find out it was you.

choices.text: Delete the email. This is not your problem. Anonymous tips are unreliable and getting involved in another organization's internal affairs is a minefield.

outcomes.text: Six months later, the fraud is exposed by a journalist. The fallout damages the entire Jewish nonprofit sector's reputation. Donors ask every organization hard questions about transparency. You wonder if you could have prevented the broader damage.

inboxScenarios.subject: Interview request: Jewish community & antisemitism
inboxScenarios.body: A popular political podcast with 2 million weekly listeners wants to interview you about rising antisemitism. The host is known for provocative takes — he's been accused of platforming both far-right and far-left extremists in the name of 'hearing all sides.' The exposure would be enormous. The risk of being taken out of context is real.

choices.text: Accept. 2 million listeners is an audience you can't buy. Prepare thoroughly and control your message. This is a chance to reach people who never hear from Jewish organizations.

outcomes.text: The interview goes well — you stay on message and the host is respectful. Several new donors reach out saying they heard you on the podcast. But a 30-second clip is later spliced out of context and shared by antisemitic accounts, making it look like you agreed with something you were actually refuting.
The host ambushes you with a question about Israel that you weren't prepared for. Your fumbled answer becomes a meme. Your media strategist wants to scream.

choices.text: Decline politely. The risk-reward ratio is wrong. Offer to connect them with a national spokesperson better equipped for that format.

outcomes.text: The producer thanks you and moves on. You miss the exposure but avoid the risk. Your media strategist says it was the right call. You'll never know what would have happened.

inboxScenarios.subject: Open letter — 47 signatures and counting
inboxScenarios.body: Forty-seven alumni of your flagship youth program have signed an open letter criticizing your organization's 'failure to evolve.' They cite the lack of diverse programming, outdated Israel curriculum, and 'a leadership structure that doesn't reflect the community it claims to serve.' They plan to publish it on Medium next week unless you agree to a meeting.

choices.text: Meet with the alumni immediately. Listen to their concerns. Commit to specific changes with a timeline. Co-create solutions rather than defend the status quo.

outcomes.text: The meeting is tense but productive. You agree to a diversity audit, an Israel programming review, and an alumni advisory board. The letter is never published. But implementing the changes takes resources from existing programs, and some long-time supporters feel you're capitulating to pressure.

choices.text: Respond publicly before they publish. Acknowledge the concerns, highlight the changes you've already made, and invite open dialogue. Don't let them control the narrative.

outcomes.text: Your pre-emptive response takes some wind out of their sails. The letter is still published but gets less attention. Community members give you credit for engaging rather than stonewalling. But the alumni feel you tried to undercut them and the relationship is more adversarial now.

choices.text: Let them publish. Don't negotiate under threat. If they want to have a conversation, they can request a meeting through normal channels — not through ultimatums.

outcomes.text: The letter goes live and gets 5,000 shares. The Jewish press picks it up. Your inbox fills with messages from both sides. The alumni are emboldened by the attention. You look rigid and defensive, even though you have a point about the tactics.

inboxScenarios.subject: Incident report: Jewish cemetery vandalism
inboxScenarios.body: Twelve headstones at the Beth Shalom cemetery were toppled and spray-painted with swastikas overnight. Police have no suspects. The cemetery caretaker discovered the damage at 6 AM and the photos are already on social media. Three families whose loved ones' graves were desecrated are calling your office. The local news is asking for your reaction.

choices.text: Organize an immediate community cleanup and restoration. Rally volunteers, set up a GoFundMe for headstone repair, and hold a ceremony of rededication at the cemetery.

outcomes.text: Eighty volunteers show up, including many non-Jewish neighbors. The cleanup is covered by three TV stations. The GoFundMe raises $25,000 in 48 hours. The families are moved. The rededication ceremony is one of the most meaningful community events of the year. Several volunteers become regular participants in your programming.

choices.text: Focus on demanding accountability. Hold a press conference calling on police to prioritize the investigation. Push for the incident to be classified as a hate crime. Name the systemic failure.

outcomes.text: The press conference generates pressure on the police department, which assigns additional detectives. The hate crime classification strengthens the potential charges. But the families feel the political response overshadowed the personal — 'We wanted comfort, not a crusade,' one says.

choices.text: Let the families lead the response. Offer your resources and support but let them decide what the community's reaction should look like. This is about their grief, not your organization's profile.

outcomes.text: The families organize a small, private ceremony. It's intimate and healing. Your restraint is quietly appreciated. But the broader community feels the incident was underresponded to, and several members ask why your organization didn't take a more visible role.

inboxScenarios.subject: Exciting opportunity for your team
inboxScenarios.body: Harold Greenberg, a longtime donor, is offering to fully fund a staff trip to Israel — flights, hotels, guided tours, meetings with Israeli officials and settlers in the West Bank. He calls it a 'leadership development experience.' The total value is $40,000. Two staff members are excited. Two others are deeply uncomfortable with the itinerary, particularly the settlement visits. Harold expects an answer by Friday.

choices.text: Accept the trip as offered. This is a generous gift and Israel trips are standard leadership development in Jewish organizations. Staff can form their own opinions about what they see.

outcomes.text: The trip goes forward. Staff who were excited return energized. Staff who were uncomfortable return conflicted — the settlement visit was exactly as politically charged as they feared. One writes a memo to you calling the itinerary 'propaganda.' Harold is thrilled and hints at increasing his annual gift.

choices.text: Accept but negotiate the itinerary. Include meetings with Palestinian civil society, Israeli peace organizations, and diverse viewpoints. Make it a genuine learning trip, not a political tour.

outcomes.text: Harold reluctantly agrees to the modified itinerary. The trip is genuinely eye-opening for all staff members. But Harold is irritated — 'I didn't donate $40,000 for my staff to meet with people who criticize Israel.' His annual gift stays flat instead of increasing.
Harold withdraws the offer entirely. 'If you can't accept a gift in the spirit it's given, maybe I should find an organization that can.' The trip doesn't happen and the relationship is strained.

choices.text: Decline the trip. Thank Harold for his generosity but explain that donor-directed staff programming creates complicated dynamics. Offer to work with him on a different leadership development initiative.

outcomes.text: Harold is offended but ultimately respects the boundary. Your staff is relieved — the uncomfortable ones especially. But you've turned down $40,000 in programming that your budget desperately needed. Harold's gift doesn't decrease, but it doesn't increase either.

inboxScenarios.subject: Urgent: tragic loss on campus
inboxScenarios.body: A Jewish student at the university died by suicide last night. The campus is in shock. The student's parents are members of your community. Hillel is overwhelmed. The family has asked that you help coordinate the community's response. Media is already asking whether bullying or antisemitism was a factor — there's no evidence of either, but the question is out there.

choices.text: Drop everything and focus on pastoral care. Organize shiva support, connect the family with grief counselors, and coordinate with Hillel on campus memorials. Keep the media at arm's length.

outcomes.text: Your pastoral response is deeply appreciated. The family says your support made the worst week of their lives bearable. The campus memorial draws 300 students. You resist media pressure to speculate about causes. Mental health awareness in the community increases. Staff are emotionally drained.

choices.text: Use this moment to launch a Jewish community mental health initiative. Partner with mental health organizations, provide resources, and advocate for better campus support systems.

outcomes.text: The mental health initiative launches to strong community support. A foundation provides seed funding. But the family is hurt that their child's death became 'a programmatic opportunity.' 'She was a person, not a case study,' the mother says quietly.
outcomes.contextNote: There's a line between responding to tragedy and instrumentalizing it.

choices.text: Coordinate with Hillel but stay in a supporting role. This is a campus tragedy and Hillel is the campus organization. Your role is to support the parents and let Hillel lead on campus.

outcomes.text: Hillel leads effectively with your support. The response is appropriate and proportional. The family appreciates your presence without it feeling institutional. But some community members feel you should have done more — 'This is our community's child. Where was our organization?'

inboxScenarios.subject: Confidential: merger conversation
inboxScenarios.body: The executive director of the Jewish Community Alliance — a neighboring organization that overlaps significantly with your mission — has reached out confidentially about a potential merger. Their board is open to it. The combined organization would have double the budget, broader reach, and reduced overhead. But you'd likely have to share or give up the ED role. Your board doesn't know yet.

choices.text: Explore the merger seriously. Bring it to your board as a strategic opportunity. The community is better served by one strong organization than two struggling ones. Your role can be negotiated.

outcomes.text: Your board is intrigued but divided. Merger talks proceed over three months. The financial case is strong. But organizational culture clashes emerge — their staff is more conservative, yours more progressive. The merger ultimately happens with you as co-ED for a transition period. It's messy but potentially transformative.

The merger talks collapse when their board insists on their ED leading the combined organization. Your board feels you brought them a deal that would have eliminated your own position. Trust in your judgment takes a hit.

choices.text: Decline politely. Your organization has its own identity and mission. Mergers sound great on paper but usually destroy the culture that makes each organization special.

outcomes.text: The Alliance merges with a different organization instead. Two years later, the combined entity is thriving and has programs you can't match. Some of your donors shift their giving to the larger organization. You kept your independence but the competitive landscape shifted against you.

choices.text: Propose a partnership instead of a merger. Shared programming, joint fundraising events, coordinated advocacy — but separate boards, separate staff, separate identities.

outcomes.text: The partnership works reasonably well. You co-host two successful events. But the Alliance ED is disappointed — they wanted structural change, not programming coordination. The partnership is pleasant but doesn't solve the underlying problem of two similar organizations competing for the same donors and members.

inboxScenarios.subject: RE: Factual correction request
inboxScenarios.body: The Metro Jewish News ran a story about your organization that contains significant errors: it misstates your budget (making it look larger than it is), misquotes your position on a recent controversy, and implies donor conflicts that don't exist. The editor says they'll run a correction if you can document the errors by tomorrow's deadline. Your board chair is furious and wants to 'go to war' with the paper.

choices.text: Send a detailed, professional correction request documenting every error with evidence. Be firm but respectful. The paper offered a correction — take it.

outcomes.text: The paper runs a correction, though it's smaller than the original article. Most readers who saw the original won't see the correction. Your board chair is unsatisfied but the record is corrected. The editor respects your professionalism and is more careful with future stories.

choices.text: Escalate. Demand a full retraction, not just a correction. Threaten to pull your organization's advertising and events listings if the paper doesn't make it right.

outcomes.text: The editor pushes back — 'We offered a correction, not a hostage negotiation.' The retraction is denied. Your advertising threat is noted but not feared — your ad spend isn't that significant. The relationship with the paper is now adversarial, which will affect coverage going forward.

choices.text: Let it go. Corrections never undo the damage. Instead, write an op-ed for the paper offering your actual perspective. Get your version of the story out there in your own words.

outcomes.text: The op-ed runs the following week and is well-received. Some readers who saw the original error now see your perspective. The editor appreciates that you took the high road. But the uncorrected errors remain in the digital archive, and someone will Google them someday.

inboxScenarios.subject: URGENT: Threatening email received
inboxScenarios.body: A staff member received a threatening email at their work address: 'We know where you work. We know what you people are up to. This isn't over.' The email was sent from an anonymous account. Your security coordinator has reported it to police. The staff member is terrified and wants to work from home. Other staff members are shaken. Police say it's likely not a credible threat but can't be certain.

choices.text: Implement full security protocols. Allow the staff member to work from home. Hire a temporary security guard. Brief all staff on safety procedures. Take it seriously even if police say it's not credible.

outcomes.text: Staff feel protected and cared for. The security guard's presence is reassuring. The threat is never followed up on — it was likely a one-off from an angry internet user. But the cost is real and the heightened security creates an anxious atmosphere that takes weeks to dissipate.

choices.text: Follow police guidance. They say it's not credible — trust their assessment. Offer the staff member flexibility but don't implement organization-wide security changes. Overreacting could create more fear than the threat itself.

outcomes.text: Most staff accept the assessment, though some are nervous. The threatened staff member feels you didn't take it seriously enough and begins job searching. When word gets out that you didn't increase security, a board member questions your duty of care.
Nothing happens. The threat was empty. Staff gradually relax. You saved budget and avoided a panic. The threatened staff member comes back to the office after a few days at home.

choices.text: Go public. Issue a statement about the threat (without details that would compromise the investigation). Use it to raise awareness about threats against Jewish organizations and push for better hate crime enforcement.

outcomes.text: The public statement generates sympathy and support. Donations increase temporarily. But the staff member who was threatened feels exposed — 'You used my fear for a press release.' Other staff are divided on whether going public made them safer or more of a target.

inboxScenarios.subject: Annual grant review — additional documentation needed
inboxScenarios.body: The Goldstein Foundation, which provides $75,000 annually for your community engagement programs, is requesting a detailed audit of how their grant money was spent. They want itemized expenses, program attendance records, and outcome measurements. Your records are adequate but not pristine — some expenses were categorized loosely and attendance numbers were estimated rather than tracked precisely. The foundation's tone is routine but your development director is nervous.

choices.text: Provide full transparency. Pull together the best documentation you have, acknowledge where record-keeping was imprecise, and commit to better tracking going forward. Honesty is the best policy.

outcomes.text: The foundation appreciates your honesty. They flag a few areas for improvement but renew the grant with a requirement for quarterly reporting. Your development director spends the next month implementing better tracking systems. The relationship is actually stronger for the transparency.

choices.text: Clean up the records before submitting. Recategorize expenses to look more organized, round attendance numbers to look more precise. Everything is technically accurate — just... polished.

outcomes.text: The polished report passes review without issue. The grant is renewed. But your development director feels uncomfortable — 'We didn't lie, but we didn't tell the whole truth either.' If the foundation ever compares your submitted numbers with other data sources, the inconsistencies could surface.

The foundation's new program officer is a detail person. She notices that your attendance numbers don't match the photos from your social media posts. She requests a call. Your development director's nervous feeling was justified.

choices.text: Push back on the level of scrutiny. You've been a reliable grantee for years. This feels like micromanagement. Provide a standard report and decline to produce itemized expenses.

outcomes.text: The foundation is taken aback by your resistance. The grant is renewed but at a reduced level — $50,000 instead of $75,000 — with a note that 'enhanced accountability' is expected. Your development director says the pushback cost you $25,000.

thresholds.heading: Exceptional Leadership
thresholds.verdict: You navigated a difficult year with integrity and skill. No decision was perfect, and you made some enemies — that is the cost of having convictions. But the organization is stronger, the community trusts you, and your staff knows you have their backs.

thresholds.heading: A Solid Performance
thresholds.verdict: You kept the organization on course through real challenges. The board renewed your contract with a vote of confidence and approved an expanded operating budget. A few decisions you'd take back, but the fundamentals are sound.

thresholds.heading: A Quiet Year
thresholds.verdict: The board renewed your contract. No one celebrated, but no one objected either. You survived — some decisions you're proud of, others you'd take back. You have another year to prove yourself.

thresholds.heading: On Probation
thresholds.verdict: The board renewed your contract — barely. You've been placed on a performance improvement plan. Discretionary investments have been frozen until the board sees sustained improvement. The next review will not be this generous.

thresholds.heading: Your Tenure Has Ended
thresholds.verdict: The board has asked for your resignation. Trust collapsed, donors walked, and staff morale hit rock bottom. The work is impossibly hard, the stakeholders demanding, the stakes real. The community will find someone else to carry this forward.

breakingNews.title: Shooting at Synagogue in Neighboring City
breakingNews.body: A gunman opened fire during Shabbat services at Congregation Beth Israel, 40 miles from your office. Three people are injured. The community is in shock. Your phone is ringing nonstop.

choices.text: Drop everything. Drive to the hospital. Organize a community vigil for tonight.

outcomes.text: Your presence at the hospital is noted by every rabbi in the region. The vigil draws 800 people. National media covers it.

choices.text: Issue an immediate statement and coordinate with security agencies to assess local threats.

outcomes.text: Your statement is among the first from any Jewish org. The security coordination uncovers a copycat threat that is quietly neutralized.
outcomes.contextNote: Your quick coordination may have prevented a second attack. Law enforcement credits your organization privately.

choices.text: Focus inward — secure your own buildings, notify your community, cancel events for the week.

outcomes.text: Your community feels safe. But other organizations notice you didn't show up for the broader Jewish community in crisis.
outcomes.contextNote: In moments of communal trauma, organizations that turn inward are remembered — and not fondly.

breakingNews.title: Viral Video: Antisemitic Attack on Jewish Student
breakingNews.body: A video of a Jewish college student being physically attacked while wearing a Star of David necklace is going viral. It happened on a campus in your city. The video has 2 million views and climbing. Cable news is covering it live.

choices.text: Go on cable news immediately to condemn the attack and demand campus accountability.

outcomes.text: Your interview is powerful and goes viral itself. You become the face of the Jewish community's response. Some love it; others think you're grandstanding.

choices.text: Connect the student with legal resources and organize a campus support network before going public.

outcomes.text: The student's family thanks you personally. The campus network becomes a model. Less visibility, more impact.

choices.text: Use the moment to launch a fundraising campaign for campus antisemitism programs.

outcomes.text: The campaign raises $45K in 48 hours. Some accuse you of exploiting a crisis for fundraising. The money funds real programs.
outcomes.contextNote: Crisis fundraising is effective but ethically fraught. The Jewish communal world has long memory.

breakingNews.title: White House Omits Jews from Holocaust Statement
breakingNews.body: The White House just released a Holocaust Remembrance Day statement that doesn't mention Jews or antisemitism. Jewish organizations across the spectrum are reacting. Your board, staff, and community are all watching to see what you do.

choices.text: Issue a blistering public condemnation calling the omission 'dangerous erasure.'

outcomes.text: Your statement is quoted by three major outlets. Allies across the political spectrum share it. One government contact goes cold.

choices.text: Reach out privately to White House contacts to push for a corrected statement before going public.

outcomes.text: The White House issues a clarification 6 hours later mentioning Jewish victims specifically. You get no public credit but insiders know.
outcomes.contextNote: Sometimes the most effective advocacy happens where no one can see it.

choices.text: Use the moment for education — publish a thread about Holocaust history and why the omission matters.

outcomes.text: Your educational thread goes viral with 50K shares. Teachers across the country use it in classrooms. A quieter but deeper impact.

breakingNews.title: Antisemitic Conspiracy Theory Goes Mainstream
breakingNews.body: A prominent cable news host just aired a 15-minute segment promoting the conspiracy theory that Jewish organizations secretly control American foreign policy. The segment used graphics featuring Star of David symbols over the Capitol building. The clip has 20 million views and counting. Jewish community members are calling your office in tears.

advisorQuotes.text: This is bigger than us. But we have a choice: add to the pile of condemnation statements, or try to do something that actually changes the conversation.

choices.text: Demand the network fire the host. Launch an advertiser boycott campaign. Go nuclear.

outcomes.text: The boycott campaign picks up steam. Three major advertisers pull their ads. The host doubles down on-air, turning you into a target. But the Overton window shifts — advertisers now think twice.
outcomes.contextNote: Your organization is now on a cable news host’s enemy list. This comes with risks.

choices.text: Focus on community care: organize emergency gatherings, provide talking points for parents, and set up a hotline for people experiencing antisemitic harassment.

outcomes.text: The hotline receives 300 calls in the first week. Your community gatherings are packed. People feel less alone. The national story moves on, but your community is stronger.

choices.text: Produce a rapid-response educational campaign targeting non-Jewish audiences: ‘What Conspiracy Theories About Jews Actually Sound Like’ — shareable, accessible, factual.

outcomes.text: The campaign reaches 5 million people in 72 hours. Schools and corporations request the materials. You’ve turned a moment of hate into a lasting educational resource.
outcomes.contextNote: Three major foundations contact you about scaling this work.

breakingNews.title: Jewish Cemetery Desecrated
breakingNews.body: Overnight, vandals toppled and spray-painted over 200 headstones at the historic Jewish cemetery in your city — the oldest Jewish burial ground in the region, dating to 1847. Swastikas, SS symbols, and the words ‘Jews will not replace us’ were painted on the memorial wall. Families are devastated. The cemetery trust doesn’t have the resources to restore the damage.

advisorQuotes.text: Kavod ha-met — honor of the dead. This is one of the highest commandments. The community will come together for this. Lead them.

choices.text: Organize a massive community restoration day. Mobilize volunteers, bring in stone restoration experts, and turn the cleanup into a public act of defiance.

outcomes.text: 400 volunteers show up — Jews and non-Jews. A 94-year-old survivor helps clean her parents’ headstone. The images go viral. GoFundMe raises $180,000. The cemetery is restored and better protected than before.
outcomes.contextNote: This becomes the defining moment of your tenure.

choices.text: Launch a targeted fundraising campaign to restore the cemetery and establish a permanent security fund.

outcomes.text: The campaign raises $250,000 — more than enough for restoration plus ongoing security. The work is done professionally. Some wish the community had been more involved in the physical restoration.

choices.text: Focus on law enforcement: push for FBI involvement, demand hate crime charges, and use your political relationships to ensure this is investigated at the highest level.

outcomes.text: The FBI takes the case. Within two weeks, three suspects are arrested. The hate crime prosecution becomes a national story. But the cemetery still needs restoration funding.
outcomes.contextNote: Justice is served, but the physical damage remains until the community raises restoration funds separately.

breakingNews.title: Antisemitic Threat Against Jewish Day School
breakingNews.body: A Jewish day school in your area has received a specific, credible threat referencing 'finishing what Hitler started.' The school is in lockdown. Parents are rushing to pick up children. Police are on scene. The FBI is involved. Parents from your community — not just the school — are calling your office in panic asking if their synagogues and community centers are safe.

choices.text: Activate your security network immediately. Contact all Jewish institutions in the area to implement heightened security protocols. Issue a community-wide alert with safety guidance. Coordinate with law enforcement.

outcomes.text: Your rapid coordination is credited with keeping the community calm and safe. Every Jewish institution in the area implements protocols within 90 minutes. The threat is later traced to a teenager in another state — real enough to be terrifying, not imminent enough to be a physical danger. Parents are shaken but grateful for your leadership. The budget hit from emergency security is real.
outcomes.contextNote: The community remembers who stepped up in the crisis.

choices.text: Rush to the school immediately. Be physically present for the families. Let law enforcement handle security coordination — your job right now is pastoral presence and emotional support.

outcomes.text: Your presence at the school is deeply meaningful. Parents see you there and feel less alone. The local news films you comforting a crying parent — the image becomes the story. But while you were at the school, calls to your office went unanswered and other Jewish institutions felt left without guidance.
outcomes.contextNote: Sometimes showing up is the most important thing a leader can do.

choices.text: Issue a strong public statement condemning the threat and demanding federal action on domestic terrorism targeting religious institutions. Use this moment to push for policy change.

outcomes.text: Your statement gets national attention and a senator's office calls about sponsoring legislation. But families at the school feel you turned their terror into a political moment. 'We needed you here, not on CNN,' one parent says. The policy push is important but the timing feels wrong to those most affected.
outcomes.contextNote: There is a time for policy and a time for presence. Knowing which is which is leadership.

breakingNews.title: Jewish Organization Financial Scandal
breakingNews.body: A major national Jewish organization — one you've partnered with on multiple initiatives — has been exposed for diverting donor funds to personal accounts. The executive director has been arrested. Media are asking every Jewish organization for comment. Your donors are calling asking if their money is safe. Three reporters have left messages in the last hour.

choices.text: Get ahead of it. Issue a statement condemning the fraud, distance your organization, and proactively open your books — publish your financials and invite donor questions. Transparency is the only play.

outcomes.text: Your transparency is well-received. Two donors increase their gifts, citing your openness. The media uses your statement as a contrast to the scandal. But the broader damage to Jewish institutional trust is real — overall community giving drops, and you feel it too despite your clean record.
outcomes.contextNote: When one Jewish org fails, every Jewish org pays a price.

choices.text: Say nothing about the other organization. Focus exclusively on reassuring your own donors that your finances are sound. This is their problem, not yours.

outcomes.text: Your silence is interpreted by some as solidarity with the disgraced organization. 'Why won't they condemn it?' becomes a whispered question. Your donors are reassured but the community senses you're circling wagons rather than leading. A reporter writes that 'Jewish organizations closed ranks' after the scandal.

choices.text: Use this as a moment to advocate for sector-wide financial reform. Propose an independent oversight body for Jewish nonprofits. Turn the crisis into structural change.

outcomes.text: Your proposal gets traction — several organizations sign onto the oversight concept. But some see it as grandstanding on someone else's downfall. The disgraced org's board members, several of whom overlap with your donor network, are privately furious that you're using their crisis to promote yourself.

breakingNews.title: Unwanted Endorsement
breakingNews.body: A controversial far-right political figure has publicly praised your organization by name, calling you 'one of the good Jewish groups that understands the threat of radical Islam.' The endorsement is unsolicited and unwanted. Progressive allies are demanding you disavow it. Conservative members say the politician has a point about security threats. Your social media is blowing up. The politician has 5 million followers and his endorsement post has been shared 40,000 times.

choices.text: Immediately and forcefully disavow the endorsement. Issue a statement rejecting the politician's worldview, condemning Islamophobia, and clarifying that your organization's security concerns have nothing to do with anti-Muslim sentiment.

outcomes.text: Your disavowal is praised by progressive allies, interfaith partners, and the Islamic Center. But the politician's followers flood your social media with harassment. Several conservative donors feel you overreacted — 'You don't have to agree with everything he says to appreciate his support for Israel and Jewish security.' The story dominates a full news cycle.
outcomes.contextNote: The harassment continues for weeks and requires moderating all social media.
