choices.text: Issue a brief, measured statement: 'Our organization does not seek or accept endorsements from political figures. Our work stands on its own merits.' Don't name the politician. Don't engage further.

outcomes.text: The measured response satisfies most reasonable people. But activists on both sides call it insufficient — progressives wanted a forceful denunciation, the politician's fans wanted gratitude. The story fades faster than it would have with a more dramatic response. Your staff appreciates not being drawn into a culture war.
outcomes.contextNote: Sometimes the most powerful move is refusing to play.

choices.text: Don't respond at all. Any statement — even a disavowal — amplifies the connection. Ignore it completely and let the news cycle move on.

outcomes.text: The story does move on after 48 hours. But screenshots of the endorsement circulate for months. Progressive coalition partners quietly drop you from a joint letter. 'We weren't sure where you stood,' one explains. The association lingers even though you did nothing to invite it.
outcomes.contextNote: In the internet age, silence is its own statement.

breakingNews.title: White Nationalist Rally Near Synagogue
breakingNews.body: A white nationalist group has obtained a permit to rally in the park across the street from your community's largest synagogue this Saturday — during Shabbat services. They're billing it as a 'free speech demonstration' but their social media is filled with antisemitic imagery. The police say they can't revoke the permit. Counter-protesters are organizing. The synagogue rabbi is asking for your guidance. Services are in 48 hours.

choices.text: Organize a massive counter-presence. Fill the synagogue to overflowing. Pack the streets with community members, interfaith allies, and supporters. Show them we won't be intimidated. Make Shabbat louder than hate.

outcomes.text: 1,200 people show up for Shabbat — a record. The counter-presence overwhelms the rally of 30 white nationalists. The images of a packed, joyful synagogue against a handful of hateful marchers go viral in the best possible way. But the security costs are enormous and one tense moment between a counter-protester and a rally attendee almost turns violent.
outcomes.contextNote: The image of that Shabbat will be used in fundraising materials for years.

outcomes.text: The counter-protest draws 300 people but the atmosphere is tense. A shoving match breaks out. Police intervene with pepper spray. The evening news shows chaos, not community strength. The synagogue rabbi is devastated.

choices.text: Ignore the rally entirely. Hold Shabbat services as normal. Don't engage, don't counter-protest, don't give them the audience they want. Jewish joy is the best response to hate.

outcomes.text: Services proceed with heavy security but normal programming. The rally draws 30 people and a few cameras. Without counter-protesters, the media story is small. Community members who attended services say it felt powerful to just... be Jewish. But some feel you should have done more. 'We let Nazis march past our synagogue and did nothing,' one member writes.
outcomes.contextNote: There is dignity in refusing to be provoked. There is also a cost.

choices.text: Cancel Shabbat services at that location. Move to an alternate venue. Prioritize safety above all else. Work with police to ensure the community is protected.

outcomes.text: The relocated services feel wrong — displaced, diminished. 'They won,' several congregants say. The white nationalists celebrate the cancellation as a victory. The rabbi gives a sermon about exile that moves everyone to tears but doesn't erase the feeling of surrender.
outcomes.contextNote: Retreating from your own synagogue sends a message no amount of programming can undo.

breakingNews.title: Jewish Americans Held Hostage
breakingNews.body: Two Jewish American aid workers have been taken hostage by an armed group in West Africa. The State Department confirms they are alive. Their families, who live in your community, are at your office right now. The media is camped outside. A congressman has called asking what you need. The families want you to lead the public advocacy campaign for their release. This is beyond anything your organization has dealt with before.

choices.text: Take the lead. Coordinate with the families, the State Department, and the congressman. Launch a public awareness campaign. Make this your organization's top priority until the hostages are free.

outcomes.text: Your coordination is effective. The congressman secures a high-level meeting at the State Department. The awareness campaign generates international media coverage. After three agonizing weeks, the hostages are released through diplomatic channels. Your role is publicly acknowledged. The community is profoundly grateful — this is what Jewish organizations are for.
outcomes.contextNote: This is the moment your organization will be remembered for.

outcomes.text: Despite your best efforts, the crisis drags on for months. The hostages are eventually released but your organization was not the decisive factor. You've spent enormous resources and emotional capital on something you ultimately couldn't control. Your regular programming suffered and staff are burned out.

choices.text: Support the families directly but defer the public campaign to national organizations with more resources and experience in hostage situations. You're a local org — this requires national infrastructure.

outcomes.text: The national organizations take the lead and handle it competently. The families appreciate your personal support. But when the hostages are released, your organization is a footnote. Some community members feel you passed on a leadership moment — 'These are our people. We should have led.'
outcomes.contextNote: Knowing your limits is wisdom. Missing your moment is regret.

choices.text: Focus entirely on the families' immediate needs. Set up a support fund, provide meals, arrange childcare, create a prayer circle. Leave the politics and publicity to others.

outcomes.text: The families say your support kept them sane during the worst weeks of their lives. The community rallies around them — the support fund raises $80,000 from small donations. But the public campaign led by others gets the credit when the hostages are released. Your contribution is invisible to everyone except the people who mattered most.
outcomes.contextNote: The families will never forget what you did. The community might.

coalitionOffers.name: Interfaith Alliance Partnership
coalitionOffers.description: The local Interfaith Council wants a formal partnership. You'll co-host monthly events and they'll amplify your work to their 15,000-member network. But they expect you to remain neutral on Israel-Palestine issues in all public communications.

constraints.description: Must stay neutral on Israel-Palestine in public statements

violationPenalty.text: The Interfaith Alliance publicly ends the partnership, calling your Israel statement a 'betrayal of the neutral space we built together.'

declinePenalty.text: Word spreads that you turned down the Interfaith Alliance. Some question your commitment to bridge-building.

coalitionOffers.name: Campus Coalition Against Hate
coalitionOffers.description: A coalition of campus organizations invites you to join their anti-hate alliance. You'll get direct access to college students and shared programming resources. But they require members to publicly support all coalition statements — even ones you might disagree with.

violationPenalty.text: The Campus Coalition votes to remove your organization, citing 'loss of community trust that undermines our shared mission.'

declinePenalty.text: Your staff is disappointed you passed on the campus partnership.

coalitionOffers.name: Major Donor Collective
coalitionOffers.description: A group of five major donors wants to form an advisory circle with guaranteed annual giving of $200K combined. In exchange, they want quarterly briefings and veto power over any public statement on Israel.

constraints.description: No public statements critical of Israel or progressive social policy without donor circle approval

violationPenalty.text: The Donor Collective withdraws all funding effective immediately. 'We had an agreement,' their letter reads. 'You broke it.'

declinePenalty.text: The donor collective redirects their giving to a rival organization. You feel the gelt impact immediately.

coalitionOffers.name: Federation Partnership Grant
coalitionOffers.description: The Jewish Federation offers a 3-year partnership grant: $75K/year in unrestricted funding plus shared staff resources. But Federation partners must participate in community-wide campaigns and avoid public disagreements with Federation positions.

constraints.description: Must maintain trust above 50 to keep Federation partnership

violationPenalty.text: The Federation pauses the partnership grant, citing 'concerns about organizational stability.'

declinePenalty.text: The Federation notes your absence from the partnership program. You’re not invited to the next community planning meeting.

coalitionOffers.name: Jewish Media Consortium
coalitionOffers.description: Three Jewish media outlets offer you a regular column and guaranteed coverage of your events. In exchange, you give them exclusive access and first comment rights on any controversy. You can't talk to outside media first.

constraints.description: Must maintain staff morale above 35 (bad internal culture = bad stories)

violationPenalty.text: The consortium runs a piece about low staff morale at your organization. So much for the friendly media relationship.

declinePenalty.text: The consortium runs a mild piece about organizations that “prefer to stay in the shadows.” It’s not hostile, but it stings.

investments.name: Security Upgrade
investments.description: Install new cameras, hire a part-time guard, improve access control.
investments.outcomeText: Your facility is noticeably more secure. Staff and visitors feel safer.

investments.name: Marketing Campaign
investments.description: Run a targeted advertising campaign to raise your profile and attract supporters.
investments.outcomeText: Your campaign reaches thousands of new potential supporters. Several reach out.

investments.name: Staff Development
investments.description: Send your team to professional development training and leadership workshops.
investments.outcomeText: Your staff returns energized with new skills and renewed commitment.

investments.name: Technology Upgrade
investments.description: Modernize your CRM, website, and internal systems.
investments.outcomeText: Operations run smoother. Your digital presence is significantly improved.

investments.name: Signature Event
investments.description: Host a high-profile community event that showcases your mission.
investments.outcomeText: The event draws strong attendance and media coverage. Your organization is the talk of the community.

investments.name: Endowment Contribution
investments.description: Contribute to the organization's endowment. No immediate stat benefit, but generates +5 gelt income every round for the rest of the year. Invest early for maximum return.
investments.outcomeText: Your endowment contribution is approved by the board. The returns will boost your operating budget every round for the rest of the year.

investments.name: Hire Lobbyist
investments.description: Retain a government affairs consultant to build political relationships and open doors.
investments.outcomeText: Your new lobbyist is already making introductions. Political capital is growing.

advisorPool.name: Rachel Steinberg
advisorPool.bio: 20 years in Jewish communal governance. Thinks in terms of risk, reputation, and fiduciary duty.

advisorPool.name: Avi Kessler
advisorPool.bio: Former campus Hillel director. Believes the mission comes first, always. Staff love him.

advisorPool.name: Miriam Gold
advisorPool.bio: Raised $40M across three organizations. Knows every major donor by name and preference.

advisorPool.name: Rabbi David Friedman
advisorPool.bio: Orthodox-trained, pluralistic in practice. Deep Talmudic knowledge and a reputation for moral clarity that crosses denominational lines.

advisorPool.name: Sarah Feld
advisorPool.bio: Former congressional chief of staff. Knows every elected official in the state and half the ones in Washington. Thinks three moves ahead.

advisorPool.name: Tali Navarro
advisorPool.bio: Built the largest Jewish young adult network in the region from scratch. Speaks for a generation that doesn’t show up to galas but will march in the rain.

advisorPool.name: Leonard Katz
advisorPool.bio: Manages giving strategy for three family foundations worth $400M combined. Pragmatic, well-connected, and always thinking about ROI.

advisorPool.name: Noa Berman
advisorPool.bio: Former editor at the Forward, now a crisis communications consultant. Controls narratives for a living. Never says anything by accident.

character.name: Harold Weinstein
character.context: Called to discuss your decision on his restricted gift

n0.characterLine: I'll be frank with you. I offered this organization $300,000 and I feel like it was thrown back in my face. I've been giving for fifteen years. I think I've earned the right to have some input on programming.

responses.text: Harold, I appreciate you calling. Your fifteen years mean a great deal, and I want to understand what this program means to you.
We didn't throw anything back. We made a strategic decision about mission alignment — which is exactly what a well-run organization does.
Before we discuss the gift, can I share what we accomplished this year with your previous support?

n1_warm.characterLine: It means a lot, actually. My father was a Holocaust survivor. This program would have honored his memory. I know that's personal, but that's where it comes from.

responses.text: That context changes things. Could we find a way to honor your father's memory that also strengthens our core mission? I have some ideas.
I hear you, and I respect that deeply. But a restricted gift for a program outside our mission isn't the right vehicle for that legacy.

n1_cool.characterLine: A 'strategic decision.' You know what I hear? I hear that my money is welcome but my judgment isn't. Maybe the Civic Alliance would be more appreciative.

responses.text: Harold, I don't want you to leave. Your judgment matters — and I should have had this conversation with you before making the decision, not after. That's on me.
I understand you're frustrated, and I respect your right to give wherever you feel valued. I'd rather keep you, but not by compromising what makes this organization worth funding.

n1_neutral.characterLine: I know what you accomplished. I helped fund it. That's exactly my point — I've been a partner, not just a checkbook. This gift was about deepening that partnership.

responses.text: You're right — you are a partner. And partners negotiate. What if we took the core idea of your program and shaped it together into something that advances our mission?
I value the partnership. But restricted gifts for off-mission programs put us in a bind that ultimately hurts the work you're funding in the first place.

n2_positive.characterLine: I'd be open to that. I don't need it to be exactly what I proposed. I need it to feel like my family's story is part of what this organization stands for. Can you do that?

responses.text: Yes. Let me draft a concept this week that honors your father's legacy through our existing programs. You'll have it by Friday.
I want to say yes, but I need to be honest — I can only commit if it genuinely fits our strategic plan. Let me take a week to think it through properly.

n2_middle.characterLine: Look, I'm not going to pretend I'm not disappointed. But I've known you long enough to know you don't make decisions carelessly. What would you want from me going forward?

responses.text: I want you at the table — not as a donor, as an advisor. Join our strategic planning committee. Help us shape what's next. Your judgment matters beyond the dollars.
Keep doing what you've been doing. Your unrestricted support has been the backbone of everything we've built. That matters more than any single program.

n2_tense.characterLine: You know, I respect that you have principles. I just wish your principles left more room for the people who make your budget possible. I need to think about next year's gift.

responses.text: I understand. Take the time you need. The door is always open, and your voice always matters here — whether or not there's a check attached.
I hope you'll stay. But I'd rather lose a gift than lose what this organization stands for. You taught me that, actually — fifteen years ago.

outcomes.heading: A Partnership Renewed
outcomes.text: Harold leaves the call energized. He’ll not only maintain his giving — he’ll deepen it. Expect a call next week about increasing his annual commitment.

outcomes.heading: An Understanding Reached
outcomes.text: Harold accepts your direction. He’s not thrilled, but he respects your leadership. The relationship will survive — and he’ll stay through the year.

outcomes.heading: A Strained Relationship
outcomes.text: Harold hangs up politely but coldly. Don’t expect a call back anytime soon. His annual gift is in serious jeopardy.

outcomes.heading: A Donor Lost
outcomes.text: Harold is done. He’ll honor his current pledge but has already called the Civic Alliance. Other donors in his circle will hear about this.

n0.characterLine: Before you decide anything — I need you to hear something. Three of my people are in crisis. Real crisis. One is seeing a therapist for the first time because of this job. I don't say that lightly.

responses.text: Avi, I hear you. Tell me specifically what your team needs to get through the High Holidays.
I understand it's hard, but the High Holidays are the most important time of year. We all need to push through.

n1_open.characterLine: They need real raises — not bonuses, raises. And they need us to either hire help or cut scope. I can't keep asking people to do the work of two positions for the salary of half a position.

responses.text: I'll go to the board with a compensation proposal this week. You have my word.
I want to do raises but the board controls compensation. What I can do right now is hire temporary help and scale back the second event.

n1_resist.characterLine: Push through? That's what we said last year. And the year before. At what point does 'push through' become 'we don't actually care about our people'?

responses.text: You're right. I'm sorry. I defaulted to the easy answer. What would actually help?
I care deeply. But we have obligations to the community too. Let's find a middle path after the holidays.

n2_end.characterLine: OK. I trust you to do right by us. Just... please don't let this be another conversation that leads to a committee that leads to nothing.

responses.text: It won't be. And Avi — thank you for being the person who says the uncomfortable thing. That's why you're invaluable.
I'll have a concrete proposal within two weeks. You'll see it before the board does.

outcomes.text: Avi leaves feeling genuinely heard. He'll go to bat for whatever you decide — and his team will feel it.
Avi accepts your direction but is watching closely. His support for your decision is conditional.
Avi is visibly frustrated. He'll comply but won't advocate for your approach with his team.
Avi leaves the meeting angry. Two staff members will hear about this conversation within the hour.

n0.characterLine: I need to tell you something before you make this decision. I’ve spent thirty years teaching the Holocaust. I’ve seen what happens when communities treat it as someone else’s problem. The curriculum bill isn’t just politics — it’s an assault on memory itself.

responses.text: Rabbi, I take this as seriously as you do. Help me understand what approach would make the biggest difference.
I agree it’s important, but we have limited resources. I need to weigh this against everything else on our plate.

n1_engaged.characterLine: The survivors who are still alive — we have maybe five, ten years left with them. Their testimony is the most powerful weapon against this bill. If we don’t use it now, we never can. I can arrange for three survivors to testify. But I need your organization’s full support behind them.

responses.text: You’ll have it. Let’s build the entire campaign around survivor testimony. Nothing is more powerful than their voices.
I want to include survivor testimony, but we also need legal arguments, educator allies, and political strategy. Testimony alone won’t kill a bill.

n1_cautious.characterLine: Limited resources. Right. You know what’s also limited? The number of people alive who can say ‘I was there.’ Every year there are fewer. If this bill passes because we were busy with something else, what will we tell their grandchildren?

responses.text: You’re right. I let pragmatism get ahead of principle. Let’s make this our top priority.
I hear the urgency. I can commit us to a supporting role, but I can’t make this the only thing we do this quarter.

n2_end.characterLine: I’ll hold you to it. I’ve watched too many Jewish leaders say ‘never again’ and then find reasons to wait. Don’t be one of them.

responses.text: I won’t be. And Rabbi — thank you for pushing. That’s what I need from you.
I’ll have a concrete plan on your desk by Monday. Judge me by the plan, not the promises.

outcomes.text: Rabbi Friedman leaves energized. He’ll personally rally every synagogue in the region. His support will amplify whatever you decide to do.
Rabbi Friedman is cautiously supportive. He’ll help, but he’s watching to see if your actions match your words.
Rabbi Friedman is disappointed. He’ll participate minimally but won’t put his personal reputation behind your approach.
Rabbi Friedman leaves the conversation visibly upset. He tells two other board members he’s ‘lost confidence in the executive director’s priorities.’

n0.characterLine: OK, I’ve been on the phone with three legislative staffers. Here’s the reality: this bill has the votes to pass. The question isn’t whether it passes — it’s whether we’re attached to it, against it, or invisible. Each of those has a different cost.

responses.text: Walk me through the political calculus. What does each position cost us and what does it buy?
I’m less interested in the politics and more interested in what’s right. What should we do on principle?

n1_strategic.characterLine: Supporting it buys us goodwill with the statehouse majority and our pro-Israel donors. Opposing it earns us credibility with civil liberties groups and younger Jews. The amendment path — broadening the bill — is the smartest move, but it requires spending real political capital. Do you have it?

responses.text: I think we do. Let’s use our relationships to push the amendment. That’s where we add the most value.
I’m not sure we have enough capital for the amendment. What’s our fallback position?

n1_values.characterLine: On principle? I think BDS is discriminatory, but I also think this bill is constitutionally suspect. The honest position is opposing BDS while defending free speech. That’s nuanced. Nuance doesn’t trend on Twitter.

responses.text: Then let’s be the organization brave enough to hold both truths. Draft me a framework for that position.
Maybe we should just stay out of it. Not every fight is our fight.

n2_end.characterLine: Whatever you decide, decide fast. The committee vote is Thursday. After that, the bill goes to the floor and our window closes.

responses.text: I’ll have our position finalized by tomorrow morning. I want you in the room when I call the committee chair.
Let me sleep on it. I’ll have a decision by Wednesday.

outcomes.text: Sarah is impressed by your political instincts. She’ll work her contacts aggressively on your behalf and keep you ahead of every development.
Sarah will execute your strategy competently but isn’t going to burn her own political capital for you.
Sarah thinks you’re not politically serious. She’ll do the minimum and start prioritizing other clients.
Sarah is frustrated. She tells a colleague you ‘don’t understand how power works.’ Word gets around.

n0.characterLine: I need to be honest with you. Whatever you just decided about the DEI module — I’m getting calls from coalition partners who are upset. They feel like we didn’t consult them. And honestly? They have a point. I’m the one who has to face these people at the next organizing meeting.

responses.text: Tali, you’re right. I should have looped you in earlier and given you a chance to prepare your partners. That’s on me.
I hear you, but we had to make a decision on our own timeline. We can’t run every decision through coalition partners first.

n1_open.characterLine: Thank you for saying that. Here’s what I need: let me go back to our partners with something concrete. A joint project, a shared event, something that shows we’re still committed to the coalition even if we made a unilateral move on DEI.

responses.text: Absolutely. What if we co-host a series on intersectionality that centers multiple communities’ experiences, including ours? Give me a proposal by next week.
I’m open to ideas, but I don’t want to make promises I can’t keep. Let me think about what we can realistically offer.

n1_defensive.characterLine: I’m not asking you to run decisions through them. I’m asking you to let me manage the relationship so it doesn’t blow up. That’s literally my job. If I can’t do that, what am I doing here?

responses.text: You’re right — I was being defensive. Your job is to manage these relationships and I made that harder. How do we fix it?
I value your role, Tali. But there are going to be times when our mission comes first and relationships take the hit. That’s the reality of leadership.

n2_end.characterLine: OK. I’ll make some calls tonight. But I want you to understand — these relationships take years to build and seconds to destroy. I need you to trust my judgment on coalition dynamics.

responses.text: I trust your judgment. And I want you to know that your work holding these coalitions together is one of the most important things we do.
I hear you. Let’s debrief in a week and see where things stand.

outcomes.text: Tali leaves feeling valued and empowered. She repairs the coalition relationships and comes back with three new partnership ideas.
Tali does her best to manage the fallout. The coalition survives but the relationships are strained.
Tali is demoralized. She goes through the motions but her heart isn’t in it. Coalition partners can tell.
Tali starts looking for a new job. She tells a friend at another organization that ‘leadership doesn’t understand coalition work.’ The friend tells someone else.

character.name: David Chen
character.context: Called after the rival organization published their attack

n0.characterLine: I’m not going to sugarcoat this. That open letter has the board spooked. Three members called me today asking if we need to ‘reassess leadership.’ I defended you, but I need to know — do you have a plan, or are we just absorbing punches?

responses.text: I have a plan, and I’m glad you called. I want to walk you through it and get your input before I take it to the full board.
David, this letter is noise. Our metrics are strong, our programs are working, and our community trusts us. I’m not going to let a jealous competitor set our agenda.
Honestly? I’m still figuring it out. This caught me off guard and I want to be thoughtful rather than reactive.

n1_collaborative.characterLine: Good. Because I’ve been doing some digging. Two of the signatories told me privately they were pressured into signing. If we can get them to say that publicly, the whole letter falls apart. But we need to move fast — by next week this is old news and the damage is permanent.

responses.text: That’s exactly the intel we need. Can you set up calls with those two? I’ll handle the conversations personally.
That’s helpful, but I don’t want to pressure people into retracting. Let’s focus on our positive story instead.

n1_confident.characterLine: I admire the confidence, but the board doesn’t share it right now. Confidence without a visible plan looks like arrogance — and three of our biggest donors are on that board. We need to give them something concrete.

responses.text: Fair point. You know the board better than anyone. What do they need to hear from me right now?
Then let’s call an emergency board briefing. I’ll present our impact data and our forward strategy. Let the numbers do the talking.

n1_honest.characterLine: I respect the honesty. Most leaders would pretend they have it all figured out. Here’s what I think: we don’t need a perfect plan right now. We need a visible response that tells the board and the community we’re not rattled. Then we can be strategic about the long game.

responses.text: That’s smart. What if we host an open community forum this week — let people ask tough questions and see us answer them directly?
OK. Can you draft talking points I can use with individual board members over the next 48 hours? I want to address concerns one-on-one before anyone panics.

n2_strong.characterLine: Now you’re thinking like someone who runs an organization, not just someone who leads programs. The board needs to see this version of you — the one who fights back strategically. I’ll make some calls tonight. Who else do we need in the room?

responses.text: You, our development director, and the board chair. Small circle, fast decisions. I’ll brief the full board after we have results.
Let’s loop in the full executive committee. I want buy-in from everyone before we move.

n2_moderate.characterLine: Alright. The cautious approach can work, but it needs to happen fast. Every day that letter sits unanswered, it gains credibility. What’s your timeline?

responses.text: Forty-eight hours. I’ll have a response strategy by Thursday morning and I want your sign-off before it goes out.
End of next week. I want to be thorough rather than hasty.

n3_end.characterLine: I’ve got your back on this. I’ve been on this board for eight years and I’ve seen three EDs come and go. The ones who survived crises were the ones who treated the board as allies, not obstacles. Keep me in the loop and we’ll get through this.

responses.text: David, I couldn’t ask for a better partner on the board. You’ll hear from me before anyone else does.
Thank you. I’ll have an update for you by end of day tomorrow.

outcomes.text: David becomes your strongest board ally. He quietly rallies support, shuts down the ‘reassess leadership’ conversation, and begins mentoring you on board management.
David is supportive but watching. He’ll defend you in board conversations but expects you to deliver on whatever you promised.
David is skeptical. He tells the board chair he’s ‘reserving judgment’ and starts attending more committee meetings to keep an eye on things.
David is deeply concerned. He initiates a private conversation with the board chair about ‘leadership continuity planning.’ Your job security just got shakier.

n0.characterLine: Stop. Before you say a single word publicly on the divestment vote, we need to talk about the media landscape. Three national outlets have reporters on campus right now. Whatever you say will be in a headline by tonight — and the headline won’t be the nuanced version. I’ve been doing crisis comms for twenty years and this is the kind of story that defines an organization. So: what’s your instinct?

responses.text: My instinct is to go on the record strongly. Silence right now looks like cowardice or indifference.
I want to say something, but I need it carefully crafted. Can you draft three versions — strong, moderate, and cautious — and let me choose?
Honestly, my instinct is to wait forty-eight hours and let the initial firestorm pass before we enter the conversation.

n1_aggressive.characterLine: I respect the courage. But let me tell you what happens next. You go strong, the other side clips the two most inflammatory sentences, they go viral without context, and by Thursday you’re doing damage control instead of leading. I’ve seen it happen to better communicators than either of us. If you want to go strong, we need to control the delivery — an op-ed in a friendly outlet, not a press scrum on the quad.

responses.text: That makes sense. Can you get me placed in a major outlet by tomorrow morning? I’ll write tonight.
What about a video statement we control completely? Script, setting, release timing — all ours.

n1_strategic.characterLine: Now you’re thinking like someone who’s been through a news cycle. I’ll have three drafts to you by 6 PM. But here’s the real question: who’s your audience? Are you talking to the campus, to the Jewish community, to the general public, or to your donors? Because the message is different for each one, and you can’t hit all four in one statement.

responses.text: Jewish community first. Our people need to know we’re in this fight. Everyone else can listen in.
The general public. If we talk only to ourselves, we lose the persuadable middle. This is a moment to educate, not just rally.

n1_cautious.characterLine: Forty-eight hours in a media firestorm is an eternity. By the time you speak, the narrative is set and you’re responding to other people’s framing. Your silence will be interpreted — by one side as complicity, by the other as cowardice. I’m not saying you need a press conference tomorrow. But you need to plant a flag somewhere within twenty-four hours, even if it’s just a brief statement on your website.

responses.text: OK, you’ve convinced me. What’s the minimum viable statement that stakes out our position without overcommitting?
I hear you, but I’ve also seen leaders get destroyed for speaking too fast. I’d rather be late and right than early and reckless.

n2_strong.characterLine: Good. I’ll start making calls. One more thing — and you’re not going to like this. You need to be prepared for the interview question you don’t want to answer. Every reporter is going to ask you a version of: ‘Do you think the students who voted for divestment are antisemitic?’ Your answer to that question will matter more than anything in your prepared statement.

responses.text: I’ll say that the resolution traffics in antisemitic tropes whether or not every student who voted for it intended that. Impact matters more than intent.
I’ll say that I won’t label students, but I will name the ideology behind the resolution as one that has historically targeted Jews. Let the audience draw the line.

n2_moderate.characterLine: A measured approach can work — but only if the moderation reads as strength, not weakness. The trick is to say something that both sides can live with but that your base reads as supportive. It’s a needle-threading exercise. Are you comfortable with ambiguity, or do you need to pick a side clearly?

responses.text: I’m comfortable with ambiguity if it’s principled ambiguity. Help me find language that’s firm on antisemitism without being inflammatory about the students.
Actually, I think ambiguity is a trap. Let me just pick a side and own it. Our community needs to know where we stand.

n2_cautious.characterLine: I’ve given you my professional advice. If you want to wait, that’s your call — you’re the executive director. But I need you to understand that my job gets exponentially harder with every hour of silence. Reporters start writing the ‘Jewish organizations divided and uncertain’ story, and that becomes the narrative we’re fighting instead of the actual issue.

responses.text: You’re right. Let’s get something out today. Even a holding statement that says we’re monitoring the situation and will have a full response within twenty-four hours.
I understand the risk and I’m choosing to absorb it. Sometimes not reacting is the strongest move.

n3_end.characterLine: Alright. Whatever we decided — I’m behind it. I’ll start executing. One last thing: when this blows over, and it will, I want us to have a real conversation about a proactive media strategy so we stop playing defense. Deal?

responses.text: Deal. And thank you for pushing back on me. That’s exactly what I need from you.
Schedule it. But right now, let’s win this news cycle first.

outcomes.text: Your media strategist crafts a rollout plan that lands your message in three major outlets within 48 hours. The narrative shifts in your favor, and other Jewish organizations start citing your framing.
Your statement lands without major backlash. The media strategist feels heard and executes competently, though the coverage is mixed.
The media response is muddled. Your strategist did her best with a message she didn’t believe in. Coverage is unfavorable and the story drags on longer than it should.
Your media strategist quietly updates her LinkedIn profile. The campus divestment story runs for a week with your organization portrayed as indecisive and out of touch.

character.name: Meghan Torres-Klein
character.context: Called after learning about the evangelical alliance proposal

n0.characterLine: I need to talk to you about what you just did with the evangelical partnership. My husband is Jewish. I converted twelve years ago. I chair your interfaith committee. And I’m telling you — as someone who grew up evangelical before choosing Judaism — that you don’t understand what you’re getting into. These relationships come with strings. Strings that aren’t visible until they’re wrapped around your throat.

responses.text: Meghan, I value that perspective more than almost anyone else’s on this. Help me understand what I’m not seeing.
I appreciate your concern, but we vetted this partnership carefully. Not all evangelical organizations are the same.
You’re right that I don’t have your lived experience with this. But the political reality is that we need allies, and they’re offering something concrete.

n1_open.characterLine: Here’s what you’re not seeing. The theology underneath the support — it hasn’t changed. They support Israel because of eschatology, not because they value Jewish life as it actually exists. When I was seventeen, my youth pastor told me Jews were God’s chosen people and also that every single one of them needed to accept Christ or face damnation. He saw no contradiction. These are the nicest people in the world, and they are praying for your conversion. That’s the deal.

responses.text: That’s a powerful story, and I take it seriously. Can we build guardrails into the partnership that protect against that theology bleeding into our work?
I believe you. But does their theology matter if the practical outcome is millions in security funding and political support when we need it most?

n1_pushback.characterLine: Vetted it? Did you vet the pastor who leads their Israel tours? Because I looked him up last night. He published a book in 2019 called ‘Israel’s Destiny: Preparing the Way for Christ’s Return.’ That’s your ally. That’s who we’re standing on a stage with. And when our members Google that name — and they will — what do I tell my interfaith committee?

responses.text: I didn’t know about the book. That’s a legitimate concern. Let me look into this more carefully before we go further.
We can’t purity-test every individual in a partner organization. If we did, we’d have no partners at all.

n1_pragmatic.characterLine: I understand the realpolitik. I really do. But let me tell you what happens in five years. They’ll want a seat at your Holocaust education table. They’ll want to co-brand your Hanukkah events. They’ll want to bring their youth groups to your programs. And slowly, incrementally, the line between ‘ally’ and ‘co-owner’ disappears. I’ve watched it happen to three other organizations.

responses.text: What if we set an explicit expiration date — a one-year partnership with clear boundaries, reviewed annually? No mission creep.
Meghan, I hear the slippery slope argument, but we’re adults. We can manage a partnership without losing control of our identity.

n2_constructive.characterLine: Now we’re talking. If you’re willing to build real boundaries — no joint theological programming, no co-branding on Jewish identity events, a morality clause about proselytizing — I’ll not only support this, I’ll help you sell it to the interfaith committee. I know both worlds. Use that.

responses.text: I want you to draft those guardrails yourself. You’re the only person on this board who’s lived on both sides. Your name should be on this framework.
Let me take your input and work with our legal team to formalize the boundaries. I’ll send you a draft before it goes to the board.

n2_tension.characterLine: I can see I’m not getting through. So let me put it in terms that might land differently: if this goes wrong, you won’t just lose the interfaith committee. You’ll lose me. And I’m the only convert on your board — the only person who can tell your members what it feels like to be a Jew by choice watching her old community treat her new community as a means to an end.

responses.text: I don’t want to lose you, Meghan. And I hear you. Let me take a week to reconsider the terms of this partnership. Will you help me get it right?
I value you on this board enormously. But I can’t make organizational strategy based on one person’s ultimatum, even yours.

n3_end.characterLine: This is personal for me in a way I can’t fully explain. Judaism saved my life — choosing it was the most important decision I ever made. I just need to know that the people leading this organization understand what they’re protecting.

responses.text: I hear you. What you chose, and what you bring — that’s exactly what this organization exists to honor. Thank you for trusting me with this.
Your commitment to this community is obvious. Let’s keep talking — I want your voice in every conversation about interfaith partnerships going forward.

outcomes.heading: A Bridge Between Worlds
outcomes.text: Meghan drafts a groundbreaking interfaith partnership framework that becomes a model for Jewish organizations nationwide. She tells her story publicly for the first time at your annual gala, and three new donors join because of it.

outcomes.heading: A Cautious Ally
outcomes.text: Meghan stays on the board and supports the partnership with reservations. She keeps a watchful eye on the evangelical relationship and reports back to you quarterly.

outcomes.heading: Growing Distance
outcomes.text: Meghan steps down as interfaith committee chair, citing ‘philosophical differences.’ She remains on the board but her engagement drops sharply.

outcomes.heading: A Resignation Letter
outcomes.text: Meghan resigns from the board. Her resignation letter — which details her journey from evangelical Christianity to Judaism — is forwarded to every board member. Two others begin questioning the evangelical partnership.

n0.characterLine: Before you decide how to handle the Greenbaum estate, I need to give you a number. Forty-three million dollars. That’s what we’ve left on the table over the past decade by not having a legacy giving program. I’ve been asking for one since I started this job. The Greenbaum bequest is either a wake-up call or another missed opportunity. So which is it going to be?

responses.text: Forty-three million. That number hurts. Walk me through what a real legacy program would look like and what it would cost to build.
I hear you on the legacy program, but right now I need to focus on the Greenbaum decision itself. Can we table the bigger conversation?
That’s a compelling number. But legacy giving raises complicated questions about donor intent and organizational independence. I have concerns.

n1_engaged.characterLine: It would cost about $120,000 a year — one full-time planned giving officer, marketing materials, legal review. Within three years, I’d project $2-4 million in new bequest commitments annually. But here’s the part you won’t like: the donors who make legacy gifts are overwhelmingly older, more traditional, and more politically conservative than your current base. They want to fund Jewish continuity, Israel programs, and security. Not everyone on staff will love where this money comes from or what it asks us to do.

responses.text: Good. We need to diversify our donor base politically anyway. If legacy donors push us toward the center, that might be healthy.
We can accept the gifts without letting donors dictate programming. That’s what unrestricted bequests are for. How do we steer legacy donors toward unrestricted giving?

n1_deflected.characterLine: With respect — the Greenbaum decision IS the bigger conversation. A family left us $1.2 million because they believed in what we do. If we fumble this, every estate lawyer in the community hears about it. If we handle it brilliantly, we set the template for the next twenty years of legacy giving. This isn’t a standalone decision. It’s a signal.

responses.text: You’re right. I was thinking too small. Let’s treat this as the foundation of a legacy program, not just a one-off bequest.
I understand the strategic opportunity. But I also have a fiduciary duty to handle this specific bequest responsibly before building a whole new program around it.

n1_cautious.characterLine: I appreciate the honesty. Here’s the concern I think you’re not saying out loud: you’re worried about strings. Legacy donors sometimes fund programs through their wills that the organization no longer wants to run twenty years later. The Greenbaum estate has some language about ‘supporting Israel education in perpetuity.’ Does ‘in perpetuity’ mean we’re locked into a program forever? That’s a legitimate governance question.

responses.text: Exactly. How do we honor donor intent without handcuffing future leadership? Is there a legal framework for that?
I think we need to consult our attorney before we commit to anything. ‘In perpetuity’ is a legal term and I don’t want to guess what it means.

n2_bold.characterLine: Now you’re thinking like an organization that’s going to be here in fifty years, not just next quarter. I have one more ask, and it’s a big one. I want to name the legacy program after the Greenbaums. It honors them, it signals to other families that we value this kind of giving, and — let’s be honest — it gives us a marketing hook that money can’t buy. The Greenbaum Legacy Society. What do you think?

responses.text: I love it. Draft the proposal, get the family’s blessing, and let’s announce it at the annual meeting. This is how we build something lasting.
I like the concept, but naming is a board decision. Let me socialize it with the executive committee first.

n2_moderate.characterLine: I’ll get the legal framework together. But I need you to understand something about development work that most executive directors learn too late: the donors who give after they die cared more about this organization than almost anyone who gives while alive. Treating their gifts as problems to manage instead of love letters to honor — that’s how organizations lose their soul.

responses.text: That reframing hits hard. You’re right — this family loved us enough to write us into their will. We should treat that with the weight it deserves.
I take your point. But love letters don’t come with legal obligations. We can honor the intent AND protect the organization. Those aren’t mutually exclusive.

n3_end.characterLine: I’ve worked here for seven years and this is the first real conversation I’ve had with an ED about legacy giving. That either says something about you or something about the leaders who came before. Either way — I’m glad we’re finally talking about it.

responses.text: Seven years is too long to wait. You should have been heard a long time ago. Let’s build this together.
Put together a full proposal with budget, timeline, and projected returns. I want it on the board’s agenda next month.

outcomes.text: Your development director launches a legacy giving initiative that generates eight new bequest commitments in the first year. She credits your leadership publicly at the annual dinner. Staff morale in the development office hits an all-time high.
The Greenbaum bequest is handled competently. Your development director is cautiously optimistic about a future legacy program but notes that ‘the window won’t stay open forever.’
The bequest is processed without fanfare. Your development director is professionally disappointed and starts presenting legacy giving data to individual board members, bypassing you.

Your development director confides to the board chair that ‘leadership doesn’t understand fundraising at a strategic level.’ Two months later she accepts a position at a competing organization, taking three major donor relationships with her.

character.name: Nadia Kalman
character.context: Arrived at the JCC during the bomb threat lockdown

n0.characterLine: My daughter is four years old. She was in the butterfly room when the alarm went off. A teacher carried her into a closet and told her they were playing a hiding game. She’s four. She doesn’t know what a bomb threat is, but she knows something is very wrong because her mother is shaking. So here’s my question: what are you going to do to make sure my daughter is safe tomorrow?

responses.text: Nadia, first — is Lily OK right now? Can I get you both some water? I want to make sure you’re both taken care of before we talk about next steps.
I want to walk you through exactly what happened and every security measure that activated. Your daughter was never in danger — the protocol worked perfectly.
You have every right to be upset. I’m going to be honest with you: our security plan needs to be upgraded and I’m committing to that starting today.

n1_warm.characterLine: She’s — she’s OK. She’s with my husband in the car. She keeps asking if the hiding game is over. Thank you for asking. Most of the other parents are just angry. I’m angry too, but I’m also... I’m scared. I brought my kid to a Jewish preschool because I wanted her to have a Jewish childhood. Not a childhood where she practices hiding in closets.

responses.text: No child should have to practice hiding. And no parent should have to feel afraid dropping their kid off at preschool. I take that personally and I’m going to fix it.
The Jewish childhood you want for Lily — that’s exactly what we’re here to protect. Can I tell you specifically what we’re changing, starting this week?

n1_factual.characterLine: I don’t care about protocols right now. I care that my daughter was carried into a closet by a twenty-two-year-old teacher who was probably as terrified as the children. What training did that teacher have? What happens if next time it’s not a hoax? What if the protocol works perfectly and someone still gets hurt?

responses.text: You’re right — I jumped to reassurance when you needed to be heard. I’m sorry. Tell me what you need from me right now, as Lily’s parent.
Those are exactly the questions I’m asking our security consultant right now. I’d like to invite you to be part of the review process. Your perspective as a parent matters.

n1_direct.characterLine: I appreciate the honesty. But ‘committing to upgrades starting today’ is what every organization says after a crisis. What does that actually mean? Are we talking about a guard at the door? Cameras? Active shooter training for the preschool teachers? Because if it’s just an email to parents saying ‘we take security seriously,’ I might as well enroll Lily at the public Montessori down the street.

responses.text: Here’s what I can commit to right now: an armed security guard during preschool hours by next Monday, a full security audit within thirty days, and a parent meeting within two weeks to present the plan.
I’m not going to make promises I haven’t fully budgeted yet. But I can tell you that security is now the number one line item in next quarter’s budget, above everything else.

n2_trust.characterLine: My grandmother survived the camps. She came to America and built a life and sent her grandchildren to Jewish schools because she believed it was safe here. I am not going to be the generation that stops sending Jewish kids to Jewish schools because it’s too dangerous. Do you understand what that would mean?

responses.text: I understand exactly what that would mean. And I promise you — on everything I have — that will not happen on my watch. This JCC will be the safest building in this city.
Your grandmother’s story is exactly why this JCC exists. I want to honor that legacy by making this a place where Jewish families feel safe, not just welcome.

n2_action.characterLine: OK. I’m listening. But I want to tell you something that might be hard to hear. Three families in the preschool are already talking about pulling their kids. Mine might be one of them. Not because we don’t love this place — because we do, and that makes the fear worse. You have about two weeks to convince us to stay.

responses.text: I’m going to call every preschool family personally this week. Not an email — a phone call. I want each family to hear from me directly what we’re doing and why they should stay.
I’m scheduling an emergency parent meeting for this Thursday evening. Bring every question, every concern, every fear. I’ll answer all of them, and the ones I can’t answer, I’ll tell you honestly.

n3_end.characterLine: I want to believe you. I really do. Lily asked me on the way here if she could go back to the butterfly room tomorrow. She doesn’t want to leave. I don’t want to take this away from her. Give me a reason not to.

responses.text: The reason is that this community will wrap its arms around Lily and every child in that building. We’ve been doing that for sixty years and we’re not stopping now. Bring her tomorrow. I’ll be at the door.
Come back next week for the parent meeting. If what you hear doesn’t convince you, I’ll understand your decision. But I think you’ll stay.

outcomes.heading: A Community Holds
outcomes.text: Nadia not only keeps Lily enrolled — she becomes the most vocal parent advocate for the JCC’s new security initiative. She organizes a fundraiser among preschool families that raises $18,000 for security upgrades. Zero families withdraw.

outcomes.heading: Holding On
outcomes.text: Nadia keeps Lily enrolled but remains watchful. She checks in weekly about security progress. One family quietly withdraws, but the rest stay.

outcomes.heading: A Slow Exodus
outcomes.text: Nadia gives it a month, then enrolls Lily at the Montessori. She tells the other parents honestly why she left. Three more families follow within the quarter.

outcomes.heading: Empty Cubbies
outcomes.text: Nadia pulls Lily the next day. She posts a carefully worded but devastating account on the neighborhood parents’ Facebook group. Seven families withdraw by the end of the month. The preschool — the JCC’s biggest revenue source — is in crisis.

n0.characterLine: I’m going to skip the pleasantries. I represent eleven families who collectively gave $1.8 million to this organization last year. Six of them called me in the last three hours about your press coverage. Two are talking about pulling their gifts. One already called his estate lawyer. So: what just happened, and what are you going to do about it?

responses.text: I appreciate you calling instead of letting this spiral. Let me tell you exactly what happened and why I made the decision I made. Then I want to hear what the donors need.
With respect, the press coverage doesn’t change the fact that we made the right call. I’m not going to reverse a principled decision because of donor anxiety.
I know this looks bad. What would you recommend I say to the donors who are upset?

n1_transparent.characterLine: Good. Because what my donors are reacting to isn’t the decision — most of them don’t even know the details. They’re reacting to the headline. They’re reacting to what their friends at the country club are saying. They’re reacting to a narrative they can’t control. And here’s what you need to understand about major donors: they don’t just give money. They give their name. And right now they feel like their name is attached to an embarrassment.

responses.text: I hear that. What if I call each of the six personally — not to apologize for the decision, but to explain the strategy and make them feel like insiders, not bystanders?
Would it help if I hosted a private dinner for the donor circle this week? Face-to-face, off the record, a chance to ask me anything directly?

n1_defiant.characterLine: Let me be blunt with you. I admire principled leadership. I do. But principles don’t pay salaries. The Feldstein family alone funds your entire young professionals program. If Howard Feldstein walks, that program dies. Is your principled decision worth thirty young adults losing their community? Because that’s the math.

responses.text: That’s a hard number to hear. I don’t want to compromise on the decision, but I need to do a better job managing how it’s perceived. Help me bridge that gap.
If Howard Feldstein pulls his gift because of a newspaper headline, then his commitment was conditional and fragile. We need donors who believe in us regardless of the news cycle.

n1_deferential.characterLine: Here’s what I’d tell them — and this is just me, not a strategy document. I’d say: ‘The press got it wrong. Here’s what actually happened. Here’s what we’re doing about it. And here’s why you should be proud to support this organization, not embarrassed.’ But that only works if you can deliver on the ‘what we’re doing about it’ part. Can you?

responses.text: Yes. I have a corrective plan that I’m implementing this week. Can I brief you on it so you can relay the key points to your families?
I can deliver on some of it. But I need you to be honest with me — which donors are truly at risk and which are just venting? I need to triage.

n2_repair.characterLine: Now we’re getting somewhere. I’ll tell you something I don’t usually share: my job exists because organizations like yours sometimes forget that donors are people, not ATMs. They want to feel valued, consulted, and proud. The money follows the feeling. Right now the feeling is bad. Fix the feeling and the money stays.

responses.text: You’re right. And I want to formalize that — a quarterly donor advisory call, early briefings on any sensitive decisions, and a direct line to me for your top families. Not just crisis management — ongoing relationship.
I appreciate the candor. Let me start with the immediate crisis — the six calls — and then let’s talk about building a better structure so we don’t end up here again.

n2_tense.characterLine: I’ve been in this business for thirty years. Donors whose commitment you call ‘conditional and fragile’ are the same people who built the building you work in. You don’t have to grovel. But you do have to respect that giving a million dollars to an organization is an act of trust, and trust is fragile. Whether you like that or not.

responses.text: You’re right. I was being defensive and that’s not fair to the families you represent. Let me come at this differently. What do they need to hear from me?
I hear you. I’ll call Howard Feldstein personally tomorrow. Not to walk anything back, but to show him the respect his support deserves.

n3_end.characterLine: I’m going to make some calls tonight and buy you some time. But I need you to understand — this goodwill account isn’t infinite. Every crisis that catches donors off guard makes the next one harder to survive. Build the relationships before you need them.

responses.text: Thank you. And I want you to know — I don’t see you as the donor police. I see you as someone who understands a part of this organization I’m still learning. I need your counsel, not just in crises.
I will. Send me the call list tonight. I’ll start dialing first thing tomorrow.

outcomes.text: Your donor representative stabilizes the donor circle within a week. No one pulls their gift. Two donors actually increase their commitments, citing your transparency and leadership under pressure.
The donor circle is calmed but watchful. One family reduces their gift by 20%. The rest hold steady, waiting to see how the next quarter goes.

Two families reduce their gifts significantly. Your donor representative is frustrated and begins cultivating relationships with donors independently, subtly positioning himself as the ‘reasonable voice’ in the organization.

Howard Feldstein pulls his entire gift. Two other families follow. Your donor representative tells the board chair privately that ‘the executive director doesn’t understand donor relations.’ Your budget next year will have a $400,000 hole.

n0.text: I need to talk to you. Half the board thinks you're pushing us in the wrong direction. I've been fielding calls all week. People are talking about a no-confidence vote.

choices.text: I appreciate you telling me directly. What specifically are they concerned about?
A no-confidence vote? Over one decision? That's an overreaction and you know it.
David, I value your perspective. Can we sit down this week and go through their concerns one by one?

n1_warm.text: Look, I'll be honest — I think some of their concerns have merit. But I also think you deserve the chance to address them. What would you say to a board town hall where you lay out your vision for the next six months?

choices.text: That's a great idea. I'll prepare a strategic presentation and address every concern head-on.
I'll do the town hall, but I want you to co-present. Show them we're aligned.

n1_cool.text: Overreaction or not, the votes are real. You can either engage with this or wait for it to blindside you. Your call.

choices.text: You're right. I'm sorry. What do you suggest?
If they want to vote, let them vote. My record speaks for itself.

n2.text: One more thing — and I'm saying this as a friend, not as vice chair. You need to spend more time listening to the people who disagree with you. Not to change your mind, but to make them feel heard. That's half the battle.

choices.text: You're right. I've been so focused on execution that I forgot about buy-in. Thank you, David.
I hear you. But sometimes leadership means making unpopular decisions.

outcomes.heading: Alliance Secured
outcomes.text: David becomes your strongest board ally. The no-confidence talk dies down.

outcomes.heading: Uneasy Truce
outcomes.text: David is cautiously supportive. The board remains divided but functional.

outcomes.heading: Board Fracture
outcomes.text: David sides with the dissidents. Three board members resign over the next month.

n0.text: I can't believe you're even considering cutting me off. I've given this organization $200,000 a year for eight years. Do you know what that adds up to? And now, when I need you most, you're running away?

choices.text: Marcus, I value everything you've done for us. This isn't about loyalty — it's about protecting the organization you helped build.
I'm not running away. But I have a fiduciary duty to this organization that goes beyond any single donor.
Tell me your side. I want to understand what happened before I make any decisions.

n1_warm.text: The charges are politically motivated. My lawyer says we'll beat this. All I'm asking is that you don't throw me overboard before the trial. Just... wait. Can you do that?

choices.text: I can give you time, Marcus. But I can't issue a statement of support. Our position has to be neutral until there's a resolution.
I'll wait for the legal process. But if new information comes out, I'll have to act. You understand that?

n1_cool.text: Fiduciary duty. That's what you're going with. After everything I've done. Fine. But let me be clear — if you cut me loose, I will never give another dollar to any Jewish organization. And I'll make sure people know why.

choices.text: Marcus, threatening me doesn't change the situation. But I hear your pain and I don't take this lightly.
I'm sorry you feel that way. The decision will be based on what's right for the organization, not on threats.

n2.text: Just... remember who was there when you were fundraising in a conference room with folding chairs. I believed in this organization before anyone else did.

choices.text: I will never forget that, Marcus. Whatever happens with the legal case, your contribution to this community is real and lasting.
And the community is grateful. But gratitude can't override governance.

outcomes.heading: Relationship Preserved
outcomes.text: Marcus agrees to step back quietly. If acquitted, he'll return as a donor. The relationship survives the crisis.

outcomes.heading: Cold Distance
outcomes.text: Marcus is hurt but civil. The donations stop but there's no public fallout.

outcomes.heading: Scorched Earth
outcomes.text: Marcus goes public with his grievances. Two other donors who were friends of his reduce their giving in solidarity.

n0.text: I need to speak with you about the Rabbi Winters situation. I've known her for 20 years. She was my study partner at seminary. I cannot believe these accusations, and I'm deeply troubled by how quickly we moved to judgment.

choices.text: Rabbi, I understand your personal connection. But we have to think about the students who came forward. Their courage deserves a response.
I respect your friendship with her. What would you have us do differently?
This isn't about belief or disbelief. It's about institutional responsibility.

n1.text: Jewish law requires us to hear both sides — shema et divrei chavercha. We have an obligation to due process that goes beyond the court of public opinion. Have we honored that obligation?

choices.text: That's a fair challenge. What would a process that honors both the accusers and the accused look like to you?
Due process is for courts. Our obligation is to the safety of our community — especially the most vulnerable.
You're right that we need to balance multiple obligations. I don't think we've gotten it perfectly right.

n2.text: I want you to know — whatever we decide, I will support the process. Even if it breaks my heart. Because the Torah also teaches us that justice is not optional. Tzedek, tzedek tirdof.

choices.text: Thank you, Rabbi. Your integrity in this moment means more than you know.
I'm glad we can work through this together. The community needs to see us united.

outcomes.heading: Moral Authority
outcomes.text: Rabbi Friedman publicly supports your handling of the situation, lending it moral authority. The community follows his lead.

outcomes.heading: Quiet Support
outcomes.text: Rabbi Friedman doesn't publicly oppose or support your decision. His silence is interpreted differently by different people.

outcomes.heading: Public Break
outcomes.text: Rabbi Friedman gives a sermon criticizing how Jewish institutions handle accusations. Everyone knows he's talking about you.

n0.text: I need you to understand something. The staff who signed that letter aren't agitators. They're our most dedicated people. They run programs that bring in half our revenue. And they're telling you something important about where the next generation of Jewish professionals stands.

choices.text: I hear you, Avi. But an organization can't function if staff get to pick which parts of the mission they support.
You're right that I need to listen to what they're really saying. It's not just about Birthright, is it?
Help me understand their perspective. What are they actually asking for?

n1.text: They want to feel like their values matter in this workplace. They went into Jewish communal work to build something meaningful, not to promote programs they find morally compromising. Can you see that?

choices.text: I can. And I want to find a way to honor that while maintaining our institutional commitments.
Their values matter. But so do our donors' values, and our mission's integrity. It's a balance.

n2.text: Here's what I know: if we lose these people, we're not just losing employees. We're losing the future of this organization. The generation that replaces them will feel the same way — or stronger.

choices.text: Then let's build something they want to be part of. Not at the cost of everything else, but alongside it.
I appreciate your passion, Avi. Let me think about this carefully.

outcomes.heading: Staff Trust
outcomes.text: Avi rallies the staff behind a compromise. Morale stabilizes and no one quits.

outcomes.heading: Cautious Peace
outcomes.text: The staff situation is managed but not resolved. Avi keeps things stable through personal relationships.

outcomes.heading: Exodus
outcomes.text: Avi himself begins considering whether this is still the right organization for him. Two staff members resign within a month.

n0.text: OK, I've been tracking the coverage. Here's where we stand: three outlets picked up the story, two got it wrong, and one is actively hostile. We have about 12 hours before this hardens into the accepted narrative. What do you want to do?

choices.text: You know media better than anyone here. What's your recommendation?
I want to go on the offensive. We need to get our version out there now.
Can we just wait it out? Maybe it dies on its own.

n1.text: Here's what I'd do: place an exclusive with the one friendly reporter — give her the full story, on the record. She publishes the corrected version, the other outlets update their stories to match, and the hostile one looks like an outlier. But you need to be willing to go on the record. Fully.

choices.text: Let's do it. Set up the interview. I'll be completely transparent.
On the record makes me nervous. Can we do background instead?
I trust your instinct. Make it happen.

n2.text: One last thing — after this is handled, we need to have a conversation about proactive media strategy. We're always playing defense. I want us to be the ones driving the narrative, not reacting to it.

choices.text: Absolutely. Let's schedule that conversation for next week. You deserve more resources for this.
Good idea. Once we're through this crisis, let's talk about a longer-term communications plan.

outcomes.heading: Narrative Controlled
outcomes.text: Noa's media strategy works. The corrected story becomes the dominant narrative. Your reputation is restored.

outcomes.heading: Damage Limited
outcomes.text: The story fades without major lasting damage, though some people saw the wrong version first.

outcomes.heading: Narrative Lost
outcomes.text: The hostile outlet's version becomes the accepted story. Correcting it now looks defensive.

n0.text: I want to talk about the Islamic Center partnership. I know some board members are nervous, but I've been doing interfaith work for 15 years and this is the most genuine offer I've ever seen. Imam Hassan isn't playing politics. He's building community.

choices.text: I believe you. But the Israel question is a landmine. How do we navigate that?
Tell me more about Imam Hassan. What makes this different from other interfaith attempts?
The board's concerns aren't unfounded, Tali. We need to be careful here.

n1.text: I've had three private conversations with the imam about Israel. He's not anti-Zionist — he's critical of specific policies, the way many of us are. He's willing to agree to disagree. What he wants is to fight hate together. After everything our communities have both been through, can we really say no to that?

choices.text: If he's willing to set Israel aside and focus on shared concerns, I think we can make this work.
I need to hear that directly from him before I can move forward. Can you arrange a private meeting?
I'm still worried about what happens when the next Gaza conflict erupts and we're formally partnered.

n2.text: Here's what I know from years of this work: the partnerships that survive crises are the ones built on personal relationships, not press releases. Give me six months. If it doesn't work, I'll be the first to say so.

choices.text: Six months. You have my support. Let's build this the right way.
I'll give it a chance, but I want monthly check-ins on how it's going.

outcomes.heading: Bridge Built
outcomes.text: Tali's relationship with Imam Hassan becomes a model for the community. The partnership launches smoothly.

outcomes.heading: Cautious Start
outcomes.text: The partnership begins tentatively. Tali keeps both sides communicating.

outcomes.heading: Missed Connection
outcomes.text: Tali is frustrated by the institutional resistance. The Islamic Center partners with a different Jewish organization.

n0.text: OK, so you wanted to meet with me. I'll be honest — I'm here because I respect that you asked, not because I think this organization is relevant to my Jewish life. I go to havurah, I volunteer with mutual aid, I study Torah with my friends. What does your organization offer that I can't get from my community?

choices.text: That's a fair question. Maybe the honest answer is: I don't know yet. I'm here to listen, not to sell.
We offer institutional support, advocacy resources, and connections that informal groups can't sustain long-term.
What if the question isn't what we offer you, but what we could build together?

n1_warm.text: OK, that's... not the answer I expected. Here's what I'll tell you: we're not anti-institution. We're anti-irrelevance. We want Jewish organizations that talk about housing, climate, racial justice — not just Israel and antisemitism 24/7. We want leadership that looks like us, not like our grandparents' country club.

choices.text: I hear that. And you're not wrong that we've been slow to evolve. What would make you want to be involved?
We do talk about those things. But I realize maybe we're not talking about them in ways that reach you.

n1_cool.text: See, that's exactly the pitch that doesn't work. 'Institutional support.' My mutual aid group raised $10,000 last month through a Google Doc. We don't need your infrastructure. We need you to care about what we care about.

choices.text: You're right. I was talking about what we are, not what you need. Let me try again: what do you care about?
That $10,000 is impressive. But what happens when you need to sustain that for five years? That's where institutions matter.

n2.text: Look, I'll make you a deal. Give us a room, a budget of $500, and zero editorial oversight. Let us program one event our way. If it works, we talk about what comes next. If it doesn't, no hard feelings.

choices.text: Deal. $500, a room, and your vision. Show me what you've got.
I can do the room and the budget. But I need to at least know the topic in advance — not for editorial control, just so I'm not blindsided.
I need to run it by my board first. But I'm inclined to say yes.

outcomes.heading: New Energy
outcomes.text: Jordyn's event draws 60 young adults. Several join your mailing list. A bridge is being built.

outcomes.heading: Respectful Distance
outcomes.text: Jordyn appreciates the meeting but nothing concrete happens. The havurah continues independently.

outcomes.heading: Confirmed Irrelevance
outcomes.text: Jordyn tells her network that the meeting was 'exactly what I expected — corporate Jewish nonprofit speak.' Your youth problem deepens.

n0.text: I just got off the phone with Torres' chief of staff. They're panicking. She wants to make this right but she doesn't know how. Her staff is asking if we can help craft an apology that actually works — not another 'I'm sorry if anyone was offended' disaster.

choices.text: We're not her PR team. She said antisemitic things. She needs to own that herself.
If we help shape the apology, we can make sure it actually addresses the tropes she used. That's better for everyone.
What's in it for us if we help her?

n1.text: Here's the political reality: if she apologizes well, we keep an ally in Congress who sponsors our legislation and secures our funding. If she crashes and burns, we lose that ally and we've also made an enemy. The replacement in her district could be worse.

choices.text: Help her with the apology. But we need a commitment — antisemitism education, a public visit to the Holocaust museum, and no more 'Jewish lobby' language. Ever.
I see the political logic. But if we're seen helping her clean up an antisemitic mess, what does that say about our principles?

n2.text: Principles and politics aren't always aligned. The question is: what gets the best outcome for Jews in this district? Not what feels most righteous, but what actually protects the community.

choices.text: You're right. Let's be strategic. Help her, but with clear conditions and accountability.
I need to think about this. There's a line between strategic and compromised, and I want to make sure we're on the right side of it.

outcomes.heading: Strategic Alliance
outcomes.text: Sarah helps craft a strong apology. Torres delivers it. The relationship is rebuilt on more honest terms.

outcomes.heading: Political Pragmatism
outcomes.text: Torres' apology is adequate but not moving. The relationship continues but with less warmth.

outcomes.heading: Burned Bridge
outcomes.text: Without your help, Torres' apology is a disaster. She pivots to attacking 'the Jewish establishment.' You've lost the ally anyway.

n0.text: I need you to understand something. My husband isn't Jewish. He's also the most supportive partner any Jewish parent could ask for. He drives our kids to Hebrew school. He lights Shabbat candles with us. And your organization just told him he's a 'community friend,' not a full member. How do you think that feels?

choices.text: It shouldn't feel like that. I'm sorry. Tell me what full inclusion would look like to your family.
I understand your frustration, Lisa. The policy was a compromise, and I know compromises often satisfy no one.
The membership categories are about Jewish communal governance, not about valuing your husband less as a person.

n1.text: My daughter asked me why Daddy can't vote at the synagogue but he can vote for President. Try explaining that to a seven-year-old. She doesn't understand why the Jewish community has tiers of belonging. Honestly? Neither do I.

choices.text: Out of the mouths of children. She's asking the right question. Maybe we should be asking it too.
I don't have a good answer for that. And that probably means our policy needs work.
There are theological reasons for the distinctions. I know that doesn't make it easier for your family.

n2.text: I'm not asking you to change 3,000 years of Jewish law. I'm asking you to make space for families like mine. We're here. We're raising Jewish kids. We love this community. All we want is to belong — fully.

choices.text: You do belong. And I commit to making sure our policies reflect that. Thank you for trusting me with this conversation.
I hear you, Lisa. Let me take this back to the board. Change takes time, but your voice matters in this process.

outcomes.heading: Welcome Home
outcomes.text: Lisa becomes an advocate for inclusive policies from within the community. Her family deepens their involvement.

outcomes.heading: Holding Pattern
outcomes.text: Lisa stays in the community but remains frustrated. She volunteers less and her husband stops attending events.

outcomes.heading: Family Lost
outcomes.text: Lisa withdraws her family entirely. 'If my husband isn't welcome, neither are we.' Three other interfaith families follow.

n0.text: I need to tell you where I'm coming from on the security question. My grandmother survived Auschwitz. She used to say, 'When they tell you who they are, believe them.' Someone photographed our exits. I believe them.

choices.text: Your grandmother's wisdom is exactly why this matters. We're not overreacting — we're being responsible.
I take the threat seriously too, Rachel. The question isn't whether to act, but how to act in a way that doesn't change who we are.
I understand the personal dimension. But we also can't make security decisions based on generational trauma.

n1.text: I know some people think armed guards change the atmosphere. You know what else changes the atmosphere? A shooting. I don't want to be the board chair who chose aesthetics over lives. I couldn't live with myself.

choices.text: Neither could I. Let's implement the best security we can afford and make sure our community knows we're protecting them.
There are security solutions that protect without intimidating. We can do both, Rachel. Let me show you what I'm thinking.

n2.text: Whatever we decide — I just want you to know that this board will back you. The community is scared. They need to see leadership, not committee meetings.

choices.text: I appreciate that, Rachel. And I promise you: this community will be safe. I won't cut corners on this.
Thank you. Your support — and your grandmother's memory — will guide how we handle this.

outcomes.heading: United Front
outcomes.text: Rachel champions the security plan to the full board. It passes unanimously. The community feels protected.

outcomes.heading: Grudging Support
outcomes.text: Rachel supports the plan but privately wishes you'd gone further. The board passes it with some dissent.

outcomes.heading: Board Divided
outcomes.text: Rachel pushes for her own security proposal, splitting the board. The community sees leadership in disarray on the most fundamental issue.
